title: Eating through Penn State with Anthony Provenzano
author: Carolena Bongi | The Daily Collegian
description:
What began as one-take food reviews on a private account has grown into a TikTok series during one Penn State student’s final year. Anthony Provenzano has taken his once private opinions to a more public platform for his senior year.

His reviews highlight food spots around Penn State and downtown State College, and have earned him over 3,400 followers.

“It kind of started in high school,” Provenzano, a fourth-year studying finance, said. “I used to do taco reviews on my private Snapchat story. One bite, say something stupid and rate it.”

The videos were just for friends, and the reviews often centered around different taco spots nearby, Provenzano said. He would give quick reactions inspired by Dave Portnoy’s “one-bite pizza review” style.

Provenzano originally intended for his videos to be an inside joke between friends, but then they gradually became something people looked forward to. After arriving at Penn State, he began to post less frequently. But with graduation approaching, decided he wanted to bring the idea back.

“I was going to go out to eat at these places anyway,” Provenzano said. “I’m only here for a couple more months.”

Since restarting his series publically, Provenzano has reviewed a variety of food spots across campus and downtown.

After trying bowls, breakfast options and go-to bar staples, some favorite spots of Provenzano’s have been Green Bowl and Sowers Harvest Cafe. He said part of his motivation was branching out and trying places he had walked past for years but never bothered to try.

When deciding on a rating, Provenzano said taste is the most important factor, but not the only one.

“The food is definitely the number one thing,” Provenzano said. “But the atmosphere, price, drinks; it's all about the overall vibe and how satisfied I am for how much I spent.”

Luke Washabaugh has known Provenzano since before college, and said seeing the reviews return this year wasn’t unexpected.

“We were kind of begging him to do reviews back in high school,” Washabaugh, a fourth-year studying health policy and administration, said. “It just feels like a natural progression he’s doing reviews at Penn State now.”

For Washabaugh, the videos have become an important part of their ending college experience. Whether it’s grabbing lunch at a spot with a unique menu item or planning future trips with friends, he said the process has added something fun to their final year together.

Connor Casten said he was excited to see Provenzano begin posting consistently again.

“He used to post a lot in high school and get a lot of views,” Casten, a fourth-year studying finance, said. “It was good to see him back.”

One of Casten’s favorite reviews, a “Drinking around State College” video, featured a stop at Doggie’s Pub where they got pizza, a large pickle and included many of their friends.

Casten said there've been moments when he might rate a meal slightly higher, but he believes Provenzano keeps his scores fair.

“He gives an honest opinion, and that’s all you can really ask for,” Casten said.

After graduation, Provenzano plans to move to Austin, Texas, where he hopes to continue trying new restaurants and expanding his reviews. But for now, the three friends are finishing their final few months at Penn State together, and the reviews have become a digital diary of their time together.

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: Yoga, theme dinners, wrestling championship | This week in State College
author: Emma Naysmith | The Daily Collegian
description:
A week before spring break, there’s still a lot happening in the State College community.

Here’s a look at some events to add to your calendar before heading home for break.

Monday, March 2

Yoga & Meditation

The first Yoga & Meditation of the week hosted by Penn State Health Promotion and Wellness will take place 12:30 to 1:30 p.m. in Room 232 of the HUB-Robeson Center.

The event is for all-levels and registration is not required, offering participants the opportunity to engage their mind and body while taking part in this restorative practice.

Additional certified instructor-led sessions will continue from 5:30 to 6:30 p.m. in 107 Pasquerilla Spiritual Center and Wednesday noon to 1 p.m. at the Palmer Museum.

Penn State Centre Stage’s The Morris & Essex Line

Taking place from 7:30 to 10 p.m. in the Pavillion Theatre, this musical follows a suburban mother’s midlife crisis and her child’s growing up, within the setting of a NJ Transit trip.

The show will run through Friday, and tickets can be bought online through the Penn State Arts Ticket Center.

Tuesday, March 3

Women’s History Month Moodboard Making

The Center for Sexual and Gender Diversity will host a moodboard making event in celebration of Women’s History Month.

Participants will be provided with all supplies needed, and also have the opportunity to learn more about the historical significance of women’s self-expression throughout history.

The event will take place from 5 to 8 p.m. in HUBLL011 in the HUB.

Cafe Laura Theme Dinner, Bridgerton Night – Diamonds of the Season

This themed dinner offers those dining the experience of original and innovative cuisines based on Netflix’s “Bridgerton” series. All courses are prepared by Penn State Hospitality Management students.

Table reservations can be booked here, with a six-person maximum.

Penn State Baseball vs. St. John’s

The Nittany Lions will face off against St John’s Red Storm at 5:30 p.m. at Medlar Field. This event is free to the public.

Wednesday, March 4

PSU Student Farm’s Nature RX Craft Drop-In

The Student Farm at Penn State will host a micro-greens starter making workshop from 3 to 5 p.m. in Room 112 at the HUB.

Penn State Women’s Lacrosse vs. Colgate

The Lady Lions will meet in competition against the Colgate Raiders at noon in Panzer Stadium.

This event is free to the public.

Men’s Basketball Senior Night vs. Ohio State

The blue and white will meet the scarlet and gray for a 7:30 p.m. tip-off in the Bryce Jordan Center.

Tickets can be purchased via Ticketmaster and the game will also be available for streaming through Peacock.

Thursday, March 5

Spiritual Wellness: Meditation with O-An Zendo

Hosted by the Center for Spiritual and Ethical Development, the first of this two-part series will introduce participants to zen tradition and Buddhism-related meditation techniques.

Session one will take place from 3 to 4:30 p.m. in 107 Pasquerilla Spiritual Center’s Memorial Lounge.

Men’s Ice Hockey vs. Wisconsin

The Nittany Lions will take the ice against the Badgers for a puck drop of 7 p.m. at Pegula Ice Arena.

Tickets can be purchased via Ticketmaster. The game will be available for streaming through Big Ten Plus.

Friday, March 6

Women’s Tennis vs. Michigan State

The Penn State Women’s Tennis team will compete against Michigan State at 4 p.m. in the Indoor Tennis Center.

This event is free to the public.

Saturday, March 7

2026 Big Ten Wrestling Championships

Penn State Wrestling will compete in the first session of the 112th Big Ten Championships beginning at 10 a.m. in the Bryce Jordan Center.

Tickets for this two-day event can be purchased via Ticketmaster.

Men’s Tennis vs. Ohio State

The Nittany Lions will meet the Buckeyes courtside at noon. This event is free to the public.

Penn State Men’s Lacrosse vs. Colgate – Youth Lacrosse Day

The Nittany Lions will compete against the Raiders at 1 p.m. in Panzer Stadium. This event is free to the public and can be streamed on Big Ten Plus.

A vehicle stop investigation revealed the operator was driving under the influence of a controlled substance at W College Ave & N Atherton Street at 1:28 a.m. The case is open.

Saturday, Feb. 28

No crimes have been uploaded.

MORE CRIME AND COURTS COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball earns extra-inning win over Indiana State in Bahamas
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State got back in the win column on Sunday after a 9-8 victory over Indiana State in extra innings from the Bahamas, its first win of the weekend.

It’s been a rocky start to the season for Penn State. Entering Sunday’s contest at 2-6, while also on a four-game losing streak, it was looking to leave Nassau on a high note.

And it did just that.

Penn State’s Isaiah Shayter took to the mound at Andre Rodgers National Baseball Stadium. The freshman pitcher made the first two outs with ease, then walked his third batter, but managed a flyout to close things out.

The Nittany Lions took to the plate in hopes of getting the bats swinging.

Cohl Mercado stepped up first at the top of the lineup. After managing a full count, he was walked to get things going.

Michael Anderson was the second hitter for Penn State. On a 1-1 count, Mercado stole second, but Anderson was unable to make anything of his at-bat.

Bryce Molinaro was next, but struck out quickly, meaning clean-up hitter Jayden Davis was up to the plate.

However, Davis grounded out to second, leaving Mercado stranded on base as the inning concluded.

The top of the second saw a quick three-and-out for the Sycamores, thanks to Shayter.

Jack Porter stepped up as the fifth hitter in the lineup for Penn State, but hit a flyout off the first pitch. Kevin Karstetter was next and took a hit on the first pitch, advancing him to first.

That brought up Jesse Jaconski, who singled for a basehit, advancing Karstetter to second.

The next two batters were unable to make anything happen, and the Sycamores closed out the inning.

The ease from the previous innings started to wear away for Shayter.

Indiana State’s Ray Jesse stepped up to the plate and was walked after Shayter threw four balls in a row, sending him to first base.

Caleb Niehaus was next up for the Sycamores and singled through the right side of the infield. With two runners on, Indiana State saw its first real chance of the game.

After a long review, Ray was confirmed safe after advancing to third on a passed ball. And with Emil Estrella up next for the Sycamores, the blue and white were in a pinch.

Estrella hit a double to send Ray home, putting Indiana State up 1-0. With two batters still on, the Nittany Lions needed to make some plays.

But, Carter Beck stepped up and made a sacrifice flyout to advance the runner on third to home, putting the Sycamores up 2-0.

After Shayter walked another batter, Caden Miller produced an RBI to get another runner home, making it 3-0.

After allowing another hit to put the Sycamores up 4-0, Shayter was pulled from the mound and replaced by Mason Horwat.

Horwat closed the high-scoring inning with just one pitch.

Back to the top of the lineup, Mercado wasn’t able to make anything happen. But Anderson stepped up next and delivered a huge home run that flew beyond the walls of the ballpark, closing the lead slightly at 4-1.

The inning closed shortly after, with no additional scoring from the blue and white. Penn State went three-and-out at the top of the fourth.

The Sycamores almost escaped the bottom of the fourth in the same fashion, but a fielding error prevented it. With another fielding blunder shortly following, Penn State had two runners on.

Preston Yaucher singled to third, sending Jaconski home to cut the lead in half, effectively ending Owen Roberts’ time on the mound.

The inning ended shortly after Roberts’ departure.

As the Nittany Lions took to the mound, Indiana State was able to get another run in, putting them up 5-2.

Kyle Emmons took over for Horwat to try to keep Penn State alive. But with Miller back at the plate, he was able to get another RBI under his belt and put the Sycamores up 6-2.

As the Nittany Lions took over at the plate, Molinaro stepped up and hit a big home run to cut the lead in half again, 6-3.

After another RBI and a sacrifice fly, the blue and white found itself closing the lead within one.

Penn State was able to keep the top of the sixth scoreless, but the Sycamores managed the same result in the bottom.

In the seventh, Ben DeMell took the mound for Penn State. While starting off strong, he allowed an inside-the-park home run to let the Sycamores go up 8-5.

While the inning closed soon after, the damage had already been done.

Penn State managed another home run from Davis to make it 8-6, but nothing else came from the inning.

In the top of the eighth, Penn State got out of a pinch and held Indiana State to no score.

The bottom of the eighth saw another Penn State RBI to make it 8-7, but it was unable to fully equalize.

In the final inning, Penn State finally equalized, making it an 8-8 game, forcing extra innings.

To close out the game, the Nittany Lions brought in a run during the 11th inning off Mercado’s swing to win it 9-8.

Up Next

The Nittany Lions will return to Medlar Field for the first home series of the season against St. John's at 5:30 p.m. Tuesday and 4 p.m. Wednesday.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: No. 6 Penn State men’s gymnastics dominates, defeats No. 12 Springfield in Rec Hall
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State continued its meet season after a week's hiatus when the Nittany Lions hosted Springfield in Rec Hall for a chance at another victory.

The blue and white dominated most of the events as it took another victory, winning 316.700-308.200 over the Pride.

Rotation one

Penn State started out quickly on the floor. Matt Underhill led the way for the blue and white, totaling a 13.600. Shaun Smith, who followed Underhill’s performance, put up a 13.500 despite his landings being a bit unbalanced. Brayden Erickson dropped a 13.200 while Kacper Garnczarek finished with a 13.250.

What really set the numbers apart was Springfield’s performance. Multiple gymnasts underrotated, or couldn’t stick the landing, leading to a marginal Nittany Lion lead, 53.550-51.600.

Rotation two

The pommel horse was up next, and Penn State grew its lead even further thanks to a standout performance from Ritam Malik, who notched a 14.050. However, that wasn’t the leading score in the event. Springfield’s Deven Felsenstein and Peyton Cramer scored 14.350 and 14.100, respectively.

An 11.300 score from the Pride’s Jacob Soederqvist was the difference maker as Penn State didn’t have a score under 12.850 from Bobby Alessio, Garnczarek and Ricky Perez gave the Nittany Lions a larger advantage, 107.300-104.600.

Rotation three

The rings rotation for Penn State featured some lower numbers, but the differential between the Nittany Lions and Pride grew larger.

Underhill notched a 13.700 for the team high, and Kellen Ryan and Smith fell just short of the 13 mark, with a 12.900 and 12.700, respectively.

For Springfield, Blaise Rousseau scored the highest mark with a 13.550. However, the other three gymnasts fell below the 13 mark to give Penn State another advantage in a rotation. The Nittany Lions led 159.150-155.850.

Rotation four

The vault is where Penn State took the biggest advantage. Of the eight competitors across both teams, the Nittany Lions had the top four. All four of their gymnasts finished above the top performer for the Pride.

Smith notched a 14.200, while Simpson scored a 14.150. Erickson wasn’t far behind with a 13.900, as the blue and white continued to showcase dominance. For Springfield, Owen Carney had the highest score with a 13.600. Evan Reichert wasn’t far behind with a 13.450, but the scores weren’t enough to close the gap.

Rotation five

The parallel bars were the first apparatus that Penn State didn’t win. Alessio began things with a 12.700, while two gymnasts later, Simpson scored a 13.100 to lead the team once again.

It was the consistency of Springfield that gave it the advantage. All four gymnasts scored a 12.600 or higher, with Rousseau matching Simpson’s score of 13.100. A 51.100 score on the parallel bars gave the Pride the advantage, with the Nittany Lions scoring just a 50.600. However, the blue and white led by a large margin, 265.750-260.800.

Rotation six

The sixth and final rotation was the bars. Multiple falters from Springfield all but sealed the deal for the Nittany Lions, but the gymnasts still needed to succeed. Simpson notched a 12.600, with Ryan scoring a 12.550.

For the Pride, two gymnasts scored under 12, leading to an even larger margin of victory for Penn State. However, Carney closed things out on a high note with a 13.050 to give Springfield a five-point lead, but the Nittany Lions still had one competitor to go. Garnczarek closed out the meet with an impressive 13.600 as the blue and white cruised to victory.

Up next

Penn State heads to Ann Arbor to face Michigan on Saturday at 1 p.m.

MORE GYMNASTICS COVERAGE

title: No. 25 Penn State softball closes out Tennessee Invitational in dominant fashion, run-rule’s NC Central
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Following a second consecutive split day on Saturday, Penn State returned to the field on Sunday morning to close out its play at the Tennessee Invitational. The Nittany Lions took on NC Central after previously defeating the Eagles 6-0 on Friday.

Penn State was dominant on both sides of the ball, defeating NC Central 14-0.

The Nittany Lions grabbed the bats first, facing right-hander Madeline Janssen to start the morning. The Eagles quickly recorded an out, but a single from third baseman Allison Oneacre put a runner on base for the blue and white.

Ahead on a 2-0 count, designated Brooke Klosowicz ripped her seventh home run of the year to put Penn State up two early.

Following the long ball, first baseman Kirsten Finarelli worked a walk after a 10-pitch at bat. Finarelli advanced to second on a muffed throw by NC Central first baseman Janea Hudson, allowing her to reach home on a two-out double from right fielder Gabby Gradishar.

Gradishar was left at second, but the Nittany Lions got the job done in their opening frame, taking an early 3-0 lead.

Right-hander McKenna Young got the nod in the circle for the blue and white, already with a cushion on her behalf. The junior completed Penn State’s strong first inning by striking out the side.

To start the second frame, the Nittany Lions picked up right where they left off as center fielder Natalie Lieto hit a lead-off home run after seeing just one pitch.

The scoring was far from over for the blue and white as the runs kept pouring in.

After reaching on a bunt, Kaylie Walters found her way to third following an errant throw from Eagles’ catcher Izabella Chaves on Walters’ attempt at second base. A bobbled ball by third baseman Elana Krupey off the bat of Oneacre allowed Walters to score.

NC Central went to the bullpen, bringing in Xenna Tompkins, who gave up a free base to Klosowicz, followed by a double from Finarelli to put another Penn State run on the board.

Macy Chamberlin knocked a two RBI single, and in her second at bat of the inning, Lieto ripped a double to bring in a pair, putting the Nittany Lions' lead at 10-0 after a seven-run frame.

It was another quick inning in the field for Penn State as Young threw just five pitches and the Eagles went down in order for the second straight time.

Three more runs came across for the Nittany Lions in the third inning, all with two outs. With a runner on second, Bridget Nemeth, more known for her abilities in the circle, knocked a pinch-hit RBI double.

The blue and white loaded up the bases, bringing up left fielder Cara Bohner, who hit a two RBI single to put Penn State’s total at 13 runs heading into the home half of the third frame.

Young and the Nittany Lions had another perfect outing, setting NC Central down in order once again.

The blue and white added one more run in the top of the fourth after a productive ground out from Oneacre to shortstop Isabella Slape brought in Walters from third.

The Eagles welcomed the top of their batting order for just the second time of the game, hoping to put runners on. NC Central came up empty again, recording two groundouts and Young tossed her fourth strikeout of the morning.

Penn State put two runners on in the top of the fifth, but the Eagles defense recorded a scoreless inning for the first time in the game. The Nittany Lions held a 14-0 lead heading into the bottom of the frame, meaning NC Central needed to score at least seven runs to avoid a run-rule defeat.

The Eagles recorded their first base runner in an interesting fashion. Young struck out Slape for her fifth of the day, but Madison Seidel fumbled the catch, allowing Slape to reach first.

However, the threat didn’t last for long as Sierra Soto grounded into a double play, completing Penn State’s strong defensive effort and run-rule victory.

Up Next

Penn State takes a trip to Salem, Virginia, to play in the Hokie Invite for its final non-conference weekend of the season. The Nittany Lions open their trip against Radford at 1 p.m. Friday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: No. 5 Penn State falls short 4-3 in overtime against Notre Dame
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 5 Penn State fell short with a 4-3 loss against Notre Dame in its final road game of the regular season, closing the series 1-1 after a battle in overtime.

With the blue and white looking for every Big Ten point it can muster, and both the Big Ten Tournament and the NCAA Tournament on the horizon, the loss at Compton Family Ice Arena was not ideal.

The first period saw a quick start, with pushing and shoving in the Penn State crease starting only 20 seconds into the match.

Following the 3-3 shootout win for the blue and white on Friday, goaltender Kevin Reidler got the start over Josh Flemming, who played the previous night.

Penn State was the first to strike three minutes into the first period, flipping the script from Friday. A centering pass by Gavin McKenna from behind the net set up Casey Aman for the goal.

Just under five minutes into the period, the Nittany Lions were already outshooting the Fighting Irish 4-0.

The Notre Dame squad was able to draw the first power play of the match-up after Carter Schade was sent to the box on a two-minute minor for holding.

The blue and white were able to pull off a successful penalty kill. And once 5-on-4 was finished, McKenna was back in action.

The freshman skated into the Notre Dame zone on a breakaway, centering the puck for a shot that went just wide.

It took over ten minutes for the Fighting Irish to record their first two shots, both of which were fought off by Reidler.

Soon after, Penn State saw its first power play of the match after a two-minute minor for slashing sent Notre Dame’s Carter Slaggert to the box.

After going no-score in the power play, Shea Van Olm rocketed the puck towards goaltender Nicholas Kempf, who made a massive glove-save.

But with just under two minutes left in the first period, Notre Dame equalized off a turnover from Schade, giving Maddox Fleming the chance to tie things up.

Matt DiMarsico and JJ Wiebusch were able to get two back-to-back shots off with just a minute of play left, but Kempf made both saves.

With 25 seconds to go in the first, Notre Dame earned its second power play after a two-minute minor for slashing was called on Jackson Smith. Despite time slipping away, the Fighting Irish got one past Reidler to put them up 2-1 at the intermission.

While Reidler didn’t see much action in the opening moments of the first period, he was definitely put to work early in the second.

Going into the second, Penn State had still outshot the Fighting Irish 9-7, but Notre Dame recovered quickly and got things tied back up, forcing Reidler to make a couple of big saves.

A hit on DiMarsico drew a two-minute minor for kneeing against Notre Dame, granting the blue and white its second power play.

However, the Fighting Irish were able to ward off any goals by the Nittany Lions and returned to full strength after a quick kill.

While the blue and white were able to keep the puck largely in the Notre Dame zone, it was unable to get many shots off. And after a slashing call on DiMarsico, the Fighting Irish were back on the man-advantage.

The blue and white penalty kill was another success. And after the power play expired, defenseman Cade Christenson picked up his third goal of the season off his own rebound, with assists going to DiMarsico and McKenna.

Now a 2-2 game, with just under 6 minutes left to play, it was back to being anyone’s game.

The Fighting Irish had a big chance after a shot from Danny Nelson that was controlled by Reidler, who was promptly pushed back into his own net. The play was reviewed by the officials and confirmed as a no-goal.

With just over two minutes left, play shifted to 4-on-4 after Aidan Fink and Michael Mastrodomenico were both given holding minors.

Heading into the third period, both teams needed to break the tie to avoid another game of overtime. And with shots at 17 each, it could’ve been anyone.

The third period opened with heavy possession from Notre Dame. While the blue and white were able to get back in it, possession evened out.

After a high sticking minor against Ben Schoen, Notre Dame’s Cole Brown received a minor for embellishment, making play shift to 4-on-4.

Moments into the 4-on-4 play, Nelson scored a tie-breaking goal through the legs of Penn State’s Aman, giving Notre Dame a 3-2 lead.

With just minutes left to play, Reidler made a massive save after a 3-on-1 attack entered the Penn State zone, keeping the game to just one goal.

With 2:15 on the clock, the Nittany Lions equalized after dominating possession in the Notre Dame zone. Luke Misa came up big to get the blue and white back in it.

The teams moved into a five-minute, 3-on-3 overtime period, similar to Friday’s match. And just 47 seconds in, Notre Dame shut it down and got the goal needed to close the match 4-3.

Up Next

The Nittany Lions will conclude the regular season with a two-game home series against Wisconsin. They will play at 7 p.m. Thursday and 7 p.m. Friday at Pegula Ice Arena.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's gymnastics falls runner up at Big Ten Quad Meet
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State, amid a four-meet losing streak, returned to Rec Hall Saturday night with a chance to pull above .500. For the first time all season, the 4-4 blue and white faced a multi-team meet, with Michigan, Nebraska and Washington arriving in town on State Patty’s weekend.

Ultimately, with the full field of foes, the Nittany Lions had a party of their own as runners-up in the meet.

Rotation one

Penn State had the tall task of starting on the floor, and the Nittany Lions immediately made do. A pair, Amani Herring and Jessica Johanson, scored a 9.850, but that was a backseat to Alyssa Kramer and Kalea McElligott, who had beautiful moves and put up team-high 9.875s.

As a team, the blue and white put up a 49.200, which tied Michigan for the lead, which started on the bars.

Nebraska started on the beam and got a 48.800, while Washington posted a 49.050 on the bars.

Rotation two

The Wolverines and Nittany Lions moved on to their next events, the vault and the beam, and vied to take the sole spot at the top. Ultimately, both matched their scores in the first round, but Michigan pulled slightly ahead by 0.275.

Johanson led scoring with 9.875, followed by Avery Piedrahita with 9.850. Michigan’s unit was led by Peyton Davis, who scored a 9.925.

Rotation three

The blue and white moved to the bars, and that’s where they actually took a brief lead with Michigan on the floor, Nebraska on the vault and Washington on the beam.

Ava Cash and Elizabeth Leary led an impressive unit with their scores of 9.900, followed by Ellie Monahan’s 9.875. Heading into the final rotation, it looked as if the blue and white had a great shot at victory, until a solid Michigan floor performance pulled it back ahead.

Nebraska and Washington stood entwined in a battle to avoid last place, but the Cornhuskers held the advantage heading into the final rotation.

Rotation four

Michigan and Nebraska kept it rolling and eventually surpassed Penn State on the leaderboard. The Nittany Lions took to the beam with a last shot to leave the meet victorious. The Wolverines strengthened their lead via the vault, with back-to-back clean moves.

However, the blue and white strung together consecutive high-scoring, confident moves on the beam with Ashley Maul’s and Dani Latronica’s 9.950s, and McElligott’s 9.900, they suddenly had a chance to worry the Wolverines.

When the action ceased, the blue and white posted a final score of 197.225, only slightly behind Michigan’s top score of 197.475. Nebraska finished with a 195.200, while Washington had a 194.425.

Up Next

The blue and white continue with another multiteam meet, this time on the road. Penn State heads to Columbus, Ohio, to face Big Ten foe Ohio State, as well as nonconference competition NC State and EMU. Action is set for 2 p.m. Sunday, March 8.

MORE GYMNASTICS COVERAGE

title: 'Guys are really on top of the scouting report' | Penn State men’s basketball beats Iowa in two-way game
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has had many low points in its season so far, but on a particular sunny Saturday during State Patty’s weekend, the Nittany Lions felt rather green.

It was expected to be more melancholy for Mike Rhoades' unit when it hosted Iowa to kick off a two-game homestand. Entering the contest, the blue and white were the second-worst defensive team from beyond the arc in the entire nation, while the Hawkeyes were rather strong from deep. Iowa also entered with an impressive 19-9 record, with an upset win over No. 9 Nebraska still in the rearview.

Yet, as soon as play started, it was the blue and white who rainbowed threes, while every Iowa attempt came up with no pot of gold at the end of it. Penn State finished 60% from three point range, nearly double that of Iowa’s 32%.

The key to it all? Limited attempts and stellar defense. The Nittany Lions attempted only 15 downtown shots compared to Iowa’s 28, and locked up long-range threat Bennett Stirtz, who paces the Hawkeyes in scoring and the Big Ten as well, at No. 3 entering the contest.

Stirtz finished 2-of-7 from beyond the arc for his worst shooting performance in a month, and that streaky clip can be attributed to technical adjustments made by Penn State. Despite playing all 40 minutes, Stritz’s long-ball became obsolete compared to the Nittany Lions' efficient makes.

“I thought Dom Stewart's defense today of Stirtz was awesome. I'm so proud of him,” Rhoades said. “He played a lot of minutes, 30 to 34 minutes and guarded that guy and made some basic plays like, I thought a lot of guys this late in the year are still fighting to help their team be successful, but also they get better.”

Stewart wasn’t the only one; Josh Reed dropped a career-high in points, and big man Ivan Jurić dominated the glass inside. But Rhoades expanded his praise to include those on the sidelines who also helped to the emphatic win.

“I thought Jamal Brunt’s scout for this game was awesome,” Rhoades said. “I think what we prepared for was what they were trying to do to us. So we, our guys, have clear understanding of what we needed to do, now doing it consistently is hard.”

Stirz still dropped 16 points and a game-high five assists, but compared to the over 20 points-per-game average he boasts, that limited impact is a pill Penn State can swallow anytime it faces him.

“You're not going to shut Stirtz out, because he impacts the game in so many different ways. And he's fun to watch too, because he plays the right way,” Rhoades said. “When he has that 25-30 point night, it's over. So we just wanted to make it really hard for him.”

Though Stirtz didn’t have the best outing of his stellar season, he still had a performance that caught the eye and admiration of Rhoades.

“I also think (Stirtz) does a really good job of getting guys on the way to the basket, rolls, cuts, easy baskets and I thought we limited them to that today,” Rhoades said. “Which helped us, so guys are really on top of the scouting report.”

Although the team did a great job rendering the Stirtz three-point threat as obsolete, Rhoades attributed that defense of Iowa’s star to one particular Nittany Lion.

“I thought Dom (Stewart) did a great job on (Stirtz),” Rhoades said. “When we switched other guys on him, I thought we did a good job of following the scouting report, and then we rebounded enough to give ourselves a chance.”

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball drops second-straight game to Indiana State in pitching duel
author: Frankie Gallagher | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State and Indiana State met in Nassau for their second matchup of the weekend, laying the groundwork for an impressive pitching matchup between sophomores Ben Hudson and Ty Brooks

The Sycamores survived a late-game comeback bid, beating the Nittany Lions 2-1 to take the series on Saturday.

The first inning was quiet, with Ty Brooks striking out leadoff hitter Cohl Mercado. Michael Anderson flew out to left field for the second out, followed by third baseman Bryce Molinaro getting hit by a pitch. To end the top of the first, second baseman Jayden Davis flies out to center fielder Caden Miller.

Pitcher Ben Hudson opens in a similar fashion, striking out Ortiz and getting out of the first inning unscathed.

Spencer Barnett tallied the first hit of the game with a single to shallow center field. The Nittany Lions failed to capitalize and retired after a groundout from left fielder Jesse Jaconski and a strikeout from catcher Avery Smith.

The Sycamores went three up, three down, with the Nittany Lions’ defense playing impressive defense through a grounder to Davis, a fly ball caught by Porter, and a calmly fielded ground ball to Hudson.

Offensive struggles continued for Penn State through the third inning, failing to get a base runner. Indiana State finally got its first hit of the game with second baseman Sebastian Torres sneaking a hit through the infield with two outs. Ortiz grounded out to the pitcher, keeping the game scoreless.

Brooks kept his foot on the gas pedal to start the fourth, striking out Molinaro. Davis was walked, but two groundouts ended the inning. Still scoreless, the Nittany Lions looked to Hudson to keep the shutout going.

That wouldn’t last, however, as Carter Beck got walked and stole second. With Beck in scoring position, the Sycamores successfully brought him home with a hit along the right field line from Miller. The Sycamores drew first blood, entering the fifth inning with a 1-0 lead.

Penn State responded with a homer over the left field fence, the swing coming from first baseman Spencer Barnett to even the score. Consecutive strikeouts from Jaconski and Smith, followed by a pop fly from Yaucher, would switch the sides.

Hudson took care of all three batters himself to end the fifth. Caleb Niehaus grounded out to the mound, followed by two strikeouts on Torres and Ortiz.

Molinaro recorded the lone hit in the top of the sixth with a single up the middle. The Sycamores’ defense picked up everything else the Nittany Lions could give them, ending the inning without any damage.

Indiana State gave Penn State a scare as Nick Sutherlin drove a single into center and stole a base. Miller ended the inning after going down swinging. Penn State’s Kevin Karstetter replaced an injured Barnett at first.

Both sides of the ballgame would struggle on offense in the seventh inning, with the only offensive input coming from Niehaus through a right field single and a stolen base. Brooks recorded another strikeout, and Hudson answered with two of his own. Still 1-1, both teams looked to take the advantage in the home stretch.

The first pitching change came at the beginning of the eighth, with Sycamores’ coach Tracy Archuleta pulling Brooks for senior Colby Morse after an eight-strikeout performance. Morse had a rocky start, giving up a single and a sacrifice fly to his first two batters. However, he forced the remaining two outs on pop flies and kept pinch runner Joey DeMucci at third.

DeMucci replaced Smith at catcher in the next half inning, and coach Mike Gambino pulled Hudson for senior Ben DeMell.

The top of the ninth opened with both Molinaro and Davis getting walked by Morse, causing him to be replaced by Justin Hoff. In his second-ever appearance for the Sycamores, he set up a double play to Niehaus and a ground out to close out the game.

Injury Report

Barnett left the game in the sixth inning with an apparent injury.

Up Next

Penn State will close out its series against Indiana State at noon on Sunday.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State track and field's Ryan Henry wins Big Ten shot put title
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After big improvements throughout the regular season, Ryan Henry is now a champion.

Henry won the men's shot put at the 2026 Big Ten Indoor Track and Field Championship, throwing a distance of 19.94 meters.

At the Nittany Lion Challenge, Henry tied the fourth-best mark in Penn State history and then set his own personal mark at the Penn State National Open.

On Saturday, he threw the No. 3 distance in Penn State history, winning the event despite coming into the weekend with the fourth-best mark in the conference.

MORE TRACK AND FIELD COVERAGE

title: No. 25 Penn State softball comes alive late, not enough to get past No.1 Tennessee
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
After an early Saturday afternoon win over Appalachian State, Penn State had little time to rest before its rematch with No. 1 Tennessee. This marked the first game of the day for the Lady Volunteers, while the Nittany Lions looked to continue their momentum.

For the second time in less than 24 hours, Penn State fell in a tight battle with Tennessee, this time by a score of 3-1.

The Lady Volunteers started back-to-back SEC Pitcher of the Year Karlyn Pickens, who was making her first appearance of the weekend. Tennessee’s defense backed Pickens up, setting Penn State down in order.

Coach Clarisa Crowell called on Bridget Nemeth, also for the right-handers first time in the circle at the Tennessee Invitational.

The Lady Volunteers found themselves with a bases-loaded one-out situation, but only came away with one run after Nemeth hit first baseman Emma Clarke with a pitch on a 0-2 count.

In the top of the second, catcher Madison Seidel worked a walk to become the Nittany Lions’ first base runner of the game, but three strikeouts from Pickens kept the blue and white from posing any threats.

Penn State recorded a quick out, but after Nemeth allowed a free base to shortstop Bella Faw, center fielder Sophia Knight knocked a single, and Tennessee threatened once again.

In a chaotic series of events, Gabby Leach grounded a ball to second baseman Michela Barbenente, who attempted to throw Faw out at home. Faw slid in for a run, but Seidel threw to second as Leach rounded first.

Seidel’s throw allowed Knight to make her towards home. After Leach was called safe, shortstop Kaylie Walters threw back to Seidel at home to try to catch Knight. Knight was originally called out, but Tennessee coach Karen Weekly immediately went to the umpires, looking for obstruction.

After a review, it was ruled that Seidel was in the base path, blocking Knight from scoring. This resulted in the Lady Volunteers extending their lead to 3-0 heading into the third inning.

Pickens and the Tennessee defense stayed on lockdown, preventing any Nittany Lions from reaching base as the blue and white remained hitless through three plate appearances.

Penn State mirrored in the home half of the third, keeping the Lady Volunteers scoreless for the first time of the afternoon.

The Nittany Lions brought their two, three and four, hitters to the plate, hoping to get something going in the fourth inning.

Designated player Brooke Klosowicz worked an one-out walk, and advanced all the way to third after a wild pitch and a fielder's choice. Center fielder Natalie Lieto stepped into the box with a chance to bring Klosowicz home, but instead became Pickens’ sixth strikeout victim as Penn State came up empty in the fourth.

Knight reached on a two-out single, but Tennessee couldn’t add on any more, still holding onto its 3-0 lead heading into the fifth inning.

Seidel hit a slow roller to Ella Dodge at second base to start the inning, but it was enough to give the Nittany Lions their first hit of the game.

Right fielder Cara Bohner tapped a short dribbler to Pickens, allowing pinch runner Kalli Karwowski to advance to second. Breanna Hanik followed with a double to the left side, bringing Karwowski home for Penn State’s first run of the afternoon.

The Nittany Lions couldn’t add on any more runs but managed to cut their deficit to two runs entering the home half of the fifth.

Penn State’s defense stayed in the zone, shutting down the Lady Volunteers in order. It was the blue and white’s bats' turn to try and break through against the nation's No.1 team.

Allison Onecare got things going quickly, taking the first pitch she saw to the left field fence, just inches away from making it a one-run game.

Klosowicz moved Oneacre over on a ground ball fielded by catcher Elsa Morrison, but two more groundouts prevented Oneacre from making her way home.

Tennessee put two runners in scoring position with just one out. Nemeth struck out pinch-hitter McCall Sims, handing things back to the top of the order.

With Knight coming up for the fourth time, Crowell turned to the bullpen, bringing in left-hander Brevyn Kellepouris. The freshman only needed to face one batter as Penn State escaped the threat, but was down to its last at-bat, down 3-1.

To start the final frame, Seidel grounded out to Pickens and Bohner struck out, bringing up Hanik as the Nittany Lions' final hope. The freshman worked a walk, allowing Walters to step into the box as the game-tying run.

Walters battled Pickens to a full count but ended as the pitcher's ninth strikeout victim, as Penn State fell short against Tennessee for the second time.

Up Next

Penn State closes out the Tennessee Invitational at 10 a.m. Sunday with a second meeting against NC Central.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: No. 20 Penn State women’s lacrosse remains undefeated, defeats Cornell on road
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
No. 20 Penn State is off to one of the best starts in program history in its first year under coach Kayla Treanor. The Nittany Lions faced another ranked opponent, as they traveled to Ithaca, New York, to play Cornell.

Penn State continued its undefeated start to the season, earning another major victory with an 11-9 victory over the Big Red to move to 6-0.

Despite not having Erika Ho back to full strength, Penn State hadn’t had issues scoring. The success of the Nittany Lions’ attack continued against the Big Red.

Payton Wainman and Delaney Radin combined for the first three goals, with Wainman finding the back of the net twice.

After a goal from Cornell’s Dillyn Patten, Penn State freshman star Rocquette Allen got herself on the board. Allen notched her team-high 16th goal of the season, proving to be the key scorer, while Ho worked her way back.

Just over two minutes later, Kelly MacKinney found the back of the net on an assist from Radin. Radin’s playmaking ability has been on full showcase throughout the season as she continues to raise her assist numbers.

Trailing 5-1, Patten got another past Nittany Lion goalie Sydney Manning to end the quarter with Penn State leading 5-2.

A much more balanced quarter took place in the second, as Allen ripped a laser past the Cornell defense to score her second of the game.

Cornell’s Kathleen Michaud responded with a goal of her own to keep the deficit at three, but Penn State answered back a little over a minute later. True freshman Elizabeth Anné found herself alone in the slot and slipped one past Cornell goalie Mackenzie Clark.

A minute later, Michaud got one past Manning, something that was a struggle for the Cornell offense.

Allen notched a hat trick, as she showed off a behind-the-back shot to throw off Clark and end the half with the Nittany Lions leading 8-4. The entry zone defense of the blue and white was giving fits for the Big Red, and when they’d get penetration, Manning was there to shut the door, totaling six saves in the first half alone.

After a quiet first part of the second half, attacker Ellie Wisch scored her first goal of the game to extend Penn State’s lead to 9-4. Wisch’s goal was assisted by MacKinney, tallying the eighth assist for the Nittany Lions in the game, proving that passing opened up shots.

Michaud seemed to have scored her third goal of the game, but a Treanor challenge sent the play to review. But the challenge was unsuccessful and the goal was upheld.

In transition, Patten knocked down a Nittany Lion, giving her a yellow card and a Penn State power play for two minutes.

After the team worked the ball around, Ho scored her first goal of the game in her first game since the blue and white’s win over Bucknell. The Tewaarton Award Watch List member played strictly on the power play as Treanor started to ramp her up to full-time minutes.

Wisch found herself in another position to score, finding the back of the net to extend the lead to 11-6 heading into the fourth quarter.

Cornell started to pick up momentum as back-to-back goals from Lilly Whaling and Lexie Tully pulled the Big Red to within three. Whaling found herself with another opportunity, but Manning shut down the scoring chance to keep the score at 11-8.

Cornell had a major chance to pull even closer as Penn State’s Hannah Hoyt was called for a yellow card, giving the Big Red a two-minute advantage.

The stifling defense of the blue and white shut down the power play chance for Cornell, but a foul on Radin gave the Big Red yet another power-play opportunity.

On this advantage, Whaling scored as the shot clock timer expired. Immediately, Treanor was waving the goal off, and an official review took place to see if Whaling’s goal passed the line prior to the clock hitting zero. The officials quickly called the goal good, as Cornell was within two of the Nittany Lions.

The ensuing faceoff was a mad scramble as Emma Kelly eventually ended up with the ball and was quickly whacked in the face to give a man-up advantage. The Nittany Lions couldn’t score on the power play, but right after Allen attempted another behind-the-back goal, this time denied by Clark.

Penn State attempted to play keep away with just under three minutes left in the game. Multiple late-possession turnovers gave Cornell minimal time to score.

Time ran out, and the Nittany Lions shut the door on a comeback attempt to take the game, 11-9. Manning came up huge throughout the game, totaling 10 saves on 19 shots, for a .526 save percentage.

Up Next

Penn State returns home to take on Colgate at noon on Wednesday in a mid-week afternoon game.

title: No. 3 Penn State women’s hockey outlasts Syracuse in AHA semifinals, advances to conference championship
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 3 Penn State — after a commanding 7-0 victory over Syracuse in Game 1 — returned to Pegula Ice Arena for Game 2 of the best-of-three AHA semifinals at 3 p.m. on Saturday.

The Nittany Lions, although unable to put together a second comfortable win, defeated the Orange 2-1 in a back-and-forth affair. Penn State will make its fourth-consecutive appearance in the AHA Championship game against No. 12 Mercyhurst, defending its three-straight conference titles.

First period

Throughout the opening frame, the blue and white controlled the pace of play, locking the Orange deep in their own zone as a consistent flow of Nittany Lions cycled the puck in the corner while searching for scoring chances. Penn State outshot Syracuse 7-0.

Abby Stonehouse earned Penn State’s first scoring opportunity in the opening minutes of the first period, striding through the slot before lifting a backhanded shot into the pads of linemate Katelyn Roberts. On the following shift, Matilde Fantin tried to pick the top corner over goaltender Ava Drabyk’s shoulder but struck the crossbar.

Tessa Janecke — who scored a hat trick in Game 1 — dangled through the neutral zone before entering a breakaway, but was stopped by Drabyk’s shoulder.

Janecke scored first for Penn State for the second-consecutive contest, breaking the ice with five minutes remaining in the first period. Roberts sent a leading neutral-zone pass to the captain on the opposing blue line, who then had time and space on a breakaway to float a shot over Drabyk’s glove.

Second period

Syracuse, after not putting up a shot-on-goal in the first period, began to build momentum early in the second. Jordan Blouin had the Orange’s first opportunity, a one-timer in the slot that goaltender Katie DeSa stopped with her chest.

However, when DeSa took a tripping penalty to put Penn State on the penalty kill, Syracuse struck fast. Stella Costabile performed a wraparound that bounced off DeSa’s pad onto Jackson Kinsler’s stick, who tapped in the rebound to knot the game, 1-1.

DeSa saw another dangerous opportunity when Rylee McLeod broke free on a clear breakaway with 7:48 remaining in the second period. McLeod made a move to DeSa’s blocker side that she matched and came up with a chest save to keep the game tied.

Maya D’Arcy slammed Janecke to the ice and received a minor penalty for holding late in the second period to give Penn State its third power play of the game. While the first two were unsuccessful, Grace Outwater’s shot was tipped by Stonehouse and found a hole short side under Drabyk’s glove with 1:11 left in the frame.

Third period

Penn State went back on the powerplay as D’Arcy returned to the penalty box, this time for cross checking 36 seconds into the third period. However, Leah Stecker took a slashing minor and evened the sides for a 4-on-4 structure.

While on her knees, Mya Vaslet kicked the puck goalie-style over to Nicole Hall who had an open look at the cage. However, Hall’s short-side shot rang off the iron and the Orange remained within striking distance.

McLeod took a tripping penalty with 10:13 remaining in regulation when her stick caught Janecke’s legs as she carried the puck out of Penn State’s zone. The Nittany Lions were unsuccessful on the advantage, closing out the game 1-for-5 on the powerplay.

With 2:01 left in the game, DeSa made a glove save on a high shot that bounced out of her glove into the slot. Amidst the chaos, the officials lost sight of the puck and called the play dead. After calling a timeout, Syracuse pulled Drabyk with a minute remaining.

The Orange called a second timeout with 21.5 seconds on the clock and used it to generate offense out of the faceoff. DeSa came up with a glove save with seven seconds remaining to secure the series sweep over Syracuse.

Up Next

The Nittany Lions advanced to the AHA Championship and will face No. 12 Mercyhurst, who’d defeated Lindenwood in double-overtime, at 2 p.m. on March 7.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's basketball thumped by Indiana with Big Ten postseason chances in its grasp
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State entered into Indiana's Simon Skjodt Assembly Hall with its best run of form this season, riding a three-game winning streak, the longest of its season, only trailing the four games the Lady Lions won against nonconference opponents to start the year.

Ultimately, former blue and white guard and current Indiana star Shay Ciezki showed that her side was even more fiery and in form, en route to an eventual 93-59 win.

To start the contest, Lady Lions boss Carolyn Kieger sent out a first unit of Gracie Merkle down low, Tèa Clèante, Nyla McFadden, Moriah Murray and Kiyomi McMiller, who entered with a shot at conference history.

McMiller scored over 30 points in her last six games, a streak that ties hoops legend Caitlyn Clark as the longest ever in the Big Ten, but once the action tipped off, it was clear that the record would remain tied.

The offense started extremely streaky for Penn State, and it quickly fell behind by 31-8 in the first quarter. Despite a much better second-quarter performance, the Lady Lions went into the half down 34. The blue and white couldn’t buy a bucket; only Murray finished with efficient shooting marks at the break.

The Lady Lions turned over the ball three times as much at the half, shot under 30%, were outrebounded and also sent the Hoosiers to the charity stripes over five times more. McMiller started 1-of-8 on her shot attempts, while every single Indiana starter except one had reached double-digits at the half.

Out of the break, however, the Lady Lions started with a 9-6 run, but unfortunately, Cleante was banged up by Ciezki and had to be looked at early in the third quarter.

Merkle became the offensive figurehead for Penn State, and despite every attempt the Lady Lions had at gaining momentum, the Hoosiers shut it down with buckets of their own.

Ultimately, it wasn’t even enough to make the game close, as the blue and white entered the fourth quarter behind 72-43.

That last quarter didn’t go any better, and Indiana didn’t let up, as the Hoosiers underwent an 11-point run via a dastardly scoring drought by Penn State that lasted several minutes. Indiana continued to pound on the pressure and cruised home to an easy win.

McMiller finished with four points on 1-of-12 shooting in her quest for history, marking the lowest-scoring game of her season. Merkle led Penn State in scoring with 22 points, while Czieki finished with a game-high 29 points and received a standing ovation when she left the game.

With the result, the blue and white finish its 2026 season at 11-18 and 4-14 in Big Ten play. Kieger has yet to post a season above .500 in conference play after seven years.

Up Next

Penn State has completed its regular season. The Big Ten Tournament will be held from March 4 to 8, and pending results and seeding, the Lady Lions will head to Chicago to participate. A win would’ve guaranteed them a spot, but instead, they need a Northwestern win over Purdue to make it.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State track and field’s Handal Roban wins 800m Big Ten title
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After setting the school record earlier this year, Handal Roban is now a champion.

Roban won the 2026 Indoor Big Ten Track and Field Championship 800-meter title on Saturday with a time of 1:46.44.

The St. Vincent and the Grenadines native ran a time of 1:44.03 at the ASICS Sound Invite earlier this year, putting him first in the Big Ten heading into the championship.

Roban also won the title in 2024 with a time of 1:15.53, but missed the 2025 indoor season due to injury.

MORE TRACK AND FIELD COVERAGE

title: Josh Reed was ‘the best player on the court’ in Penn State basketball’s win over Iowa
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Josh Reed received a pass from Ivan Jurić at the block on the left side of the court as Iowa held a one-point lead over Penn State with just over 45 seconds left. Reed dribbled to his right, jumping up to attempt a layup that would give the Nittany Lions a late lead.

Reed made that layup, which put Penn State in a position to secure a 71-69 win over the Hawkeyes on Saturday afternoon in the Bryce Jordan Center. The basket capped off a 25-point performance by Reed, setting a new career high as his play is peaking toward the end of his collegiate career.

“He was the best player on the court for the whole game,” Rhoades said postgame. “And there's some really good players on the court today, but he was the best player on the court. I was super impressed watching him play, but I'm glad he was on my team today.”

Rhoades said Penn State wanted to play through Reed, who shot 9-of-12 from the field during Saturday’s game. Standout guard Kayden Mingo left the game with 4:04 left due to injury, and Rhoades cited Reed’s performance as why the Nittany Lions managed to respond and pull off a narrow victory.

Reed pulled down three rebounds in addition to his scoring, including two late defensive rebounds that helped generate stops on Iowa’s offense. He had the rebound right before his layup that retook the lead and another board with just 31 seconds left.

The forward, who drew three fouls, made four free throws down the stretch to help the Nittany Lions erase the Hawkeyes’ five-point lead late in the second half. Reed also knocked down 3-of-4 shots from 3-point range.

“Just being confident, trusting myself, knowing that my teammates and coaches also trust me, and just going out there trying to make plays for myself or others,” Reed said postgame. “Then also, people like Freddie and Kayden, they're making plays for me as well, so it was a whole team effort tonight.”

Reed, who is Penn State’s lone senior, transferred in from Cincinnati last offseason for his final year of eligibility. He’s turned this season into a career year, averaging 12.5 points per game with 52.2% shooting from the field.

While with the Bearcats, Rhoades said Reed had a specific role that saw him not have much leeway. Rhoades mentioned that’s different than how the Nittany Lions play, saying he told Reed last June to not look over his shoulder if he makes a mistake.

“I always say, you know who wins the Kentucky Derby? The horse that runs the fastest,” Rhoades said. “He was so aggressive today, like, ‘I'm getting this done.’ And that's running the fastest, let it go. I just think it's the personality trait coming out of him that he has in him, and he's playing incredible.”

Reed has scored in double figures in 19 games this season, including in each of the last 13 games. He’s played multiple positions all year, and the team has played him at power forward more than it has expected.

Rhoades said that Reed “has not complained one time” despite having to guard players who are bigger than him, which highlights the importance he has to Penn State with Senior Day coming up next Wednesday.

“I'm so proud of him,” Rhoades said. “He's a great young man to be around. He's very coachable. He takes ownership in his play, good and bad. He wants to get better. For an older guy, late in the year, tough season, he's still working hard to get better. I think he's taken on my personality and our personality, trying to be really aggressive and let it out. He's done that, and you've seen a guy that's just improved throughout the year.”

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State track and field’s Hayley Kitching wins 800m Big Ten title
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Hayley Kitching’s senior season just keeps getting better.

Kitching won the 800-meter Big Ten title on Saturday with a time of 2:00.55, setting the meet record for the second time this weekend.

The Coffs Harbour, Australia, native is coming off a dominant regular season, where she broke her school record in the 800m with her first-ever sub-two minutes in 1:59.22. She also set the NCAA all-time record in the 1,000-meter with a time of 2:38.45 at the Nittany Lion Challenge.

Kitching now has three indoor Big Ten titles after winning both the 800m and 600m at the 2024 Big Ten indoor championship. She also won the outdoor title in the 800m later that year.

MORE TRACK AND FIELD COVERAGE

title: No. 15 Penn State men's lacrosse dominates Yale in blowout victory
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
The final day of February saw No. 15 Penn State make its season debut at Panzer Stadium.

Following another upset loss to Navy in Annapolis, the Nittany Lions had an opportunity to bounce back against Yale on Saturday, a chance that they would capitalize on.

The blue and white defeated Yale 17-7, notching their third win of the season.

“You could tell the energy coming out of the locker room today was at a different level,” coach Jeff Tambroni said.

Colby Baldwin won the initial faceoff for Penn State, but the game’s first goal came just under three minutes into the contest when Kyle Lehman fed cutting attackman Jack Iannantuono, who dunked on the Yale cage to open the scoring.

The Nittany Lions found the back of the net again less than a minute later, when Luke Walstrum scored on a close shot fed by Iannantuono, the previous goalscorer.

Iannantuono recorded his third point in under five minutes when he dodged from behind the net and sunk another ball past Yale goaltender Ben Friedman to put the blue and white up 3-0, which forced a timeout from Bulldogs coach Andy Shay.

“He’s played with so much poise for a freshman,” Tambroni said, “and we’re putting him in a really tough position. To play behind the goal, that quarterbacks a lot of what’s going on.”

Yale finally gained offensive possession soon after, but quickly turned the ball over. Luke Pascal got the Bulldogs on the board with just over five minutes remaining in the frame with a strong sidearm shot from ten yards out.

Star attackman Hunter Aquino responded to the Yale goal with his first goal of the game, being assisted again by Iannantuono. The opening quarter came to a close with Penn State leading 4-1, with Iannantuono being involved in all four goals.

Reid Gillis won the second quarter’s initial faceoff, giving Penn State a possession that would result in another goal, this one coming from the stick of senior midfielder Michael Faraone.

“Our faceoff has been a strength for us,” Tambroni said. “Both those guys do a great job, both Reid and Colby have been very consistent thus far.”

The Nittany Lion lead stretched to 6-1 when senior defenseman Will Costin stretched the field on the clear, finishing on the cage for the pole goal.

While initially disallowed due to a crease violation, a dive on the cage from midfielder Mark Watters was ruled a goal after a challenge from Tambroni to give Penn State a six-goal lead near the midpoint of the second quarter.

Penn State recorded another quick goal with 7:15 remaining in the frame when Patrick Carragher scored off another assist from Iannantuono, giving him five first-half points.

Yale ended the five-goal Nittany Lion run soon after on a man-up goal from senior attackman David Anderson.

Andrew Beard scored Penn State’s ninth goal of the first half on a close shot after a quick feed from Walstrum with just over two minutes remaining in the half. The second quarter expired with the Nittany Lions holding a 9-2 lead.

Penn State opened the second-half scoring with a feed from Walstrum to Liam Matthews, who dunked on Friedman to stretch the Nittany Lion lead to eight. They’d light the lamp again after a hidden-ball trick from behind the net, followed by another feed from Walstrum that was finished off by Chase Robertson.

The Bulldogs managed to find another goal when Sean Grogan scored off a feed from Asher Ziv. They’d get another soon after when Pascal scored his second of the game with 8:45 remaining in the third frame.

Yale scored their third unanswered goal when Cole Cashion scored on an outside shot to record his first goal of the season, but the run would be ended by Walstrum with a goal to mark his fifth point of the game.

John Jude Considine regained the eight-goal lead for Penn State with a sweet dodge around a defender and a smooth finish into the Yale net. Lehman scored another after almost losing the ball, before repossessing it and firing it past Friedman.

Penn State’s lead was stretched to 10 after a feed from Aquino resulted in a second consecutive goal from Considine, giving the Nittany Lions a 15-5 lead at the end of the third quarter.

Iannantuono continued to build on his career day, feeding Faraone for his sixth point of the contest. The four-goal Penn State run was ended by a man-up score from William Sheehan, following an assist by Connor Gately

Grogan would score his second of the game on a dodge over the top, shooting sidearm to blast one past Penn State goaltender Preston Hawkins.

Redshirt junior Wesley Scurci, who transferred to Penn State after his freshman campaign at Lindenwood, notched his first goal as a Nittany Lion with just over four minutes remaining in the contest.

The fourth quarter came to a close with the Nittany Lions holding a 17-7 lead. The scoring total marked their second-highest on the season, and seven goals allowed tied for the least allowed by the defense on the campaign.

“We’ve had leads in the fourth quarter against Villanova and Navy, and we’ve let those slip through our hands,” coach Jeff Tambroni said. “We just tried to reset after each quarter, every timeout, to make sure the mindset was ‘what else can we do’ rather than celebrating what just happened.”

Up Next

The Nittany Lions will stay at home for a tough test next week, with a matchup against No. 3 Cornell set to face off at 1 p.m. from Panzer Stadium. The Big Red were upset by No. 4 Richmond on Saturday, falling 8-7 in Ithaca.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: No. 25 Penn State softball's offense erupts again, defeats Appalachian State
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State took the field on Saturday afternoon for Day 2 of the Tennessee Invitational after going 1-1 on Friday. The Nittany Lions faced Appalachian State in their first of two matchups of the day. The Mountaineers posted an identical record to the blue and white on Friday, while also defeating NC Central early Saturday morning.

The Nittany Lions defeated Appalachian State 10-5 behind a six-run fourth inning.

Penn State was listed as the home team, and freshman right-hander Mackenzie Duncan got her second start of the weekend in the circle.

Center fielder Julia Girk worked a lead-off walk to start the opening frame, but a strikeout and double play set the rest of the Mountaineers down in order.

Appalachian State turned to senior left-hander Sophie Moshos, and the Nittany Lions attacked her right away. Second baseman Michela Barbanente knocked a single to center field and was brought all the way around thanks to a home run from third baseman Allison Oneacre. This marked the freshman's ninth long ball of the season.

Moshos allowed a walk to put one more runner on, but shut down the rest of the Nittany Lions. However, the blue and white needed just two hits to take an early two-run lead.

The Mountaineers couldn’t get anything back in the top of the second behind a groundout and two strikeouts from Duncan, turning things over back to Penn State quickly.

The bottom of the second inning mirrored the top as Moshos and the Appalachian State defense set the Nittany Lions down in order.

After seeing just one pitch from Duncan, first baseman Emilie Ching ripped one over the center field fence to put the Mountaineers on the board.

Duncan and the Penn State defense recorded the next two outs, but a throwing error from Oneacre that could’ve closed out the inning instead allowed Girk to reach second base. A single from second baseman Makayla McClain brought Girk home, tying up the game with an unearned run.

Shortstop Grace Barrett kept the party going with another single, putting two runners on for the Mountaineers as the Nittany Lions made a pitching change.

Right-hander McKenna Young entered for Penn State, and after a nine-pitch battle with third baseman Taylor Schafer, the batter knocked a single, bringing in both Appalachian State runners.

The tides turned quickly as the Mountaineers turned a two-run deficit into a two-run lead heading into the home half of the third.

With the Nittany Lions’ top of the order due up, Moshos and her defense recorded two quick outs. Appalachian State couldn’t fully avoid damage as designated player Brooke Klosowicz hit a home run to left field, cutting the blue and white’s deficit to 4-3.

Penn State posted a clean sheet in the fourth inning and Young recorded her first strikeout of the afternoon, giving the Nittany Lions a chance to chip into the Mountaineers’ lead.

Chipping into the lead is just what the blue and white did. On just the second pitch of the frame, first baseman Kirsten Finarelli launched the third Penn State home run of the game, tying the contest at four.

Appalachian State turned to the bullpen, bringing in right-hander Camryn Fisher, who quickly allowed the Nittany Lions to load up the bases.

Gabby Gradishar stepped into the box as a pinch hitter with no outs and three ducks in the pond. The freshman worked a nine-pitch at bat and won the battle as Fisher walked in the go-ahead run.

Barbanente followed with a ground ball to third base, but a throwing error by Schafer allowed Barbanente to make it all the way to third and all three runs to score.

Oneacre brought Barbenete home for the sixth and final run of the inning as Penn State held a 9-4 cushion heading into the fifth frame.

Right-hander Abigail Britton entered the circle for the Nittany Lions, quickly giving up a single to McClain, who was brought home on a double from Barrett. That was all the Mountaineers got back as Penn State held a four-run lead.

In the home half of the inning, doubles off the bats of center fielder Natalie Lieto and left fielder Breanna Hanik gave the blue and white a run back entering the sixth frame.

Appalachian State came up empty at the top of the inning, going three up, three down. Penn State posted a threat in the home half, putting runners on second and third with one out. Fisher escaped the scare as the Mountaineers entered their last at bat down 10-5.

Left-hander Brevyn Kellepouris stepped foot into the circle in hopes of closing out Game 1 for the Nittany Lions.

Appalachian State put two runners on, but couldn’t bring any runs home, guiding Penn State to its 15th win on the season.

Up Next

Penn State has a quick turnaround, facing No. 1 Tennessee for the second time at 3 p.m Saturday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Shamrock success | Penn State men’s basketball defeats Iowa in State Patty’s matinee game
author: Chase Mitchell | The Daily Collegian
description:
With the sun shining down on the Bryce Jordan Center on a warm February Saturday, Penn State welcomed Iowa in the two squads’ first meeting this season.

Mike Rhoades’ career record against the Hawkeyes improved to 2-2 in the Nittany Lions’ penultimate home game this season, as the blue and white picked up a 71-69 last-second victory.

“It feels really good. Kind of just shows us what we’re capable of, you know, we’ve had flashes of that throughout the season,” Josh Reed said.

Iowa won the opening tipoff and looked like it was about to get an easy bucket until Kayden Mingo poked the ball out of bounds, but Cooper Koch knocked down a triple off the inbound and gave the Hawkeyes an early 3-0 lead.

The third-highest scorer in the Big Ten, Bennett Stirtz, almost added on to Iowa’s 7-3 lead with a three-pointer, but rimmed it out and jogged away disappointed as Freddie Dilione V got the rebound.

At the first media timeout of the game, the black and gold held a 9-7 lead after scores from

Reed and Ivan Jurić, but a Kayden Mingo foul sent Stirtz to the charity stripe, where he drilled both of them to extend the Hawkeyes’ lead.

Dilione hit from downtown to give the blue and white another three points and tie the game at 13-13 before hitting another trey less than a minute later to give Penn State its first lead of the game.

The redshirt junior from Fayetteville, North Carolina, is averaging 13.9 points per game and is shooting 29.2% from three-point range. He entered Saturday’s game having attempted 113 total three-pointers this season – the most of any Nittany Lion.

A beautiful pass from Stirtz led to an uncontested triple from Tate Sage, which gave the Hawkeyes a 21-16 advantage.

The three-pointers were flying all over, as Reed hit one for Penn State, which tied the game at 21-21 until Stirtz immediately followed with a three-pointer of his own that put the Hawkeyes back out in front.

Stirtz, who averages 20.7 points per game, is shooting 39.8% from three-point land this season.

Dominick Stewart drained a three-pointer off the assist from Dilione that got the Bryce Jordan Center buzzing as the Nittany Lions took a 26-24 lead, but the celebration was short-lived as Koch wrote back with two more three-pointers.

On fire, Koch knocked down his fourth three-pointer of the first half with just over a minute to play as Iowa captured a 36-34 lead. Koch’s triple was the 14th three-point make of the day from either side.

The Nittany Lions put the ball in Dilione’s hands for their last shot of the first half, but it was off, and Stirtz went on the fast break and gave Iowa two more points as halftime arrived with the Hawkeyes holding a 38-34 lead.

Just like the first half, Koch opened up the scoring in the second frame with a triple to give the Hawkeyes a comfortable seven-point lead.

The freshman forward gave himself 15 points after the make on 5-for-6 shooting from beyond the arc – the most points of the game on either side to that point.

A flashy play from Jurić under the rim saw him pull out a little spin move into a hook shot to tie the game at 45-45 with 15 minutes left to play.

Not long after, Penn State grabbed its first lead since near the end of the first half thanks to a Mingo three-pointer that gave him nine points after being held relatively quiet throughout the day.

Scores were traded back and forth for several minutes, but Iowa looked like it could pull away anytime soon after picking up the momentum backed by Stirtz and Alvaro Folgueiras buckets that made Penn State’s 53-51 lead disappear and turn into a three-point Hawkeye lead.

Jurić grabbed the Nittany Lions’ first offensive rebound and turned it into a tip-in make, but Mingo went down and looked to be in pain, not getting up for a little bit until he had to be helped up. He walked off the court on his own and returned to the game a couple of minutes later.

Koch went up to 18 points after an and-one play where he was fouled by Jurić. The score sucked Penn State’s momentum away as its 62-58 lead turned into a five-point deficit.

A referee must have seen something, because Mingo was ushered off the court and taken away to be looked at by the trainers, and Melih Tunca checked in for him. Tunca was one of three Nittany Lions without a single point with just under four minutes left to play.

Stirtz entered foul trouble territory after getting called for a huge foul on Reed, allowing him to knock down both shots from the free throw line to cut Penn State’s deficit to one point with a minute and a half left on the clock.

The senior from Atlanta finished the day with 25 points, four assists and three rebounds. Reed’s 25 points were a new career and season-high.

“Josh Reed was pretty good today. We wanted to play through him,” Rhoades said. “... Today, he was the best player on the court for the whole game.”

Reed reclaimed the lead for the blue and white with 46 seconds left on the clock and it looked like that was all she wrote, but Iowa managed to score off a following inbound with 8.6 seconds left to play.

Just when it looked like overtime might be needed, Dilione was fouled with 1.5 seconds left and knocked down both shots to seal the deal, giving Penn State a 71-69 win to snap a three-game losing streak.

“If you’re the guy that has to make big free throws, you make a big shot and you do that and can do it again because you have that experience,” Rhoades said. “There’s that opportunity, he did it already… I thought he played great.”

Injury Report

Tibor Mirtič, who has been sidelined with a leg injury since early January, was recently downgraded to ‘out’ for the remainder of the season.

Up Next

Penn State returns to the Bryce Jordan Center on Wednesday for its final home game of the season against Ohio State. Tipoff is scheduled for 7:30 p.m, and it’ll be the Nittany Lions’ Senior Night.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Which Penn Staters will impress in the NFL combine? | The 1-0 Podcast
author: The Daily Collegian Football Staff
description:
As former Penn Staters continue deep into the draft process, the current program is continuing to settle in.

The “1-0 Podcast” co-hosts Avery Hill, Lyle Alenstein and Will Horstman got together after the Nittany Lions’ first player availability of the new season.

First, they took a look at transfer quarterback Rocco Becht’s first media experience. The trio also discussed the culture and structural shift Matt Campbell has brought to Penn State.

After that, they turned their attention to the 2026 NFL Scouting Combine, recapping strong performances from Dani Dennis-Sutton and Zane Durant.

Finally, the group discussed a couple of key elements reported on Thursday, including Trace McSorley’s move to the Buffalo Bills.

MORE COVERAGE

title: No. 5 Penn State men's hockey completes comeback, ties Notre Dame, earns extra Big Ten point in shootout
author: Maggie Hardy | The Daily Collegian
description:
With just two series remaining in the regular season and the NCAA Tournament looming, No. 5 Penn State began a crucial final stretch on Friday in South Bend, Indiana.

The blue and white completed a three-goal comeback, tying Notre Dame 3-3, and taking the extra point in the shootout.

For the Nittany Lions looking to make a last-minute climb up the Big Ten standings, fast starts are critical. The blue and white struggled to find that early momentum, however.

The Fighting Irish came out hot despite a history of first-period struggles, and opened the scoring early at Compton Family Ice Arena.

Freshman Dashel Oliver tipped home an initial shot from defenseman Henry Nelson and gave Notre Dame a 1-0 lead about five minutes in.

The Fighting Irish had a prime opportunity to double its lead shortly after the score, when given an early 5-on-3 halfway through the first frame.

Forward Ben Schoen was called for the game’s first penalty, and was joined in the box just 52 seconds later by Jarod Crespo, both being called for tripping.

The blue and white held Notre Dame’s No. 4-ranked powerplay to no score, but the strong penalty kill generated little momentum.

With six minutes remaining in the first period, the Fighting Irish found the back of the net once again. After freshman forward Will Belle intercepted a Nittany Lion pass, he was sent on a breakaway and beat goaltender Josh Fleming up high to register his first collegiate goal.

The Fighting Irish weren’t done with the scoring, however, and less than 30 seconds later, Cole Knuble added on to the lead after beating Fleming with a glove side shot.

Penn State eventually had an answer late in the first, starting with defenseman Mac Gadowsky batting a puck out of the air to keep it in the zone. Gadowsky found freshman Shea Van Olm with a cross-ice pass, who beat Notre Dame goaltender Nicholas Kempf to get on the board.

The blue and white wrapped up the first period trailing 3-1, and getting outshot 16-6.

After all the early excitement, the middle frame was much tamer, although still largely dominated by Notre Dame.

At the halfway mark, the Fighting Irish held a shot advantage of 25-9, but the Nittany Lions began a late surge.

After Shea Van Olm and Henry Nelson got into it at center ice late in the second period, both were called for matching unsportsmanlike conduct penalties.

Notre Dame came inches from going up by three scores, after Sutter Muzzatto rang the puck off the bar, but Penn State took advantage by taking the rebound the length of the ice.
