During the 2024-25 fiscal year, Penn State Athletics reported it spent $3,004,666 on NIL revenue share for the men’s basketball team. The Nittany Lions went 16-15 and 6-14 in Big Ten play last season.

With Rhoades mentioning Penn State not always being at the same level as other Big Ten programs, he highlighted the importance of being correct in player evaluations.

“You got to be right getting the right guys on the board,” Rhoades said. “We've taken pride as a staff of finding those guys that have turned into special players and pros, and they might not be the sexy recruit at the time, but then they turn into one. You got to be okay with that.”

Tibor Mirtič out for season

Forward Tibor Mirtič hasn’t played in the last 13 games due to injury, and Rhoades confirmed Thursday that he doesn’t anticipate getting him back this season.

“He’ll be done,” Rhoades said.

Mirtič exited the Michigan game on Jan. 6 with a knee injury. While rehabbing that injury, he injured his shoulder during his first practice back. He was a rotational player in the frontcourt when he did see the court, averaging 3.7 points and 3.3 rebounds per game.

Mirtič is listed as a freshman on Penn State’s roster, but he was given two years of eligibility by the NCAA as he’s a 2003-born player who has professional experience overseas in his home country of Slovenia. Since he played in 15 games in 2025-26, it doesn’t appear he would be able to attain a medical redshirt for this season.

Week off

Penn State had more time off this week as it didn’t have an early-week game. After getting back from Nebraska late Saturday night, the team took Sunday and Monday off.

“We had a crazy stretch there, but everybody does, so no excuses, but just letting the guys be able to get their bodies to calm down a little bit, get some real sleep, some longer sleep, just get to some type of normalcy here on campus I think was great for them,” Rhoades said. “Having those two days off as good has been very good for them.”

The Nittany Lions were back practicing on Tuesday, which was a "great practice,” according to Rhoades. Penn State had Wednesday off as well, but players were still in the weight room, training room or the gym getting shots up.

It’ll be another day of practice on Thursday before the Iowa game tips off at noon Saturday in the Bryce Jordan Center.

“Just getting rid of some of the fatigue that we had to endure here the last couple weeks,” Rhoades said.

MORE BASKETBALL CONTENT

title: Mock draft roundup | Where are Penn State players projected ahead of 2026 NFL Scouting Combine?
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
The 2026 NFL Scouting Combine is underway this week, giving nine former Penn State players the chance to showcase their skills.

Those Nittany Lions will also have the opportunity to boost their NFL draft stock as scouts evaluate where they might place them on their big board.

With the pre-draft process continuing, here’s where the latest mock drafts have ex-Penn Staters getting selected.

Vega Ioane, left guard, 1st round

Left guard Vega Ioane has multiple first-round projections with ESPN and NBC Sports each expecting him to go No. 22 overall to the Los Angeles Chargers. PFF has a mock draft of Ioane going No. 14 to the Baltimore Ravens. The Draft Network slots him in at No. 24 to the Cleveland Browns.

Ioane was a starter for the Nittany Lions the past two seasons. In 2025, he played in 11 games before achieving second-team All-America honors for his efforts.

Zakee Wheatley, safety, mid rounds

Safety Zakee Wheatley has Day 2 potential in mock drafts, with PFF predicting a Round 3 pick from the Los Angeles Rams. Sports Illustrated projects a Round 3 pick to the Chicago Bears for Wheatley, while The Draft Network expects Round 4 to the Las Vegas Raiders.

Wheatley was the veteran presence in Penn State's safety group this past season, tallying 74 tackles, two tackles for loss, an interception and a pass breakup.

Dani Dennis-Sutton, defensive end, rounds 2-4

Though early indications gave a first-round grade to Dani Dennis-Sutton, a recent mock draft by A to Z Sports sees Dennis-Sutton getting drafted in the third round at pick No. 79. PFF’s pre-combine mock draft has him going No. 84 to the Green Bay Packers.

Dennis-Sutton’s calling card is his ability to cause disruption in the backfield, totaling 42 tackles, 12 tackles for loss, 8.5 sacks and three forced fumbles in 2025.

Drew Allar, quarterback, mid rounds

Drew Allar has a wide range for where he is getting selected in mock drafts with NFL.com projecting him going in Round 3 at No. 76 overall to the Pittsburgh Steelers while Saturday Blitz has him going No. 209 overall to the Los Angeles Rams. ESPN ranks him as the No. 3 quarterback, and he’s the No. 138 overall player by PFF.

Allar endured a season-ending ankle injury during 2025 but has recovered and will throw in the combine, according to Adam Schefter of ESPN. He’s passed for 7,402 yards, 61 touchdowns and 13 interceptions in his Penn State career.

Nick Singleton, running back, mid rounds

Running back Nick Singleton was projected to the Denver Broncos at pick No. 94 overall by BroncosWire while PFF expects him to get selected by the Philadelphia Eagles at pick No. 98 overall. He’s the No. 120 player overall by PFF and the No. 8 running back by ESPN.

Singleton broke his foot during a practice at the Senior Bowl and isn’t expected to participate in on-field drills at the combine. He became Penn State’s all-time leading touchdown scorer last season with 55 career touchdowns.

Kaytron Allen, running back, mid rounds

Running back Kaytron Allen ranges from late Day 2 to early Day 3 in mock drafts as the Denver Post and Sports Illustrated has him going in Round 3 to the Denver Broncos and Jacksonville Jaguars, respectively. Clutch Points also expects Allen to go in Round 4.

Allen is the Nittany Lions’ all-time leading rusher with 4,180 career yards, including 1,303 yards during the 2025 season.

Drew Shelton, left tackle, mid rounds

Left tackle Drew Shelton is another mid-round prospect in mock drafts with Draftwire projecting him to get selected in the third round. Vikings Wire has Shelton going in the fifth round to the Minnesota Vikings and Bleacher Report grades Shelton as a fifth-round prospect.

Shelton was a mainstay on the offensive line last season, starting every regular season game before opting out of the Pinstripe Bowl.

Zane Durant, defensive tackle, mid rounds

Defensive tackle Zane Durant has garnered Day 3 buzz in mock drafts as The Draft Network has him going in Round 4 to the Pittsburgh Steelers. Bleacher Report gave Durant a fourth round grade.

Durant appeared in all 12 regular season games last year, tallying 25 tackles, 4.5 tackles for loss, four sacks and an interception.

Khalil Dinkins, tight end, late rounds-undrafted

Tight end Khalil Dinkins is ranked as the No. 188 overall player by Bleacher Report and No. 398 overall player by PFF ahead of on-field drills at the combine.

Dinkins was known for his blocking in both the run and pass game during his time as a Nittany Lion. He had 14 catches for 167 yards and two touchdowns in 2025 as well.

Nolan Rucci, offensive tackle, late rounds-undrafted

Offensive tackle Nolan Rucci wasn’t invited to the combine, but his name popped up in a mock draft by Last Word on Sports, projecting him getting selected in the sixth round by the Detroit Lions.

Rucci was another starter on the offensive line for Penn State in 2025, appearing in all 12 regular season games.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

Vikings Wire has Shelton going in the fifth round to the Minnesota Vikings and Bleacher Report grades Shelton as a fifth-round prospect.

title: No. 25 Penn State softball set for big test against No. 1 Tennessee
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
Tennessee Invitational this weekend. No. 25 Penn State will face its biggest test of the regular season, with two games against the top-ranked Lady Volunteers.

The games are slated at 5:30 p.m. Friday and at 3 p.m. Saturday. This will be just the second and third meetings between the two squads. The lone previous matchup ended in a 9-1 Tennessee win over the Nittany Lions in Happy Valley in 2007.

Tennessee enters its home invitational undefeated at 14-0 on the season, while recording six ranked wins over Oregon, Nebraska, Florida Atlantic, UCLA, Florida State and Liberty. Penn State is fresh off an undefeated weekend at the Space U Classic, which included its first-ranked victory of the season over then-No. 25 UCF.

Powers clash

The blue and white’s explosive offense will clash with the Lady Volunteers’ shutdown pitching. Tennessee holds the lowest ERA in the SEC at .32 and has surrendered just four earned runs off 32 hits and one home run over 87 innings.

Meanwhile, Penn State has been raking in its red-hot start, scoring 121 runs off 132 hits, 104 RBI and 20 home runs. The key to the upset will be the blue and white’s offense cracking one of the best pitching staffs in the nation.

The Lady Volunteers are batting .340 while limiting their opponents to just a .112 average and are also out scoring them with a whopping 113 runs to five. Penn State can’t waste its opportunities with runners on base because they rarely come against Tennessee.

However, the Nittany Lions pitching has also contributed greatly to the winning streak, with a low 2.10 ERA, while surrendering 71 hits, 30 earned runs and just four home runs. They will have their hands full facing the Lady Volunteers steady offense.

Despite its pitching being its strong suit, Tennessee has also accumulated 118 hits and 20 home runs.

Players to watch

Both teams are loaded with star players in both pitching and batting. Sophia Knight holds the best batting average for the Lady Volunteers at .543, delivering 25 hits and drawing seven walks in 46 plate appearances. Mackenzie Butt and Ella Dodge have both homered a team-best four times.

Tennessee's whole pitching staff is loaded, but Karlyn Pickens has been utilized the most this season. Pickens has thrown a 0.22 ERA with 42 strikeouts over 31.1 innings.

Freshman Allison Oneacre spearheads Penn State’s offense, with two straight Big Ten Freshman of the Week honors. The Alpharetta, Georgia, native has been on fire, leading the team with a .419 average, along with 18 hits, 27 RBI and seven home runs over 43 at-bats.

The freshmen have been the stars early on both sides of the ball. Brevyn Kellepouris was pivotal in multiple games at the Space U Classic. The Olathe, Kansas, native has totaled a team-best 0.71 ERA, with 15 strikeouts over 19.2 innings.

All cylinders must be clicking for Penn State to put the softball world on notice and win over Tennessee.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s basketball gearing up for State Patty’s matinee game against Iowa
author: Chase Mitchell | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is coming off a 23-point loss to Nebraska suffered on Saturday. Now, the Nittany Lions are set to face a team that recently handed the Cornhuskers a surprising five-point loss: Iowa.

The Nittany Lions host the Hawkeyes on Saturday in the two squads’ only meeting this season and first in the Bryce Jordan Center in two years. Tipoff is scheduled for noon.

Iowa sits at 20-8 on the season after defeating Ohio State on Wednesday night. Meanwhile, the blue and white got a week off after suffering a big loss to the Cornhuskers, which could prove to be beneficial as the Hawkeyes have a quick turnaround.

The black and gold is led by senior guard Bennett Stirtz, who is third in the Big Ten in points per game, averaging 20.7 – good for top-20 in the nation.

The 6-foot-4 transfer out of Drake dropped a career-high 36 points against Northwestern in early February and is shooting 50.9% from the field this season.

A huge drop-off in scoring is present with the Hawkeyes, as their second-highest scorer, guard Tavion Banks, averages 10.6 points per game, but leads the team with 4.6 rebounds on average.

The 6-foot-7 senior is also statistically Iowa’s best three-point shooter, shooting 43.8% from beyond the arc, though he doesn’t attempt them very often.

Stirtz is the real weapon from three-point range, attempting and making the most triples on his squad this season, shooting 39.9% from downtown.

With a season-high of 75% on 6-for-8 three-point shooting against Ole Miss back in November, Stirtz contributes to Iowa being the fourth-best three-point shooting team in the Big Ten, while Penn State is dead last in the same category.

The Nittany Lions are second-to-last in the nation in defending the three-point shot, meaning Stirtz and the Hawkeyes should have the green light from start to finish.

Guards Freddie Dilione V and Kayden Mingo are both averaging 13.9 points per game, though Mingo also leads the blue and white in assists and steals.

Despite leading the team in minutes, assists and steals, the highest-rated recruit in Penn State program history is also responsible for the most turnovers with 48 on the season.

As the blue and white’s best on-ball defender, Mingo will most likely be covering Stirtz for most of the action.

Dilione was held to a measly five points in the Nittany Lions’ loss to Nebraska but recorded a career-high 25 points in two games this season.

The redshirt junior will have a hard time getting back into rhythm as he’ll have his hands full with one of the premier defenses in the nation that allows opponents to score just 65.1 points per game.

With big man Tibor Mirtič still injured, Penn State will look to seven-footer Ivan Jurić to create some high-percentage looks from inside.

Jurić is coming off a 13 point, 10 rebound double-double against Nebraska and is looking for what could be his third this season against the Hawkeyes.

The Zagreb, Croatia, native will likely guard Spaniard Alvaro Folgueiras, who is shooting 50% from the field. The Robert Morris transfer averages 20.8 minutes as a key rotation player off the bench.

The tale of two opposites sees first-year Hawkeye coach Ben McCollum visit Happy Valley for the first time in his illustrious 16-year career, seeking a win over a Big Ten bottom-feeder.

Meanwhile, Mike Rhoades looks to improve his all-time record against Iowa to 2-2. Rhoades’ pair of losses against the Hawkeyes occurred in Iowa City, while the lone win was at home in the Bryce Jordan Center.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Staff predictions | Which Penn Staters could deliver top performances at NFL combine?
author: The Daily Collegian football staff
description:
Drivers, start your engines.

Nine Penn State players are expected to be in attendance for the 2026 NFL Scouting Combine in Indianapolis. While the status of Nick Singleton remains in question, the expectation is that the other eight, including Drew Allar, will participate in some capacity.

Last year, it was safety Jaylen Reed who made headlines among former Nittany Lions with a 4.49 40-yard dash.

Here’s who we think could put up big numbers or strong performances in on-field drills for this year’s version of everyone’s favorite shirt and shorts showcase.

Avery Hill: Zane Durant, defensive tackle

40-yard dash: 4.62 seconds

Short shuttle drill: 4.34 seconds

3-cone drill: 7.12 seconds

Unofficially listed at 6-foot, 290 pounds, Zane Durant wins the combine with impressive numbers in any drill that’s based on speed and explosiveness.

While at Penn State, Durant was highly regarded due to his freak athleticism, and I think he checks off that box on Thursday.

That starts with his 40-yard dash, a drill Durant previously said he runs in around 4.6 seconds. A 4.62-second run would also place him second all-time among defensive tackles in the combine, and that should be his goal.

Regardless of what he runs, he’ll certainly be compared to NFL legend Aaron Donald athletically. That means his short shuttle drill and 3-cone drill times must be in the top percentile among defensive tackles all-time.

A 4.34-second short-shuttle would tie him with last year’s defensive tackle leader, Tyrion Ingram-Dawkins. Clocking 7.12 seconds in the three-cone drill would tie him for fourth with Taven Bryan.

To round out what could be a big day for Durant, expect him to test well in on-field drills, showcasing impressive twitch and instincts as he did in the blue and white.

Lexie Linderman: Zakee Wheatley, safety

40-yard dash: 4.44 seconds

3-cone drill: 6.85 seconds

Vertical jump: 36.5 inches

Although the play that will be remembered most from Zakee Wheatley’s last season at Penn State is a toe-drag touchdown catch by Indiana’s Omar Cooper Jr., Wheatley still had a redshirt senior year to be proud of.

He followed it up with a standout performance at the Senior Bowl, earning Safety of the Week honors from his fellow teammates and scouts.

I expect Wheatley to continue the strong impressions at the NFL Scouting Combine. He’s currently a consensus top-10 safety available, but he has a chance to move his name up that list with a solid performance in Indianapolis this week.

The top 40-yard dash time among safeties last season was 4.37 seconds, and while I don’t expect Wheatley to clock in that fast, I think it’s safe to say he’ll run a faster time than Reed.

Overall, I don’t think Wheatley’s numbers will necessarily pop off the page, but his consistency throughout the combine will leave scouts with his name on their draft boards.

Lyle Alenstein: Dani Dennis-Sutton, defensive end

40-yard dash: 4.78 seconds

3-cone drill: 7.38 seconds

Vertical jump: 35 inches

Entering the season, there was a chance Dani Dennis-Sutton would be the first Nittany Lion selected in the NFL draft, and it could’ve been in the first round.

That no longer seems like the case, as the first former Penn State player likely to be picked will be offensive lineman Vega Ioane, who is drawing first-round projections. Dennis-Sutton has been mocked to go late Round 3.

With that being said, I think Dennis-Sutton can easily boost his stock early into the third round and even become a second-rounder with a strong showing at the combine.

Standing at 6-foot-6, the 256-pound defensive end has a mix of size, strength and speed that could easily appeal to NFL front offices based on upside alone. At Penn State, he showcased his power by bullying offensive lineman and a quick burst off the edge in pursuit of opposing quarterbacks. His high school background of basketball, paired with his ability to jump up and bat down passes at the line of scrimmage also indicates a respectable vertical.

His stock likely saw a boost by playing in the Pinstripe Bowl when he didn’t have to, and likely will handle his meetings with teams well after being a well-spoken voice during his time in State College.

If he tests well during on-field drills, Dennis-Sutton could hear his name called in Round 2.

Will Horstman: Kaytron Allen, running back

40-yard dash: 4.52 seconds

Broad jump: 129 inches

3-cone drill: 6.74 seconds

Running back Kaytron Allen capped off his collegiate career by breaking Penn State’s all-time leading rushing record with 4,180 career yards. He had 1,303 rushing yards in 2025, averaging 6.2 yards per carry.

His combine numbers might not lead the position group, but it’ll further back up his strong finish to his collegiate playing days. Listed at 219 pounds, I think he’ll turn some heads by showcasing a fast speed for a player at his weight.

Allen was known as a power back during his Penn State career, which should translate well to the broad jump. He has the ability to keep his legs moving through contact, so his strong lower body should rattle off a long broad jump.

Performances like his 226-yard day against Rutgers last season is likely what could get Allen selected in Day 2 of the draft, but a strong combine with quality testing numbers would only help his case.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: In the best company | Kiyomi McMiller’s 40-point game against USC compares her to Caitlin Clark
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Amidst a three-game winning streak, Penn State has looked the best it has all year, mainly due to Kiyomi McMiller’s success.

In the team’s upset win over USC on Wednesday, McMiller recorded her sixth straight 30-point outing as she continues to lead the team with 21.6 points per game

“She’s been a terror lately and super confident,” coach Carolyn Kieger said. “It’s obviously contagious right now with their teammates.”

In addition to tying women’s basketball legend Caitlin Clark’s Big Ten record for six straight 30-point games, McMiller also set another milestone on Wednesday as she reached 1,000 points on her career. Despite only being a sophomore, McMiller moved within six points of reaching Penn State’s top-40 scorers of all time.

After a pair of three-pointers gave the Trojans an early 6-0 lead, McMiller earned her first basket of the day off her first of many triples.

In response to asking what led to Penn State’s 3-point success — shooting 57.9% from deep — Kieger pointed at McMiller and said, “Seven for 10? She’s the one to ask.”

In part to her early-game 3-point success, including multiple 30-foot buckets, the former Rutgers star recorded 20 points with 3:49 left in the first half, ultimately scoring 22 points in the first 20 minutes and cutting the deficit to seven points heading into the half.

However, not all of her points came from deep, as McMiller also had highlight plays, taking the ball to the basket, performing multiple shot fake-layups in a single drive.

On the defensive end, the Silver Springs, Maryland, native also recorded three steals and took each of them the other way down the court for fastbreak opportunities.

“It’s just rewarding for her to see the hard work come into fruition and play complete games,” Kieger said at the weekly media session on Tuesday. “I think the only thing she cares about right now is winning.”

That’s exactly what she did against USC, as she scored nearly half of the team’s 85 points in a career-high 40-point outing. In the final quarter of action with the Lady Lions trailing 59-58, McMiller contributed to nine of Penn State’s 14 scoring plays with 10 points and four assists.

When she recorded her career 1,000th point in a deep 3-pointer, McMiller looked over at her family, who sat courtside in nearly every home game this season. After being the reason Penn State upset the Trojans — entering the game with just a 7.2% chance of winning — McMiller ran right over to her family after the final buzzer.

“I think I did pretty good,” McMiller said while laughing postgame. “We just gotta keep our mind going and keep our momentum.”

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Report: Penn State TE Andrew Rappleyea to miss spring ball
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
A key returning piece for Penn State has suffered a setback.

Tight end Andrew Rappleyea will miss spring practice, per Blue-White Illustrated. According to the report, the injury is one of "short-term variety" and Rappleyea should return to training this summer.

Rappleyea shared on his Instagram story a photo of himself with a cast on his right foot. He was in attendance at team facilities on Saturday for the THON Explorers Program without any form of boot and was in good spirits. Rappleyea was not made available for a media session of over 30 players at the Beaver Stadium recruiting lounge on Wednesday.

Penn State's tight end room for spring ball will consist of Iowa State transfers Ben Brahmer and Cooper Alexander as well as returners in Finn Furmanek and Brian Kortovich. Per Tyler Donahue of 247Sports, Iowa State transfer tight end Gabe Burkle will not be available for spring practice either.

Rappleyea came alive to end the 2025 season, surpassing Luke Reynolds as the No. 1 guy on the depth chart when Terry Smith took over as interim head coach. He finished the year with 20 receptions for 180 yards and three touchdowns.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Iowa State transfer TE Benjamin Brahmer had sights on Penn State the moment he hit portal
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
After he decided to transfer from Iowa State to Penn State, Benjamin Brahmer departed his hometown of Pierce, Nebraska for an 18-hour drive to State College with his dad, Mark, behind the wheel.

Once he found out Matt Campbell and offensive coordinator Taylor Mouser were headed to Happy Valley, he had no doubt he’d transfer to Penn State. In fact, before he even committed he was looking for apartments to live in. He was also the first transfer on board.

“Once (Mouser) told me he was coming with coach Campbell, I told him ‘yeah, I'm going with,’” Brahmer said. “Other schools did contact me, and I told them ‘thanks, but no thanks.’”

Brahmer cited the long-term relationship he had with both coaches as a big factor in the transfer decision. He said if the tandem left the Cyclones to coach a Division III school, he’d follow and wouldn’t think twice about it.

Trust is huge for Brahmer, something he has for both Mouser, who is also the tight ends coach, and Campbell a lot. He originally committed to Nebraska out of high school, but backed off his pledge when head coach Scott Frost, the current head coach at UCF, got fired.

He found an immediate fit for himself at Iowa State due to Campbell and Mouser in particular.

“Through high school recruiting and getting into college, they did everything that they said they would in recruiting,” Brahmer said. “They're great. They're great people. They care. They care about us players more as men than they do as football players.”

Much like the rest of the other transfers, whether they’re from Iowa State or not, Brahmer knows there’s a need to gel and build relationships. Whether you’re a returner or a player who needs to get acclimated, it’s about all 105 guys uniting as one on Saturdays.

From Brahmer’s perspective, it’s been going well.

“It's been a good, smooth transition so far, keeping it as one team. It's going to take some more time to get there as one culture,” Brahmer said. “It's been a great transition, and I think all the guys that are here from Penn State and other transfers from other schools are all great guys. So we've gotten along, and the more we work together, the better we're going to be together.”

The process of forming chemistry will hopefully lead to success on the field for Penn State. Part of that success will likely depend on Brahmer, who had 75 receptions for 977 yards and nine touchdowns during his time with the Cyclones, which included just eight games in 2024 due to an injury.

He’s coming off a 2025 campaign where he was second-team All-Big 12, and projects to be one of the top pass-catchers in the offense.

“I'd say my size and athletic ability is really good,” Brahmer said. “Obviously, there's a lot of room for improvement for me. I think I can always get better. That's where my main focus is right now, is getting better.”

There’s been a recent run of Penn State tight ends to reach the NFL and get drafted early, which includes Tyler Warren, Theo Johnson and Brenton Strange. Pat Freirmuth and Mike Gesicki are two more who have carved out sustainable careers.

Brahmer, who watched Warren’s record-breaking senior year with a close eye, is still learning the history behind playing tight end at Penn State. Mouser is planning on getting in contact with the lettermen and getting them around the team. Once he does, Brahmer is thrilled at the idea of soaking in their knowledge.

For the time being, Brahmer is focused on building relationships with his teammates and getting better.

“I'm a hard worker off the field and on the field,” Brahmer said. “I'm going to give it my all no matter what.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball faces two unranked nonconference opponents in Tennessee Invitational
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
Fresh off an undefeated weekend at the SpaceU Classic, No. 25 Penn State travels south for a slate of games at the Tennessee Invitational. While the two games against the top-ranked Lady Volunteers highlight the invitational, the Nittany Lions will also face N.C. Central and Appalachian State.

The blue and white are 13-2 and are riding a nine-game winning streak into its first game against N.C. Central at 12:30 p.m. Friday, with its first crack at Tennessee at 5:30 p.m later in the day.

Penn State takes on Appalachian State at 12:30 p.m. Saturday, followed by its second game at 3 p.m. against the Lady Volunteers. The invitational wraps up with the rematch game against the Eagles at 10 a.m.

NC Central

It’s a tale of two teams, as the Nittany Lions are blazing hot and the Eagles enter Friday on a five-game losing streak and a 1-12 record. One of the keys to the blue and white’s winning streak is the red-hot offense, while NC Central’s pitching continues to struggle.

The Eagles hold a bottom three ERA in the MEAC with a 6.76 team ERA, including 91 hits, 56 earned runs and 20 home runs over 58 innings. If the pitching staff can’t contain the Nittany Lions’ explosive offense, NC Central will likely have no chance in either of the two games.

Penn State’s bats have totaled 121 runs, 132 hits and 20 home runs throughout its fast start. There is no weakness in the lineup, so the Eagles will face steady batters throughout the two games.

NC Central’s offense isn’t much better, with a bottom-two batting average in the conference at .215. Additionally, the Eagles have homered just once in the season.

Appalachian State

The Mountaineers are also red-hot on a two-game winning streak and hold an 11-2 record. The key to this game is Penn State’s offense against Appalachian State’s solid pitching staff.

With a 2.61 ERA, the Mountaineers have the second-lowest ERA in the Sun Belt. The squad also holds top spots in the conference in hits, earned runs, walks and home runs.

Sophie Moshos and Camryn Fisher spearhead the pitching staff, so the Nittany Lions will likely face them. Moshos has accumulated a 1.80 ERA and 29 strikeouts, while allowing 11 earned runs, 35 hits and just five walks over 42.2 innings. While Fisher is just as steady with a 2.96 ERA and totaling 44 strikeouts.

While on offense, Appalachian State relies on its power hitting, as it plated 91 runs off 109 hits and 20 home runs. Makayla McClain leads the offense with a .426 average, 20 hits, 18 RBI and seven home runs.

If the Mountaineers can shut down Penn State’s powerful offense, this game may be a close one to watch.

Key Players

There have been many contributors to the blue and white’s winning streak. Freshman Allison Oneacre is a rising star and was awarded two straight Big Ten Freshman of the Week honors. The Alpharetta, Georgia, native leads the team with an explosive .419 average, 18 hits, 27 RBI and seven home runs over 43 at-bats.

While on the mound, freshman Brevyn Kellepouris was essential for the Nittany Lions undefeated weekend at the SpaceU Classic. The Olathe, Kansas, native holds the squad’s lowest ERA at 0.71, while surrendering just 13 hits and two earned runs.

Based on numbers, Penn State’s chances are high to win both games if its dominance continues on both sides of the plate.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Column | Between the dash
author: Annelise Hanson | The Daily Collegian
description:
I spent a few days alone in Florence. I walked for three hours through residential streets miles from my apartment, past shuttered windows and laundry lines swaying in the breeze, with nothing but my AirPods and thoughts, until I eventually stumbled upon a Florentine graveyard.

As a child, I used to beg my grandmother to take me to the cemetery near her house and ask who the names on the gravestone really were. Of course, she didn’t know. But I imagined.

I calculated the years they lived. But how do you measure a life by the dash between two dates?

Sitting on a bench in Florence decades later in this graveyard, the impending feelings of doom and oblivion eventually got the best of me. I realized I will never know the legacies of the people beneath those stones. Their love. Their arguments. The songs they hummed while washing dishes. The slings and arrows of misfortune that accompany a long life.

One day, hopefully many years from now, I will lie beneath a stone too. My name will be carved, my life measured with a dash and the only mark left of me — my signature on the wall of The Lion’s Fountain Pub.

The years will pass and generations will continue, but my life is merely just a fading memory in the minds of my loved ones until they pass on.

Now, oblivion is inevitable, so we mustn’t fear; it’s a misuse of our limited time. Just to be alive is a grand thing. Sometimes you must sit amongst the dead to decide how you really want to live.

Recently, I traveled alone to Cinque Terre. The sea was impossibly blue, homes resembling all of the colors in a stained glass window and vineyards lining the cliffs.

Marveling at the scene, the ocean and the agriculture, I’ve decided I want to spend my time, or dash, falling madly in love with the world. Experiencing new places, having as many once-in-a-lifetime opportunities as life will award me.

I want to spend my time dipping my feet in different seas with the new friends I’ve made across the world. I want to dance at the club until dawn in Barcelona, and watch the ocean with the boy who got me roses afterward. I want to live in a city influenced by Antoni Gaudí. I want to explore the mountains of Andorra and step foot in four countries in a single day just because I can.

I want to eat Florentine steak by myself and learn to find solace in solitude. I want to smile at strangers, befriend small business owners and learn from the guy at the pizza shop whose lifelong dream is to snowboard. I’ll connect with locals in Budapest about their hopes, and dreams and how they exist in a geopolitical context.

To be close to the heart of the world.

I will camp in the Sahara Desert, ride in a hot air balloon, see the wonders of the world, learn languages foreign to my native tongue. Most importantly, I will observe and adapt a way of life where I uncover the secrets of the universe and learn what it truly means to live. To create, to write, to cook, to dance, to sing, to paint, to eat, to drink, to express, to experience, to think, to laugh, to love, to explore and to feel.

I’m in the process of learning how to be ferociously connected with the ground beneath my feet in a throbbing world with rich treasures.

On a train back from the Olympics, I sat near a French girl and an Italian boy speaking English, debating which word carries more weight, adore or love, in the three languages. She insisted “adore” was highest. He said no, “love” meant the most, especially in English. What a beautiful disagreement to have. To argue about which word best captures devotion.

But one large question still remains: how do I carry these lessons back to a society where this way of life doesn’t exist?

I think creatively and deeply here. It’s visceral how I return when I have left parts of myself in different countries, scattered across the continent or do I take every opportunity that leads me back here? I love the thrill of departure, the ache of arrival, the way airports feel like thresholds into alternate versions of myself.

This year, I have four homes: Florence, Pittsburgh, Washington D.C., and State College. My life is not stationary for right now, it’s migratory, and I love it this way.

Maybe the lesson is this: If oblivion is certain, then to expand and to live is rebellion.

You must never allow childlike wonders to resolve. Even as preposterous as it seems, a graveyard may lead you to a new life. When I’m withering in my final years, I wish not to die in another person’s world because I never had the audacity to make my own.

And if I’m only to be remembered as a fading echo, let it be said that I ran toward the world, instead of away from it.

MORE ABROAD COLUMNS

title: ‘Iron sharpens iron’ | Penn State track and field’s elite mid distance group
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Over the past three months, the Penn State middle distance squad has set 13 top-5 marks in school history across six events, finishing one of its greatest regular seasons in school history.

“We’ve just been building towards this over the last five years since I came to Penn State,” assistant coach Ryan Foster said. “Growing the group and expanding the horizons of what we’re trying to do.”

The team found its biggest success in 2018, when senior Isaiah Harris capped off his historic collegiate career with the Nittany Lions’ first of two mid-distance national championships in over 50 years. Now, it’s looking to top that with one of its best seasons ever.

This year, that team has continued to grow and has put itself in a position for multiple potential national titles. Between the men’s and women’s 800-meter groups alone, Penn State has five runners tentatively qualified for the national championship, with three in the top-five.

“They’re all going to want to make the NCAA meet, obviously,” Foster said. “There’s some who have been there to the meet, and now they’re going to want to win it, which they should.”

While several individual runners have a chance to bring gold home to Happy Valley, the men’s team as a whole has a chance to earn their best finish at both the NCAA and Big Ten championships in program history, with a chance to win both.

Given the season-best performances from all teams this season, the men’s squad is projected to finish third at this year’s NCAA championship — much-improved from their all-time best of eighth place — and runner-up at the Big Ten championship.

Much of the blue and white’s success this year has been due to the large depth in the mid distance group.

At the Nittany Lion Challenge in January, four Nittany Lions took the starting line and swept the race, breaking the school record and earning four of the top marks in NCAA history. Graduate student Allon Clay led the way with a Japanese national record 2:18.09, followed by Handal Roban, Yukichi Ishii and Nick Sloff who all ran sub-2:20.

“Each one of the guys wants to be the best they can be,” head coach John Gondak said. “They know they have to race each other, and they know they have to push each other in training, so the more talent that’s in the group, the more they’re able to push each other.”

In addition to the men’s success, the women’s mid distance group has seen Hayley Kitching take the school record in three events and freshman Boh Ritchie the No. 2 time in two events.

While the overall group has competed against each other in the 600-meter, 800m and 1,000-meter this year, they also train against each other and push one another in practice every day.

“As a coach, I always keep culture at the forefront of the group,” Foster said. “We have to find a balance between being teammates and being competitive.”

For the men’s team in particular, that group has become much more than a team, but has pushed to make each and every one of them better.

“Ever since we’ve gotten closer, it’s not even like a squad anymore, it’s more like a brotherhood for us,” Roban said. “So we look at each other as brothers, and we hold each other accountable. I think that’s made each other better, as iron sharpens iron.”

In addition to the 1000m performance earlier this season, both squads have also excelled in the 600m and the 800m.

At the Penn State National Open, Penn State earned three of the top four spots in the men’s 800m, as Roban set the school record in 1:44.91, and Clay and Ishii set the No. 2 and No. 5 marks in school history. Just two weeks later, Roban broke his own record again at the ASICS Sound Invite, running 1:44.73 for the second-best mark in the NCAA this season.

Roban also set the school record and Big Ten lead in the 600m as the only sub-1:15 runner in the Big Ten. He’s accompanied by Niko Schultz, Kaleb Nastari and Austin Gallant, who each are in the top-10 times in the Big Ten this year.

But despite the repeated success throughout the entire season, the Nittany Lions have taken each opportunity to race as a fun experience, not holding themselves to a hard standard.

“We don’t pressure ourselves because of the expectation,” Roban said. “We know there’s a lot of expectation on us, so we just enjoy the moment.”

With the indoor Big Ten Tournament on the horizon, the Nittany Lions have an opportunity to earn individual and team accolades, with the mid distance groups leading the charge for both the men’s and women’s teams.

“We have a program in the Big Ten that does almost everything and does everything well,” Foster said. “We know that we’re kind of the engine room for taking a run at something like a championship title.”

This year’s Penn State success has been made possible by the years of growth and improvement by the blue and white, all culminating with the postseason championships over the next few weeks.

“Each year, the plan is always to be 1% better and our 1% sure is quite a lot,” Foster said. “They’ve been able to do that, so I’m excited.”

MORE TRACK & FIELD COVERAGE

title: No. 16 Penn State men’s volleyball hosts Merrimack for single-match home stand
author: Chase Mitchell | The Daily Collegian
description:
Now sitting at 10-4 on the season, Penn State is ranked 16th in the country and is in the middle of winning six consecutive matches.

The Nittany Lions have an eight-day break coming up before they visit No. 2 Long Beach State, but must first get through Merrimack when it visits on Friday. Action is set to get underway at 7 p.m.

The Warriors sit at 4-8 on the season with notable losses to Princeton, Lincoln Memorial and BYU. Merrimack is coming off a 3-2 victory over LIU that split the series.

Meanwhile, the blue and white arrive off three consecutive series sweeps over Harvard, Princeton and Saint Francis.

Head coach Hayden West-Mather is in his first year with Merrimack after leading Nichols to an NCAA tournament appearance last season. The Bison’s victories over Randolph-Macon and Stevens cemented West-Mather as the youngest head coach in history to win an NCAA tournament game.

On the other end of the court, Mark Pavlik, who will retire following the conclusion of this season, now has a career coaching record of 704-245 – the fourth-most wins across all divisions in the history of NCAA men’s volleyball.

Sean Harvey is coming off a 19 kill series against the Crimson and is greatly contributing to Penn State being first overall in the EIVA in kills, individually ranking third in the conference in the same category and third in total points.

Tristan Hassell also had a monster series when Harvard was in town, netting 21 kills over the two matches. As just a freshman, Hassell has the eighth-most points in the EIVA.

The spotlight won’t just be on Harvey and Hassell, though.

Penn State middle blocker Owen Rose was recently named EIVA Defensive Player of the Week and is third in the NCAA in blocks per set, averaging 1.367. The 6-foot-7 Rose is contributing to the Nittany Lions' ranking second in the country in blocks per set, behind just Tusculum.

Libero Ryan Merk leads the EIVA in digs, averaging 2.33 per set. With 114, Merk sits with the sixth-most total digs in the NCAA.

The Nittany Lions have the best opponent hitting percentage in the EIVA, allowing opponents to hit .204, good for 11th in the NCAA.

As a team, the warriors hit .138 and likely won’t see a huge increase in that number against the stout blue and white defense.

Merrimack’s kill leader, Dallas Birkenbeuel, has 148 on the season and averages 3.52 per set. The sophomore is coming off a season-high 24 points against LIU.

Birkenbeuel sits second in digs behind libero Kyle Standage with 109. The senior notched 19 digs against LIU, a season-high. The two could prove to be tricky for the Nittany Lions, but likely not enough to completely disrupt them.

With the Warriors’ struggles this season, combined with their inconsistency and inability to piece wins together, it seems like Penn State has an easy win on its hands.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: No. 5 Penn State men’s hockey prepares for road series against Notre Dame
author: Greg Finberg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a four-game winless streak, No. 5 Penn State returned to the win column with a sweep of Ohio State.

It took an eight-point outing from Gavin McKenna in Game 1 and a three-goal comeback to take Game 2, but the Nittany Lions handled business at Pegula Ice Arena to maintain the No. 3 spot in the Big Ten standings.

Their upcoming series features another matchup against a struggling team, this one against a Notre Dame squad that ranks dead last in the conference standings — 13 points behind the next closest team. But as Penn State witnessed in Saturday’s gritty overtime win, no Big Ten team can be taken lightly.

“It's a down year for them in the Big Ten, (but) it’s not a down year for them as a team,” Guy Gadowsky said. “They are very good, and I don't think we have to remind (the team) of that. They know that it's Notre Dame. They've got great players. They played the best team, Michigan State, extremely tough on Friday. It’s a tough league.”

Scouting Notre Dame

Penn State outscored Notre Dame 10-4 and took all six points in the teams’ mid-January series. Six weeks later, the sides will meet again, this time at Compton Family Ice Arena in South Bend.

Evan Werner, who scored Notre Dame’s lone goal in their Jan. 16 loss to Penn State, leads his team with 15 goals and 28 points. Sutter Muzzatti’s 16 assists sit second for the Fighting Irish, who enter Friday’s contest with a Big Ten-worst 6-22-4 record.

“(Notre Dame) is a good team. They're a hard, physical, fast, skilled team,” Lev Katzin said. “From what I remember that weekend, our power play was hot … if we play our game, I don't see anyone else beating us. So we just got to play our game.”

Notre Dame’s struggling offense, which scores a conference-low 2.69 goals per game, will face Penn State goaltender Josh Fleming, whose 93.2% save percentage is tied for the Big Ten lead.

The same can’t be said for Nicholas Kempf, the Fighting Irish’s starting netminder. Among Big Ten goaltenders with 20 or more appearances, Kempf ranks last in goals-against average.

Notre Dame’s conference-worst 132 goals allowed is 21 more than the next closest team. And their upcoming opponent, a Nittany Lions squad that posted 16 goals in its recent sweep of the Buckeyes, won’t make things easier.

Injuries

Penn State is depleted at forward.

The Nittany Lions are already without Dane Dowiak and Keaton Peaters for the rest of the season and Alex Servagno and Charlie Cerrato through the regular season. Gadowsky said Braeden Ford could return “this weekend or next,” but if the forward misses this weekend’s games, Penn State will be without five forwards.

Add to that list defenseman Nick Fascia, who had been playing forward before he suffered a “bad cut” in Friday’s win.

Gadowsky ruled Fascia out for both games against Notre Dame and called him “day-to-day” moving forward.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Preview | No. 15 Penn State men’s lacrosse eyes redemption in Military Appreciation game
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State fell to the No. 15 spot in the USILA Coaches Poll after suffering a 12-11 defeat to Navy in Annapolis, Maryland.

The Nittany Lions aim to bounce back when they make their 2026 Panzer Stadium debut, kicking off a two-game homestand against Yale at 1 p.m. on Saturday.

Tale of the tape

Penn State currently posts a 2-2 record, beating Colgate and then-No. 3 Princeton with its other loss to unranked Villanova. Meanwhile, Yale possesses a 2-1 record, 1-1 against opponents ranked at the time of the games.

On March 1, 2025, the Nittany Lions defeated the Bulldogs 15-9 in New Haven, Connecticut, behind a blazing 11-goal first half. Both Kyle Lehman and Hunter Aquino had breakout performances along with five other Penn State goal-scorers.

Aquino scored two goals and added five assists, while Lehman scored five goals, three of which came in the first quarter. Coach Jeff Tambroni retained each attackman, and recent head-to-head history is on the Nittany Lions’ side.

Upsets, upsets, upsets

The 2026 season began differently than last year for the Nittany Lions.

After opening the schedule with an offensive war against Colgate that ended 18-14, in favor of the Nittany Lions, Tambroni’s squad fell in overtime to Villanova at Holuba Hall in Week 2.

Unranked programs continued to haunt Penn State, as Navy battled to a one-goal regulation victory at Navy-Marine Corps Memorial Stadium. Both Navy and Villanova earned spots on the rankings after defeating the Nittany Lions but only the No. 20-ranked Midshipmen remained, per the Inside Lacrosse KANE Division I Media Poll, as the Wildcats dropped games to Saint Joseph’s in Week 2 and Rutgers in Week 3.

Between the pair of losses, Penn State surged to a 13-7 victory over then-No. 3 Princeton on the road in the Tigers’ season-opener.

Meanwhile, the Bulldogs are on a two-game winning streak, including a 16-11 victory over then-No. 18 Boston University and a 15-9 win over Marist. Yale’s only loss was in its season-opener against then-No. 11 Army in West Point, New York, when the Black Knights defeated the Bulldogs 12-6.

In the most recent USILA Poll, Yale received 65 votes as the highest-ranked team not currently among the top-20. Navy sits right behind the Bulldogs, earning 45 votes.

Young guns

Penn State’s and Yale’s offensive units feature star-studded youths.

Aquino — the reigning Big Ten Freshman of the Year — paces the Nittany Lions with 15 points, spread between 11 goals and four assists. Lehman and freshman Jack Iannantuono round out the primary attacking core, as Lehman posts 13 points and Iannantuono tallied 10 so far this year.

Sophomore Chase Robertson and redshirt freshman Andrew Beard each have contributed alongside redshirt senior Luke Walstrum on the first-line midfield. Robertson scored five goals through the first four games, while Beard currently has two goals and two assists. Walstrum’s nine points lead Penn State’s midfield production.

Goaltender Preston Hawkins improved greatly since being pulled in his debut inside Holuba Hall, currently showing a .439 save percentage in his freshman campaign. The Powell, Ohio, native stole the show against the Tigers, posting a .611 save percentage as the biggest defensive weapon.

Connor Gately is Yale’s leading point scorer with 13. He was a key contributor in the Bulldogs’ victory over the Terriers, recording four goals and three assists.

Freshman Sean Grogan tallied 10 points through three games, with a four-goal performance against Marist and a two-goal, two-assist game to aid Gately against Boston U.

Between the pipes, sophomore Ben Friedman started in both of Yale’s victories, earning a 2-0 record with a .583 save percentage and 10.47 goals-against average. Against the Terriers, Friedman made 21 saves on 32 shots to keep the Bulldogs ahead on the scoreboard.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: No. 3 Penn State women’s hockey hosts Syracuse for AHA semifinal
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 3 Penn State begins its postseason journey with a best-of-three AHA semifinal series against Syracuse. The Nittany Lions swept the Orange in both series this season and tied the most goals scored in a game in program history against their conference rival.

However, Syracuse is familiar with upsetting Penn State in the playoffs. The last time both sides saw each other in the postseason was in 2021. The blue and white ultimately lost 3-2 and were upset in the first round of the CHA playoffs. With a double overtime win in the first round this year, the Orange enter Happy Valley with momentum.

The teams will begin the series at 6 p.m. Friday, with Game 2 scheduled for 3 p.m. Saturday. If necessary, Game 3 is slated for 3 p.m. Sunday for a chance to advance to the AHA Championship Game.

The Orange’s path

Syracuse took an entirely different route to the semifinals than Penn State. The Orange finished fifth in the AHA and had to play third-place RIT to advance. Emma Gnade secured a spot in the semifinals for her team after she scored the game-winner just over three minutes into the second overtime period. The Fenton, Missouri, native leads the Orange in all offensive categories and enters this game with 20 total points.

Additionally, Syracuse netminder Ava Drabyk continues her starting role after winning her first collegiate hockey playoff game. The freshman currently sits at third in goals against average at 2.31 and a .924 save percentage in the conference. Drabyk played a vital role in the double overtime win after playing over 80 minutes and saving 28 shots.

Due to their performances in the quarterfinals, three players earned AHA Player of the Week honors for the Orange. Most notably, Peyton Armstrong was named forward of the week after being a part of all four goals for Syracuse. The Bancroft, Ontario, native had a four-point performance, recording two goals and two assists.

Returning Olympians

Although Penn State hasn't seen much action in over a week, a few of its players have. The three current Nittany Lions wrapped up the Olympic Games on Feb. 19 after Tessa Janecke won gold with Team USA. Nicole Hall finished the games in fourth place with Team Sweden and Matilde Fantin finished as the top scorer for Team Italy with three goals.

Janecke returns to Happy Valley, currently leading Penn State in points with 40. Additionally, the Orangeville, Illinois, native is only one point shy of the AHA point leader, Jessica MacKinnon.

Meanwhile, Fantin and Hall are no strangers to finding themselves on the scoresheet against the Orange. The forwards combined for eight total points throughout the four games in the regular season.

The Olympians returned to the states in time for the semifinal series against Syracuse. While the Nittany Lions haven’t shown many signs of weakness with the loss of these three elite forwards, the returning additions can make for a dominant offense that was on display throughout the season.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Off the record | Ex marks the spot
author: Miss Behave I The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor’s Note: Off the record is a weekly column written by a third-year Collegian staffer writing under an alias. It aims to promote a space for honest conversations about love, sex and relationships. Those interested in sharing their experiences or submitting a topic are invited to do so through this anonymous form.

Dearest reader,

Breakups, whether they’re with a romantic partner or best friend, can hurt just the same.

Breakups can be a lot like misreading a treasure map. You were so sure you found gold. You followed every clue, trusted every sign and then, all the sudden, you realized what you unearthed wasn’t treasure at all — it was heartbreak. Endless tears and hours and hours of mind spiraling thoughts you can’t control.

And just like a treasure map, every breakup leaves an “X,” on the landscape of your love life. It marks the spot where love once was. Where something beautiful once existed.

But before you can move on from that spot, you learn to dig a little deeper and uncover what’s beneath the surface.

The real treasure lies in what you uncover about yourself in the healing process: your strength, your resilience and the part of you that only emerges when you’re faced with stitching up a broken heart.

Breakups are a self-healing journey disguised as chaos. When someone leaves you, the initial instinct is to fill the void with distraction, or worse, longing for what’s gone and what you can’t get back.

But the magic happens when you take a minute to pause and actually let yourself feel all of the emotions. The confusion, sadness or anger. Those feelings are there for a reason and you’re entitled to them.

Don’t ignore them or push them away. Accept them. Recognize them. That’s the first step.

And yes, it hurts. Sometimes for days, weeks, months or years. But in that pain, you’re growing. You’re discovering layers of yourself that can only surface when love falls away.

Every tear shed is a reminder that you feel deeply and every sleepless night is a testament to your courage to keep moving forward despite the pain.

The second step is to reflect. Take the time to understand what the relationship has taught you. Not just about love, but also about yourself. What you need. What you deserve. What you’re no longer willing to accept.

Journal. Talk to your best friend. Sit alone with your thoughts. Let yourself process.

Understand why the relationship ended and approach that with kindness, honesty and compassion for both of you. We’re all human. Sometimes we make mistakes. Sometimes we choose to love without having the capacity in our lives to sustain it.

And honesty, no matter how hard it may be, it’s always worth it. The third step: release and rediscover.

This is where you get to choose yourself again. Reclaim your interests and your routines. Revisit old passions, or explore new ones. This step isn’t about becoming someone new overnight. It's about remembering who you’ve always been, while allowing yourself to evolve.

You’re allowed to change. You’re allowed to want different things now. Heartbreak has a way of redefining your standards and sharpening your sense of self.

Rediscovery also means learning to enjoy your own company again. Finding comfort in solitude instead of fearing it. And slowly, your confidence will return and you will begin to trust yourself again.

This is the stage where you stop searching the map for what you lost. You start drawing new paths forward.

So the next time a breakup hits, don’t rush to “get over it.” Instead, honor the journey. Explore the map of your own heart and treasure the discoveries you make along the way.

Because the greatest treasure of all isn’t the love that’s left, it’s the love you will find once the storm clears and you refuse to chase what already proved it couldn’t stay.

Yours truly,

Miss Behave

MORE COLUMNS

title: Blog | Outfit guide to State Patty’s
author: Talia Rittenhouse | The Daily Collegian
description:
The beloved Penn State tradition of State Patty’s is making its annual return this weekend.

For the holiday, Penn State students will be dressed in green, like walking four-leaf clovers, downtown ready for the day-long event celebrating St. Patrick’s day.

You might ask, why celebrate now instead of March 17? The official holiday is mid-week, right after students’ spring break. But traditionally, State Patty’s is squeezed in before the chaos of the post-spring break semester. Being the weekend after THON, students are eager to celebrate after all of their hard work and long dry periods.

To celebrate the occasion, follow these guidelines in order to be dressed like a State Patty’s all-star.

GREEN!

Green, green, lots of green. If you don’t wear green this holiday, expect to get pinched by anyone you interact with.

To minimize this annoyance and embrace the weekend's theme, be sure to include any amount of green in your outfit.

This can be worn through a pair of pants, a T-shirt, a tank top or a jacket over the rest of your outfit.

Sometimes people buy clothes that say State Patty’s on the front of their shirts, so this is another option that can help you dress for the occasion this Saturday.

State Patty’s merch

Speaking of State Patty’s shirts, you can buy some merch for this Penn State holiday downtown at many different stores.

McLanahan’s will most definitely have State Patty’s shirts or sweatshirts, just like they’re always stocked with last minute game-day merch. Some other boutiques may be selling green attire, too, if you want something that doesn’t scream State Patty’s.

Rumor has it, Bill Pickle’s Tap Room was handing out themed shirts for this upcoming weekend, so if you are at least 21-years-old and are lucky enough to have gotten one, you’re fully set for State Patty’s.

Who knows, maybe you can even find a State Patty’s sweat set to wear. It would be comfy, cute and perfect for the day.

Gold jewelry

Be the pot of gold for your friends this holiday!

I know this outfit idea could be hard to execute if you’re loyal to silver jewelry all year long, but this weekend is the perfect time to break out the gold hoops and rings in order to have the best outfit.

However, silver jewelry just won’t do the trick. This weekend is the time for the gold jewelry girls to thrive and show off every piece they own.

You could even wear a gold top to completely embody a pot of gold. And if it were warmer, you could’ve worn a gold skirt.

Shamrocks

You can’t have State Patty’s without a shamrock.

Some shirts may have white or green four-leaf clovers on them making you fully prepared for the holiday.

Having one on your clothes will bring you lots of luck, and maybe even make your day more special than it started — you never know what luck a clover will give you.

If you are unable to find an outfit that has one, you can always drink a Shamrock Shake to bring the vibe with you throughout the day.

Leprechaun onesie

Be the talk of the town, carry through with the bit and feel comfortable all day long — dress as a leprechaun this weekend.

I’m sure you can order one from Amazon or another bulk-clothing website, so you can be the life of the party.

Think about the memories this outfit will bring, and the pictures you’ll get out of it. Wearing this outfit will automatically make your day even more fun, as you skip from place to place with your friends dressed as a leprechaun.

You may even find a rainbow and a pot of gold if you dress this way, so as a broke-college student, this may be the desperate move you have to make.

MORE BLOGS

title: Letter to the Editor: Students oppose solidarity town hall for marginalized students at UPUA meeting
author: Letter by Students for Justice in Palestine at Penn State
description:
This letter was written by Students for Justice in Palestine, an organization at Penn State dedicated to fighting for the liberation of Palestine and the freedom and self-determination of the Palestinian people. Anyone interested in submitting a letter to the editor may do so here.

On Feb. 11, the University Park Undergraduate Association (UPUA) met for what was supposed to be the student government’s standard Wednesday night general assembly meeting. What followed was a staggering display of individual and institutional anti-Palestinian racism that derailed the session. The star of the night was a bill to allocate funds for food at the Solidarity Rising Town Hall organized by Penn State Students for Justice in Palestine (SJP) in coordination with other member organizations of the Paul Robeson Cultural Center (PRCC) Liaison Board. This town hall was conceived as an open space for marginalized students across campus to talk about their concerns and build across various communities as a first step toward collective action. The programming for the event had gone through the standard approval procedures and was in line with the mission to promote diversity and equity on campus.

During the open comment section preceding the start of the general assembly, several Zionist students used their time to read off pre-written comments that slandered SJP and our members, accusing the town hall of being partisan and a threat to Jewish safety on campus. As Palestinians and those in solidarity with Palestinians, we immediately recognized this as yet another blatant display of anti-Palestinian racism that has time and time again gone unacknowledged on this campus.

In the fall of 2024, one year into the ongoing Israeli genocide in Gaza, SJP organized a memorial on the HUB Lawn to honor the tens of thousands of Palestinian lives lost and to educate students about the reality of Israel’s genocidal campaign. At the time, the vast majority of international humanitarian organizations had declared that a genocide was occurring in Gaza, and the International Court of Justice had already released a preliminary injunction against Israel. Despite this international consensus, Zionist students proceeded to desecrate the memorial by removing flags representing murdered Palestinians from the ground, riding bikes through the lawn, and using a variety of racist, homophobic and fatphobic language against our members. These hateful acts were documented by students through video recording and garnered widespread national attention. However, Penn State failed to acknowledge or condemn the pervasive anti-Palestinian racism on campus, which had enabled this incident to occur.

What happened at UPUA, therefore, was not new to us, but an expected outcome of a culture and an institution that does not value Palestinian life. At least one of the students who made slanderous remarks against our organization at the general assembly also participated in the desecration of our memorial, drawing a direct link between the two incidents and displaying the inherent connections between Zionism and bigotry. When people claim that the existence and presence of Palestinians and their struggle is inherently antisemitic, it engages in not only the racist caricature of an entire group but also the kind of rhetoric that has manufactured consent for Israel’s current genocide in Gaza. Additionally, when members of UPUA accept this framing and deem Palestinian existence as fundamentally partisan, it sets a dangerous precedent that can ultimately be weaponized against any other marginalized group on campus. Continuing to allow blatant, institutionalized anti-Palestinian racism to prevail is a threat not only to Palestinians and their allies but to all marginalized students.

What occurred at UPUA did not go unopposed, as nearly 40 students streamed into the general assembly in solidarity with SJP and the town hall — showing that the vast majority of students on campus are opposed to such rhetoric being weaponized against Palestinians and that UPUA’s ultimate decision did not reflect the values and desires of the student body they claim to represent.

Despite the bill not passing, SJP and the endorsing organizations proceeded to host the Solidarity Town Hall and brought together our various communities in meaningful dialogue, resulting in a standing-room-only event inside the PRCC library this past Tuesday. The success of the event is a strong rejection of an institutional culture that benefits from seeing us isolated and provides a foundation for our communities to continue to build relationships that will allow us all to achieve real progress on this campus.

MORE LETTERS TO THE EDITOR

title: Penn State women’s basketball honors 2 players on Senior Night in big win over USC
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
In addition to Penn State’s victory over USC, it celebrated its seniors during the last home game of the season.

Despite only playing for the Lady Lions for a combined three years, Amiya Evans and Vitoria Santana made their mark on Penn State, and it was evident in the game against USC.

Evans was named to the starting lineup for the ninth time this season, tallying 16 minutes in the game. Santana came off the bench but still picked up 20 minutes in the win against the Trojans.

“They’re just phenomenal humans,” coach Carolyn Kieger said. “We wanted it real bad for the seniors, and I was so proud for them to go out and make huge plays and contribute to the win.”

Trailing early, Evans took the ball at the top of the key and dribbled her way through a pair of defenders, drawing the foul and cashing in on both free throws for her first points of the day.

While she didn’t have any other scoring opportunities the rest of the day, Evans contributed to the blue and white as a vocal leader, helping rally the team during its scoring droughts and keeping them in the game.

“(She has) phenomenal energy, great presence and is just super bubbly,” Kieger said during the weekly media session on Tuesday. “She’s super great with the team in terms of holding them accountable, but knows when to keep it positive. She knows how to rally them.”

Both Santana and Evans were part of the Lady Lions’ defensive substitutions, both taking the court in the defensive rotation less than three minutes into the game.

For Santana, that specialty has been on display for her entire career, who came to Happy Valley as the 2023-24 ACCAC Defensive Player of the Year. While her defensive success has remained dominant over the years, she’s grown in many ways since coming to Penn State.

“This was her first time that she got immersed into full English speaking, and she took it by grace and a lot of humility,” Kieger said. “I don’t think I’ve ever seen anybody make that jump in terms of just understanding culture, understanding language and just buying in as quickly as she did — what a great leader.”

Against the Spartans, Santana had one of the biggest nights of her career as she sealed the victory with a free throw with four seconds left, a moment Kieger hopes “she’ll remember for a while.”

“It’s really easy to point to someone right here to show what college is all about, and that's Vitoria,” Kieger said. “She came here to develop, she came to the (United) States to get better in all areas, and she completely did.”

Evans had just as big of a game, getting the physicality started early and often with three rebounds on the day and a pair of free throws.

Amidst the team’s biggest win of the season and a crucial contest that kept them alive in the Big Ten postseason race, the Lady Lions continued to make it all about the seniors in their final game at home.

“It's gonna be really fun to follow both of them because these two women are high achievers, and they're going to be really successful in life,” Kieger said. “So just really proud of both, they're great humans, great teammates and great representatives of Penn State.”

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Young CB Jahmir Joseph ‘shooting for the stars’ in return to Penn State
author: Avery Hill | Football Editor
description:
With plenty of uncertainty following the firing of James Franklin, cornerback Jahmir Joseph was left with a decision: should he stay or should he go?

“I didn't know if Terry (Smith) was going to come back, and I didn't know what I was going to do. So obviously there was moments where I was thinking to leave,” Joseph said.

Smith was in the running for the head coaching job, so as the two spoke, the cornerback's coach retention became a factor in Joseph’s decision. But even as Matt Campbell landed the head-coaching job, Joseph jumped at the chance to play for his position coach and former interim head coach, who returns to his role as associate head coach and cornerbacks coach.

“Thank God we’re back,” Joseph said in reference to he and Smith.

Joseph played in five games last season, maintaining his redshirt, but his pick-6 against Villanova put him on the radar to become a must-keep for the new era in Happy Valley.

After that moment, he didn’t see significant playing time again until the Pinstripe Bowl, but his interception was a point of growth for the young cornerback.

“It made me more comfortable in my game and more at peace,” Joseph said. “At fall camp, everything was kind of coming at me fast, and coming out of high school, I'm thinking it would be the same.”

Joseph is one of two intriguing cornerbacks set to return for their second year in Happy Valley, the other being Daryus Dixson.

Dixson and Joseph came into Penn State as opposites, with Joseph hailing from Nyack, New York, while Dixson is a native of Perris, California. On the same accord, Dixson was an immediate contributor, largely noted for his maturity as a freshman — these contrasts brought them together.

“When we combine it, it’s crazy,” Joseph said, noting their differences. “So that's my guy. I’m glad I met him. Him staying, it helped me be a better person because we push each other to the limit.”

The duo still didn’t speak much when they came to Happy Valley, but they were able to grow close eventually. Naturally, when it came time for them to make their decisions, they talked through it, announcing their returns two days apart.

“We’re tryna obviously get all the accolades we can,” Joseph said. “We’re young and shooting for the stars.”

Even at a position that typically boasts 2-3 players on the field at once, the thought of running it back together was intriguing to them.

It’s a point that especially stands out with the return of starting cornerbacks Zion Tracy and Audavion Collins, decisions that didn’t bother Joseph. In fact, he said having the two veterans back was a big factor in his choice to return.

So, now that Joseph remains, he has a chance to mesh not just with the veterans but also with Dixson, as the room looks forward to working together to become a successful unit in the new era of Penn State football.

“None of us are jealous of anybody. We all push for the same goal,” Joseph said of the cornerback room. “We all push each other to be better … we're all humble and good human beings.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s basketball upsets USC on senior night, keeping postseason hopes alive
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State returned to Rec Hall on Wednesday night for its final home game of the season. The Lady Lions hosted USC with a chance to creep closer to a Big Ten Tournament bid.

Penn State exploded late to upset the Trojans 85-80, increasing its postseason odds.

“So happy for this team, obviously, we've been getting better this last month, every day, game, practice,” coach Carolyn Kieger said. “Just so fun to see them with smiles on their face and playing together, playing composed.”

It was senior night in Happy Valley, and Penn State started by honoring forward Amiya Evans and guard Vitória Santana. This was Evans’ lone season as a Lady Lion after spending her first three at Georgia. Santana has spent the last two years with Penn State after transferring from Eastern Arizona of the NJCAA.

After a possession each in the first 15 seconds, both sides came up empty in their first attempt. Then, it was raining three-pointers from there.

USC’s Jazzy Davidson and Londynn Jones nailed back-to-back three-pointers to put the Trojans up 6-0. Kiyomi McMiller answered with her own make from beyond the arc to cut the game back to one possession.

The first quarter scoring continued to go back-and-forth as USC held an 11-7 lead halfway through.

Kennedy Smith scored 11 straight points for the Trojans to give USC its largest lead of the game at seven points with just over a minute left in the opening frame.

A deep three from guard Moriah Murray cut the lead to four, and an offensive foul committed by Vivian Iwuchukwu gave Penn State the ball back with six seconds to play. The ball stayed in the hands of McMiller, who beat the buzzer and made it a 26-24 game at the end of the quarter.

Both sides were hot from shooting in the opening frame as the Trojans went 5-of-9 from deep and the Lady Lions shot 4-of-6 from beyond the arc.

USC wasted no time in the second quarter, going on a 7-0 run to start the frame. The blue and white responded with five unanswered points of its own, putting the Trojans' lead at 33-29 with just under six minutes left.

The cardinal and gold remained unfazed without letting Penn State find too much momentum. USC took its first double-digit lead of the game with under five minutes to play in the half, behind another 7-0 run.

McMiller broke the Lady Lions’ scoring drought with one point from the foul line, but following a turnover after the second shot, the blue and white stayed at nearly three minutes without a field-goal.

Penn State redeemed itself on the defensive end after a steal from Santana turned into three more points for McMiller.

Following four straight USC turnovers, the Lady Lions exploded for an 8-0 run, cutting their deficit from 10 to three with under two minutes to play.

“We just got to keep our mind going and keep our momentum, that's our big word.” McMiller said “Keep it going into the next game and just keep it going.”

The Trojans ended the blue and white’s mojo with a three-pointer from guard Kara Dunn. Penn State got two points back, but three points on the charity stripe from Davidson gave USC a 46-39 lead heading into halftime.

McMiller led all scorers with 22 points at the break, as Davidson and Smith each netted 15 for USC.

After Penn State outscored the Trojans 5-3 in the first minute of the third quarter, the scoring went silent on both sides for nearly three minutes. McMiller nailed her sixth three-pointer of the night, the Lady Lions’ second basket of the third. This also put the sophomore at the 1,000-point mark of her career.

Davidson came up big for USC, recording a turnover, assist and layup all in the span of 30 seconds, to give the Trojans a 53-44 lead with just about five and a half minutes left in the frame.

USC rode a 7-0 to the 3:56 mark in the third quarter, taking a 12-point lead for its largest of the game, until it was broken by a corner three from guard Tèa Clèante.

Both teams continued to trade baskets in the final minutes of the quarter, but the blue and white ended the frame on a 9-0 run, putting the score at 59-58 heading in the final frame and making the contest the closest it's been all night.

After Smith started the frame with a 1-of-2 effort from the foul line, center Gracie Merkle got two points for Penn State on the other end, tying the game for the first time since it was 0-0.

Jones hit a three to put the Trojans back up and for the next few minutes the Lady Lions kept creeping with one point, but USC didn’t let itself get down.

At least that was the case for about four minutes until Merkle scored another bucket to give the blue and white its first lead of the game at 68-67.

At the end of the day, we know that every game is a you're playing towards the postseason right now,” Merkle said. “So this win, we know we're going to celebrate tonight, but tomorrow, we have practice.”

Jones hit another three to put the Trojans up again, but was once again answered by a layup from Merkle to tie things up with just over four minutes to play.

After continuous lead changes for the next couple of minutes, a three-pointer from Murray gave the Lady Lions a two-possession lead with two and a half minutes to play.

Following a 1-of-2 effort from Dunn at the line, McMiller drove down the middle to give Penn State a 79-74 lead as the clock ticked under two minutes.

The Lady Lions held this lead till there was under a minute left of play, forcing the Trojans to start fouling.

Clèante was the first to make an appearance at the line in crunch time, putting Penn State up six with 40 seconds left.

Dunn hit a three-pointer as USC took just five seconds off the clock, but still opted to foul the Lady Lions on defense. Clèante was the victim once again, going 1-of-2. A lane violation from the Trojans gave the freshman another shot, but she couldn’t come up with it.

Following a missed three-pointer from Davidson, Penn State regained possession with 22 seconds left.

Merkle went 1-of-2 from the line, but USC responded with another three to cut the lead to 82-80. After the inbound, McMiller got the ball in her hands, making both attempts from the stripe and giving the guard the first 40-point game of her career.
