title: What are the top storylines for former Penn State players heading into NFL Scouting Combine?
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
It’s that time of year again for former Penn State players, as the 2026 NFL Scouting Combine is underway this week for ex-Nittany Lions.

Penn State will have nine expected representatives showcasing their skills in Indianapolis at Lucas Oil Stadium, home of the Indianapolis Colts, from Feb. 26-March 2.

Here are the top storylines amongst the contingent heading into the event.

Health of Drew Allar, Nick Singleton

Of the nine entrants, Drew Allar and Nick Singleton are heading into the event coming off injuries.

Allar broke his left ankle on Oct. 11 against Northwestern. The quarterback was seen in a boot throughout the regular season with a motorized scooter, but didn’t have the scooter at the regular-season finale against Rutgers.

Five days before facing the Scarlet Knights, Allar told ESPN’s Pete Thamel he was ahead of schedule with his rehab. He’s the third-best quarterback in the draft, per ESPN’s Mel Kiper.

“I’m going to be a better version of myself out of this,” Allar told Thamel. “The doctors said it’s going to be stronger than before because of the type of surgery they did. It won’t prohibit me from anything in the future or need to get it redone. A lot of guys have done it and come back.”

Singleton injured the fifth metatarsal bone in his right foot at Senior Bowl practice in Mobile, Alabama, and underwent surgery the following week, his father, Timmy, told Rich Scarcella of the Reading Eagle.

At the time of injury, the running back reportedly planned to not participate in on-field drills at the combine.

If that remains the case, Singleton will likely just be doing media and meeting with teams privately in Indianapolis. He could go anywhere from Day 2 to Day 3, but if he’s unable to participate in drills, which includes his Penn State Pro Day on March 18 at Holuba Hall, he likely could trend toward being a Day 3 pick.

Can Vega Ioane’s stock rise?

Entering the season, names like Allar, defensive end Dani Dennis-Sutton and ex-cornerback A.J. Harris, who has since transferred to Indiana, were the Nittany Lions drawing first-round draft grades.

Entering the combine, left guard Vega Ioane is the lone former Penn State player projected to be selected in Round 1, with some having Ioane go as high as the top 15.

Ioane was a two-year starter at left guard for the Nittany Lions, making the Associated Press All-America second team as well as the All-Big Ten first team from the conference’s coaches. In addition to his play on the field, the offensive lineman went viral for helping out an equipment staffer postgame after losing to Ohio State.

There’s a good chance the 6-foot-4, 336-pounder won’t participate in drill work because of his firm stock, but if he does and puts together a strong showing, he could hear his name called earlier than later in Round 1.

Who could climb draft boards?

Dennis-Sutton and safety Zakee Wheatley could climb draft boards with strong performances.

Both are currently averaging Round 3 projections amongst mock drafts.

Dennis-Sutton certainly helped his stock later in the season, playing in the Pinstripe Bowl instead of opting out like the majority of his draft-eligible teammates. He finished the game with four total tackles and two sacks, and was praised by the team for suiting up one last time in the blue and white.

He had highs and lows in his senior season, finishing with 42 total tackles, 8.5 sacks and three forced fumbles. Dennis-Sutton also adds value with his play on special teams, blocking a trio of punts in 2025. If he tests well, he could flirt with second-round territory.

Wheatley wrapped up his time in Happy Valley with 223 total tackles, six interceptions, six pass breakups and three fumble recoveries.

Wheatley was the leader in the safety room this past year, and still made his mark the two seasons prior with Kevin Winston Jr. and Jaylen Reed in the room, both of whom flashed this past year in their rookie seasons.

Based on the pedigree from the Penn State safety room, his past production, and a potential for strong numbers at the combine and quality interviews, Wheatley could truly see a sizable jump in his projections as well.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s basketball looks for win over USC to increase postseason odds
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is the closest it's been to the Big Ten tournament all year, and it has another opportunity to get another step closer.

The Lady Lions will host USC at 6 p.m. on Wednesday in their final home game of the year, needing at least one more win for a chance at a tournament bid.

While this year’s Trojans look a little different than last year’s Big Ten runner-ups, USC will be one of the toughest opponents for Penn State in February.

All-time matchups

Since USC was one of the recent teams to join the Big Ten back in 2024, the Lady Lions have only had a few matchups with the Trojans over the years.

USC has dominated the blue and white over the years, with Penn State boasting a 1-5 all-time record. After the Nittany Lions won the first contest in 1980, the Trojans have won five straight over the years, including a trio of games in the 1980’s and two games over the past few years.

Last year, the Lady Lions traveled to the West Coast and were demolished by USC, falling 95-73 amidst a seven-game losing streak.

Three of Penn State’s top players in last year’s matchup — Gabby Elliott, Alli Campbell and Talayah Walker — combined for 42 points, but have since transferred away from the team. That leaves just Gracie Merkle, Moriah Murray and Shaelyn Steele, who tallied a combined 17 points against the Trojans.

Meanwhile, USC was led by JuJu Watkins and Kiki Iriafen, who scored 35 and 28 points, respectively.

This year, Watkins is out with a torn ACL, while Iriafen is now playing in the WNBA. Of the Trojans who scored in last year’s battle, only Kennedy Smith, Malia Samuels and Vivian Iwuchukwu, who scored a total of 10 points, are back this year.

2025-26 Trojans

Without Watkins, USC has been a little worse off than last year. The Trojans come into Wednesday’s game with a 17-10 record, including a 4-8 record against top-25 teams.

USC was in the top-25 up until the start of 2026, when it lost four straight to UCLA, Oregon, Minnesota and Maryland. However, the team experienced a recent six-game winning streak, including a big upset win over then-No. 8 Iowa.

In Watkins’ absence, a pair of new faces has shouldered the load this year for the Trojans.

Freshman guard Jazzy Davidson leads USC in scoring and sits ninth in the Big Ten with 18.0 points per game. The No. 1 athlete in the 2025 recruiting class has recorded a dozen 20-point games this year and is coming off a career-high 32 point game.

Kara Dunn joined the Trojans with more experience after three seasons with Georgia Tech.

This year, she’s averaging the most points in her career with 15.6 points per game.

Wednesday’s matchup

While USC is statistically the better team coming into this week’s contest, Kiyomi McMiller has been unstoppable over the past month.

McMiller has recorded five straight 30-point games, the first Big Ten athlete since Caitlin Clark, who was drafted No. 1 in the 2024 WNBA Draft. But even before those five games, she still led the Lady Lions in scoring with 21.6 points per game while increasing her field goal percentage significantly.

If Watkins was playing, it would be an all-star showdown between her and McMiller. But even without it, the Trojans have a star-studded backcourt that will be a problem for the late-climbing blue and white.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: 'Continuing the service, leadership and excellence' | Alpha Kappa Alpha reinstates Penn State chapter
author: Neel Ved | The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor's Note: A previous version of this story stated that Crear said the sorority was experiencing administrative challenges. This quote was taken out of context. We've since updated the story to include the full quote. The Daily Collegian apologizes for this misstep.

Alpha Kappa Alpha (AKA) was founded at Howard University in Washington D.C in January of 1908, making it the first and oldest sorority within the Divine Nine. In fall 2025, Penn State’s chapter of AKA was reinstated, which Alanna Burton describes as an honor.

“The chapter has been here since 1953,” Burton, the membership chair, said. “It is great to be part of the legacy of Alpha Kappa Alpha.”

Currently, AKA Incorporated has 1,115 chapters in 15 nations and territories.

The sorority was founded on five main principles: "to cultivate and encourage high scholastic and ethical standards, to promote unity and friendship among college women, to study and help alleviate problems concerning girls and women in order to improve their social stature, to maintain a progressive interest in college life and to be of 'Service to All Mankind.'"

Burton said being a part of the AKA legacy has been “the most rewarding part.” Since becoming an AKA she’s given back to the community in a number of ways, including supplying holiday dinners to over 20 families in Colorado Spring, and working with local school districts to support military families in need during the holiday season.

Chapter President Brooklyn Swen said the process of reinstating the chapter involved paperwork done mostly by its graduate chapter in Philadelphia. She said her role has come with challenges as she has to balance her newfound responsibilities with her academics, but it’s been “rewarding.”

“Being back on campus means continuing the service, leadership and excellence,” Swen said. “It also means the continuity of Alpha Kappa Alpha and Delta Gamma.”

Kennedy Crear said having AKA back on campus has been an amazing opportunity.

“I think it is absolutely amazing that Alpha Kappa Alpha Sorority Incorporated has been reinstated at Penn State,” Crear said. “The legacy of Alpha Kappa Alpha is just everlasting and to be able to join sisterhood while I am in my undergrad has been a wonderful opportunity. It could not have come at a better time as I am a senior, so I am happy to be part of this.”

As Penn State and the State College community host Black History month celebrations in the month of February, there is definitely a sense of responsibility for AKA according to Crear.

“I think that we always feel that sense of responsibility, but we always want to make sure that we are honoring our history and Black history during this month,” Crear said.

One of the main goals for AKA this semester was for members to serve the community. Through building sisterhood, members are able to “empower” Penn State by giving back.

"Being able to meet all of these wonderful, successful women has definitely been the best part of the whole process, and everything comes with its challenges," Crear said. "Just the administrative side of trying to (reinstate) an org back on campus was a bit challenging."

The chapter's main goals are to build sisterhood and give back to the Penn State community.

“Our first goal is always to be a ‘Service to All Mankind’ and just to make sure that we are helping our community anyway that we can and most effectively doing that," Crear said.

Having a sisterhood of Black women who understand and support each other has been amazing for Crear.

“It feels amazing, honestly,” Crear said. “Whenever I am around campus I can talk to them and relate to them. It is also not just Penn State. It is knowing that it is international, no matter where I go. There will always be someone in the sisterhood that is delighted to help me and I can try to help them in any way I can. It is just about uplifting each other and I love it so much.”

The Centre County Pearls of Pennsylvania, a recently formed interest group for AKA has assisted the organization in many ways.

“We had a founder’s day luncheon with them on Jan. 17,” Crear said. “They have been very welcoming. They are all very nice ladies and it has been great getting to know them. They are our mentors and someone that we can always go to for help.”

As for future semesters, AKA intends to continue holding many events. The sorority is currently hosting a Book “Klub” where they’re reading and discussing Bell Hooks’ “All About Love: New Visions.”

Crear said their Meet the AKA’s event was the most meaningful, as she got to see how many people were there to support them. She described the turnout as “heartwarming,” and said it was “a really great welcome back on campus.”

“I have been going to (a predominantly white institution) since middle school, so I know how important having organizations like this where you can have a support system and have people who understand what you are going through,” Crear said. “Even having these organizations to educate other people who may not know about Black history or about Divine Nine sororities in general so I think it is extremely important.”

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: Big Ten Recap | Midwestern seeding shakeups as men’s basketball season comes to close
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Big Ten Tournament is only a few weeks away from embarking on its new 18-team format, where all schools participate for the first time, and the anticipation for the action in Chicago is palpable.

Seedings are starting to solidify, but so are doubts over teams that look to contend. Here’s how the Big Ten slate played out with just two weeks of regular season play to go.

Cornhuskers continue for consistency

Nebraska has been one of the best storylines in college sports this season, as a squad that entered Big Ten media day predicted to finish in the bottom five of the conference.

The Cornhuskers rattled off 20 straight wins to begin their season, immediately snapping any negative prediction placed on them. While that streak eventually was snapped with Nebraska’s first loss, they rebounded, and until this last week of play looked like a real postseason contender.

First, a three-point loss to No. 13 Purdue on Feb. 10 raised doubts about the Cornhuskers’ ability to compete against upper-echelon teams. They then smashed Northwestern, 68-49. Nebraska suffered another bad loss to Iowa by five points on Feb. 17, then smashed Penn State by over 20.

Despite solid defense against the Hawkeyes, who shot under 35%, the offense couldn’t cash in, with only two double-digit scorers. Against the Nittany Lions, the offense followed the trend of the defense, with forward Pryce Sanford orchestrating the blowout behind a career high 33 points and a new single-game program record of eight made threes.

Cornhusker play has been very up and down, with great performances against bottom-of-the-barrel Big Ten teams, but struggling performances against better squads — a worrying sign for them as the postseason looms.

Mesmerizing moments in The Mitten State

Michigan, typically known for its Great Lakes, has been cherished on the hardwood this season for its great makes.

Both of the region’s top universities, Michigan State and Michigan, employ two of the highest-scoring offenses in the country, which have led them to be regarded as two likely national championship contenders.

Over the last week of play, both the Spartans and Wolverines were tested by two very different foes, with only one leaving the state victorious. Regardless of those split results, the consensus is that both need to find a way to rebound if they want to make good on their March Madness goals.

On Saturday, two of the teams ranked as consensus NCAA No. 1 seeds for the first bracket reveal of the season, Duke and Michigan, faced off, and the matchup lived up to all the hype.

The Blue Devils marched into a packed Chrisler Center and pulled off a nailbiter 68-63 win. Two of college basketball’s forwards went face-to-face as Yaxel Lendenborg outscored star freshman Cameron Boozer with his game-high 21 points.

Boozer’s 18 points led Duke, as it was able to put the brakes on a Wolverines’ offense that had a chance to tie things up with under 30 seconds left in regulation.

For Michigan, it was its lowest-scoring performance of the year, while Duke has consistently pulled off multiple mid-60-point wins with its staunch defense. While the Wolverines' loss saw them drop two places from their previous No. 1 AP Poll spot, the Spartans flew up a pair of spots to No. 13.

It doesn’t mean Michigan State’s win came easily; it was a one-possession game with under 20 seconds left, and the Buckeyes couldn't mount a comeback. At halftime, Ohio State actually led by three points and looked poised to win behind guard Bruce Thornton, who finished with a game-high 32 points and no turnovers.

Carson Cooper led Michigan State with his team-high 20 points. While the nation’s assist leader, Jeremy Fears Jr., had a quiet 2-of-7 night with eight assists and four turnovers.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Column | State Patty’s: Invented tradition
author: Julia Kushner | The Daily Collegian
description:
In 2007, when St. Patrick’s Day fell during spring break, Penn State students created State Patty’s. It’s an invented tradition where students celebrate the holiday the weekend between THON and the start of spring break.

What seems like a historical ritual is less than two decades old, yet its impact has been felt by hundreds of thousands of Penn Staters who’ve come together for one last hurrah before we all go home and then return for finals.

A tradition or a holiday usually stems from some sort of top-down governance and marks a miraculous day. Think of the celebration of Easter instituted by the early Church or the remembrance of Passover established by G-d Himself. State Patty’s defies the norm in that it was invented off the cuff by a student FaceBook group.

State Patty’s is indicative of social cohesion, symbolic unity and an extra moment of fraternization. Seemingly, the whole town dresses in green and gets together for a fun-filled weekend.

Even the local businesses partake. Bars downtown used to sell green beer. Although food dye in alcohol was recently nixed, the bars take advantage of this special event where I’m sure the monetary reward makes all the craziness worthwhile.

State Patty’s has created a local legend unique to Penn State and Penn State alone, which is worthy of mention in and of itself. Can you believe we just sort of made up a holiday and it’s lasted this long?

And to me, State Patty’s is representative of certain aspects of American civic culture.

A young nation with young holidays, often celebrated with at least a cold one or two. State Patty’s emulates these Americanisms. Ritual creations are important for communal unity and create healthy self-esteem in who we are as a unit.

For example, Thanksgiving, though celebrating a centuries-old event, was only formally instituted by Abraham Lincoln, and July 4th wasn’t even a federal holiday until 1870. Both of these holidays are part of the fabric of the nation, and our calendars wouldn’t be the same without them.

This may be where the similarities end, but celebrating a tangentially Christian holiday. I would encourage every Christian to religiously partake in the actual holiday but drinking on a college campus seems pretty American to me.

And the institutional pushback, including the borough police and the university itself, only makes this tradition stronger.

If you’ll indulge me and put on your advertising hat for a minute, I’ll explain: To combat teen smoking, early campaigns told the youth that cigarettes were bad for them. It’ll come as no shock that a young person being told what not to do immediately went out and did said no-no. When advertisers changed their approach with the “truth” campaign, teen smoking rates dropped.

When the administration and the police tell students to lay off the drinking and partying, it only reinforces students’ desires to do so.

And I can’t fault them for it. State Patty’s is now part of the Penn State experience.

Happy drinking, students.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Column | Architecture as a cultural indicator
author: Julia Kushner | The Daily Collegian
description:
My freshman year, I was (un)fortunate enough to live in the Pollock Residence halls. I was placed in Ritner, one of the oldest residential buildings on campus.

As a freshman, I spent some time frequenting the other dormitory halls, which each had their own unique quirks. For example, in Mifflin there were urinals in all communal bathrooms, despite the fact that some floors were female-only.

Ritner had no urinals. It was obvious to me that these dormitories were built with the intention of women living in certain buildings and men in others.

Compared to East Halls, where men and women share single stall bathrooms and live right next door to each other (albeit with a same-sex roommate), Pollock was old-fashioned central.

If you’ve ever seen an old movie, you’ve probably heard both men and women express the sentiment that living together with the opposite sex is “improper.”

Back then, it was simply a cultural norm for men and women to operate in more defined individual spheres and interact with each other in more public, formal spaces.

The coexistence of the sexes has dramatically changed within the last fifty years or so, and our architectural landmarks reflect these changes.

For example, when my grandmother began attending the University of Michigan in the late 1950s, men were required to wait for their dates in the dorm lobbies during specific “open house” hours and were required to sign in and out. This same standard was held at the male dormitories.

In fact, these rules were created and implemented by women themselves. Penn State had almost identical standards, clearly illustrating the societal norm of gender separation at the time.

We once engaged in opposite-sex relationships with great caution and clear boundaries, but now we operate in a much more mutual manner.

In many universities, we share the most intimate of spaces, even if not at the same time. We may even see each other in various stages of undress or impropriety as we pass each other in the dorm hallways.

Whether you think coed dorms are a net positive or net negative for the student population, it’s interesting to note that the design of our dorm rooms is in itself indicative of the current cultural climate.

The fact that so many schools were willing to make this change in the first place reflects a certain level of societal acceptance for gender-inclusive spaces.

I haven’t seen the floor plans for the renovated Pollock dorms, but I’d bet they’re similar to East. With the destruction of these old buildings is the riddance of old societal norms and rituals.

Gone are the days of structured sexual interaction and enforced gender separation. Gone are the days of earned intimacy and administrators acting in loco parentis, in place of a parent.

The next time you stroll through campus, take stock of what’s around you. It may just be gone within the next few decades or so.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Olympic Recap | Penn State’s 5 athletes’ results in the 2026 Milano-Cortina Winter Olympic Games
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Three active Penn State student-athletes and two alumni competed on behalf of their respective nations in the 2026 Milano-Cortina Winter Olympic Games.

Four of the five Olympians, senior Tessa Janecke, sophomore Nicole Hall, freshman Matilde Fantin and Jessica Adolfsson, 2021 class, played in the women’s ice hockey tournament, while Daniel Barefoot, 2013 class, raced in a pair of skeleton events.

Janecke, Hall, Fantin and Barefoot each made their Olympic debuts, whereas Adolfsson returned for her second Olympic Games.

Here’s how the current and former Nittany Lions did in the Olympics:

Tessa Janecke (USA) | gold medal, women’s ice hockey

Gold. The Olympic Games’ most coveted prize, awarded to the champions of the world. Now, Penn State’s captain can call herself an Olympic gold-medalist.

Janecke was the first Nittany Lion to etch her name in the history books as the program’s premier gold medalist as the Americans defeated Team Canada in overtime, 2-1, to secure the first-place finish. The Orangeville, Illinois, native tallied five assists in the tournament as the fourth-line center to aid Team USA’s historic sprint to the gold. Additionally, she was a consistent member of the power play unit.

After contributing a pair of helpers in the opening game against Team Czechia, Janecke added three assists in three games, including the quarterfinal game against host-nation Team Italy and the semifinal game against Team Sweden.

Janecke was also on the ice when captain Hilary Knight, a member of the PWHL’s Seattle Torrent, scored the game-tying goal in the gold-medal game against the Canadians, playing a key part along the boards to create space for the goal.

The Americans, backed by goaltenders Aerin Frankel, Gwyneth Philips and Ava McNaughton, outscored their competition 33-2.

Nicole Hall & Jessica Adolfsson (Sweden) | fourth place, women’s ice hockey

Hall and Adolfsson teamed up to play for Team Sweden in the Olympics. The Swedes fought all the way to overtime in the bronze-medal game against Team Switzerland and fell, 2-1.

Adolfsson scored her lone goal of the tournament against Italy in the first period of its preliminary-round game. Meanwhile, Hall went pointless throughout the Olympics, as a member of the fourth line.

Team Sweden finished the Games with a 5-2 record, sweeping Group B in round-robin play and taking down Czechia, 2-1, in the quarterfinals before falling to Janecke and the Americans, 5-0, in the semifinals.

Matilde Fantin (Italy) | quarterfinals, women’s ice hockey

Fantin represented host Team Italy for her Olympic debut and ended up being the Italians’ top scorer with three goals in the tournament.

Italy posted a 2-2 record through preliminary round-robin play in Group B and a 2-3 overall record. Fantin scored her first Olympic goal in Italy’s 4-1 victory over Team France and tallied two in a 3-2 win over Team Japan.

Italy's losses came at the hands of Hall and the Swedes, 6-1, as well as Team Germany, 2-1.

In the quarterfinals, Fantin's squad was shut out by Janecke and Team USA, 6-0.

Daniel Barefoot (USA) | men’s and mixed-team skeleton

Barefoot took part in two events for Team USA, men’s singles skeleton as well as the mixed-team skeleton competitions.

The Penn State alum finished 20th out of 24 in men's skeleton with a time of 3:49.86 at the conclusion of the fourth heat, 6.53 seconds behind leader Matt Weston of Team Great Britain.

In mixed doubles, Barefoot and his partner Kelly Curtis placed 10th out of 15 duos with a time of 2:01.43, 2.07 seconds behind gold-medalists Weston and Tabitha Stoecker.

MORE OLYMPICS COVERAGE

title: See you at the French Alps 2030 | Olympic sports Penn State majors would compete in
author: Avery Pelletier I The Daily Collegian
description:
With champions crowned and names being etched in the history books, the Milano Cortina 2026 Olympic Games ended this past weekend. After watching curling or bobsledding, many Penn State students reflect on the end of the games with the same question:“Surely I could compete in the next Olympics?”

While there’s no way for me to accurately determine who could land the triple axel, shred the half pipe or score the goal, this is what I believe different students could qualify for in 2030 based on their majors.

Figure Skating - English

As a sport, figure skating is more than just a jump or a spin. What makes this event one of the most watched during the winter is the drama of it all.

Competitive figure skaters know how to draw in an audience through very precise movements. They use every muscle in their body to tell a story that leaves viewers moved and judges wanting more.

English majors follow a similar sentiment. They know their essays are so much more than just a collection of sentences or paragraphs. Their style, organization and storytelling are what make their final product golden.

Both figure skating and English rely on a person's ability to not just create a compelling component but fit them together to tell a story.

Curling - Communications

In both of their respective fields, curling and communications majors have one significant thing in common: they often aren’t respected.

Curling as a sport is one of the more niche events. With this unfamiliarity, most spectators watch the matches without an understanding of the sport and then assume it’s easy. However, even though it’s less physically demanding, it takes immense strategy and overall communication for a team to win a match.

Similarly, communication majors don’t usually have the best reputation overall. Many people see this major as “easy” or not a real degree in comparison to STEM degrees, even though it is arguably one of the most universally useful majors.

At the end of the day though, an Olympian is an Olympian and a degree is a degree and for that, both of these deserve respect.

Skeleton - Physics

With speeds faster than a car, skeleton is not for the weak. One wrong move and a slider is barreling head first into ice at speeds upwards of 80 mph.

This Olympic sport is risky and requires an athlete who can remain collected and control their movements with immense forces against them.

As a sport rooted in motion and angles, a physics major would shine in skeleton. Not only is it an extremely challenging field, but physics majors are always forced to account for multiple variables even when they cannot see them.

While worrying about falling off their sled or hitting their head may be a new obstacle to try and maneuver, a physics major would be able to analyze and approach it like a problem set.

Hockey - Economics

This one may come with no surprise as the stereotypes around both of these fields are very similar.

Hockey players are usually seen as hyper-competitive and intense players who rely on strategy, and only care about the number on the score board. They’re not afraid of slamming other players, or getting into fistfights if they think it would help their teammates.

Similarly, economics majors are also seen as hyper-competitive students who rely on their knowledge of game theory to get them through life.

More than just the stereotypes though, economics majors would compete in hockey because, despite how difficult either of these fields are, both are able to do whatever it takes to maintain their success.

Cross-country Skiing - Pre-med

Usually viewed as one of, if not the most physically demanding sports at the Winter Olympics, cross-country skiing is not for the weak. Unlike moguls or downhill, this type of skiing is much less rooted in pure technique but physical and vascular strength.

Pre-med students here at Penn State face a similar uphill challenge, even though theirs may not come with skis. From learning organic chemistry to biology to physics to math, pre-med students need to be mentally and physically strong to get through all of their problem sets and labs.

MORE BLOGS

title: Weekly Recap | Penn State softball goes undefeated in the SpaceU Classic
author: Brielle Gari | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State headed to Orlando, Florida, last weekend to face off against four different nonconference teams in the SpaceU Classic.

Leaving the Sunshine State victorious, the Nittany Lions went 5-0 in a weekend tournament for the first time since the 2024 season. The blue and white improved to 13-2 overall throughout the course of their first 15 games.

The 2026 squad already started breaking records, as it is currently tied for its second-best start to a season in program history. Penn State collected their ninth consecutive win in a row, while also marking its longest win streak.

Starting their weekend against Charleston Southern, the Nittany Lions secured a 10-3 win, shortly after going head-to-head against then-No. 25 UCF. The blue and white led throughout the course of seven innings, but the Knights attempted a rally, scoring five runs in the bottom of the seventh.

Defeating their first ranked opponent of the season, 6-5, Penn State dominated its second day of the SpaceU Classic, outscoring Ohio and Notre Dame by 30-3. To continue the unbeaten streak, the blue and white closed out their weekend with another win, 7-0 over the Buccaneers for its second game of the weekend.

Newcomers

The Nittany Lions continued their offensive attack in Orlando, led by Penn State's dominating 2025 signing class.

Freshman Allison Onearce was in command at almost every single at-bat over the course of the last five games. Having her best performance of the weekend in the opening match of the SpaceU Classic, going 4-for-5 with 3 RBI against Charleston Southern.

With a combined total of 30 runs on Saturday, freshman Gabby Gradishar and Kristen Finarelli contributed heavily to the blue and white’s offensive run. Gradishar went 2-for-3 with 4 RBI and bashed her first collegiate home run against Ohio.

Finarelli also had a compelling time at the plate, finishing the day going 3-for-4, a home run and four RBI against the Bobcats.

In the circle, the freshmen continued to make their mark over the weekend. Left-handed pitcher Brevyn Kellepouris’ best performance this past weekend was against UCF, pitching 5.2 innings. The Olathe, Kansas, native earned the start against the Knights, allowing four hits without giving up a run.

Pitching depth

One of the biggest flaws the 2025 squad had was the control in the circle. This year's pitching staff took note and has appeared to have found a solution.

The blue and white chose the first-year sensation, right-hander Mackenzie Duncan, as she made a return to her home state this past weekend. The Winter Garden, Florida native threw 6.0 innings against the Buccaneers, finishing the day with zero earned runs along with eight strikeouts.

Sophomore, right-handed pitcher Abigail Britton earned the start against Ohio on Saturday, throwing 4.0 innings. Only allowing one earned run, three walks and four strikeouts.

To close out the weekend, junior right-handed pitcher Bridget Nemeth and Kellepouris combined for a shutout in the circle on Sunday. Nemeth started the game and threw 5.0 innings, allowing one hit with zero runs scored, and striking out three Charleston Southern batters.

Relieving Nemeth, Kellepouris entered the circle to close out Penn State’s second shutout of the 2026 season.

Offense sets the tone

For the second weekend in a row, the Nittany Lion offense has been unstoppable. With dominating performances in Saturday’s matchups, Penn State celebrated its second day of competition with high-powered hits.

Taking control from the start, the blue and white struck first in every single one of its games this past weekend. Penn State produced at the plate throughout the entire game, from the top to the bottom of the lineup.

Senior catcher Madison Seidel was efficient at the plate in their first meeting against the Buccaneers. The Hilltown, Pennsylvania, native went 2-for-5, with three RBI and a pair of doubles.

Brooke Klosowicz led the Nittany Lions to victory against host, UCF. Leading off the top of the fourth, she hit her fifth home run of the season, giving Penn State the lead.

After the series, Penn State earned its first ranking of the year, at the No. 24 spot. The blue and white close out its season-opening tournaments with the Tennessee Invitational, where the Nittany Lions face No.1 Tennessee twice in the course of the weekend.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Weekly recap | Penn State women’s lacrosse continues undefeated season in pair of wins
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Kayla Treanor experiment is off to a hot start in Happy Valley as Penn State is 5-0 for the first time since 2017.

The Nittany Lions capped off Week 2 of action with a set of big wins, including their first-ranked victory of the year.Between high goal-scorers and an early injury bug, here’s a look at Penn State’s second week of the 2026 regular season.

Payton Wainman’s success

After starting in just seven games last year with eight goals, senior attacker Payton Wainman has already reached double-digit goals this year after a big game on Sunday.

The Delray Beach, Florida, native came into the matchup against Drexel with nine goals, coming off a two-goal game against Pitt on Wednesday. Against the Dragons, she scored a career-high five goals, adding an assist to tally six points on the day.

While Wainman led the team in total goals on Sunday, she also went 5-for-5 with her shots on goal.

Through two weeks of the 2026 season, Wainman sits second on the team in scoring, just one goal behind star freshman Rocquette Allen’s 15 goals.

Erika Ho’s absence

On Feb. 10, last year’s leading scorer Erika Ho was named to the 2026 Tewaaraton Watch List, an award that will later be awarded to the nation’s best college lacrosse player. However, she’s been limited in the amount of playing time following an injury.

Ho played in the first three games of the year, scoring a total of six goals with another five assists.Over the last two games, she hasn’t appeared in the starting lineup, leaving a question as to when the star player will return.

Clutch Rocquette Allen

Allen made her collegiate debut in the season opener against Richmond in the starting lineup and has continued to make a name for herself over the past five games.

The Lone Tree, Colorado, native scored five goals against UMass and another five against Bucknell, shooting with 100% accuracy against the Bison.

Against Pitt on Wednesday, it appeared as though her hot streak would end with just one goal through 60 minutes. However, she earned the final dagger in overtime, ruining the Panthers’ late-game comeback.

Allen is now up to 15 goals on the year, leading the Nittany Lions and tied for third in the Big Ten and just outside the top-20 in the nation in total goals.

1st big win

Following a pair of dominating wins over UMass and Bucknell, Penn State faced its first true test of the year under Treanor when Pitt came to town on Wednesday.

The blue and white opened with a 5-1 commanding lead after the first quarter, which was later extended to 11-6 with 15 minutes left. Five players logged multiple goals, with senior attacker Emma Kelly leading the squad with a hat trick.

In the final quarter, Pitt scored five unanswered goals, sending the game to overtime tied up with 11 goals each.

After the Nittany Lions secured the ground ball to open the overtime period, they attempted a pair of shots by Kelly MacKinney and Allen before Allen scored the game-winner.

title: Penn State THON raises record-breaking $18.8 million
author: Mercedes Hamilton | Editor-in-Chief
description:
THON Weekend 2026 concluded with a record-breaking fundraising total of $18,841,726.53. This is the fifth consecutive year THON has broken its single-year fundraising record.

This is $1,104,685.60 more than last year's total of $17,737,040.93.

THON is the world's largest student-run philanthropy that aims to provide emotional and financial support to Four Diamonds families who have been impacted by childhood cancer.

As of 2026, THON has raised more than $254 million since it partnered with Four Diamonds in 1977.

Dancers stood in the BJC at 6 p.m. Friday night and sat down at 4 p.m. Sunday, moments before the final reveal by the THON 2026 Executive Committee.

TAKE A LOOK AT THE TOP FUNDRAISERS

title: Penn State LS Blaise Sokach-Minnick waited on the bench for 4 years, now it's likely his turn
author: Avery Hill | Football Editor
description:
A fresh pair of legs will align itself over the kicking ball for Penn State in 2026.

Redshirt senior long snapper Blaise Sokach-Minnick is expected to replace Tyler Duzansky as the Nittany Lions’ starting snapper. He started his career under both Duzansky and current Seattle Seahawk Chris Stoll — now it’s his turn.

“It’s kind of like passing the torch almost,” Sokach-Minnick said on Saturday. “I learned some things from Stoll coming in. And Tyler, I learned a lot from him.”

Sokach-Minnick is trying to follow in the footsteps of two of the more decorated long snappers in Penn State history.

Duzansky was named a Patrick Mannelly Award finalist in 2025, the honor given to the best long snapper in the nation, and is ranked as the No. 2 long snapper in the 2026 NFL Draft by ESPN’s Mel Kiper Jr.

Stoll, on the other hand, won the Mannelly Award in 2022, Sokach-Minnick’s freshman year. He went on to be signed as an undrafted free agent by the Seahawks, and a consistent performance from Stoll helped his special teams unit have a big day in the Super Bowl win over the New England Patriots earlier this month.

Duzansky learned a ton from Stoll, on and off the field, on his way to becoming Penn State’s lead snapper. A similar sentiment stands for Sokach-Minnick, who noted the small tips he was able to pull from the 2026 draft prospect helped in preparation for potentially the starting reins.

“He’s one of the greatest snappers I’ve met,” Sokach-Minnick said. “Working with him and challenging myself and challenging each other has been really what’s made us the best in the past however many years.”

But the difference between the two rises to the snapping throne is that it only took Duzansky two years as a spectator, while it took Sokach-Minnick four.

Coming into Penn State, he was ranked a five-star snapper by Rubio Long Snapping. He was an intriguing, athletic prospect with experience at both quarterback and defensive end for in-state Wyoming Area High School, and yet he has only seen the field in six games in his career.

It’s tough being a long snapper, as even with a successful offseason, trying to prepare more than one snapper could cause inconsistencies. But the West Pittston, Pennsylvania, native knew what he signed up for, so he remained patient.

“It’s been tough at some times,” Sokach-Minnick said. “There’s some setbacks that you have to go through, but at the end of the day, you have to go back to your roots and your values and your foundations and understand what you’re really doing this for, and I chose to come here.”

That same patience, paired with being an Pennsylvania kid, made it easy for Sokach-Minnick to decide he’ll return to Penn State for the 2026 season under new head coach Matt Campbell — Campbell’s decision to retain special teams coach Justin Lustig likely made it even easier.

“He’s one of the greatest coaches I’ve ever had,” Sokach-Minnick said. “He’s keeping everything consistent.”

Sokach-Minnick likely only has one year to do so, but it’s now his turn to man the team’s most unique position.

It’s an opportunity he has embraced, not just for his own sake, but for the sake of his teammates as he now becomes the veteran, and potential captain, for a special teams unit that thrived in 2025.

“It's a special opportunity, being an older guy in the program,” Sokach-Minnick said. “I’m taking it day by day, through every workout, trying to bring the best out of my teammates, which will bring the best out of the whole team.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State seniors 'excited to just have these memories' as they wrap up final THON
author: Neel Ved | The Daily Collegian
description:
Whether as a dancer, committee member, general member of a student organization or just within the stands, many seniors are taking one last stand during THON Weekend 2026.

THON's status as a student-run philanthropy means many of the individuals who power it eventually graduate. Though many opt to attend THON as alum and continue to donate after graduation, emotions are still high as reality settles in.

Courtney Peterson is at THON this year with Gamma Phi Beta and Kappa Alpha Theta. She has participated in THON since her freshman year, and said it’s amazing to see Penn State come together for the event.

“Seeing that final total of how much money we raise and being paired with families, and seeing the difference we actively make in their lives is the best thing ever,” Peterson, a fourth-year student studying mechanical engineering, said.

Peterson said it’s a rare opportunity to be part of something as important as THON, and is grateful she’s able to get a chance to participate.

“I am really sad that it is my senior year, and it is my last time doing this, but I feel like that it gave me even more of an incentive to really go hard,” Peterson said. “Now, I am doing 46 hours in the stands because of that.”

Rosie Seidenberg is at THON this year as a member of Alpha Kappa Psi. Seidenberg, a fourth-year studying finance, said she’s never been involved with fundraising of this level until she came to Penn State.

“Penn State has made this experience really magical, and I am grateful for that,” Seidenberg said.

Seidenberg said she participates in THON because it is rare to live a life that has not been impacted by cancer.

“There is a whole other (side) of it that is just standing up for something so meaningful,” Seidenberg said. “Losing anyone to any cause, and being able to do something — even if it is not cancer — is a huge experience, because you can feel really powerless when you can not help someone.”

As this is Seidenberg’s last THON Weekend, she has been reflecting on the importance of everyone’s work at THON.

“I have been someone who has shown up in the stands for the past four years, and it is important to me that people realize how important it is to the rest of THON, and I hope that stays a thing when I leave,” Seidenberg said.

For Sicile Heun, THON has only been a part of her time at Penn State for a year. Her first year with THON, Heun serves as a Rules and Regulations committee member.

She said she wants to focus on supporting families so they can create more memories with their kids.

“I THON for the kids, so they can have more moments with their kids,” Heun, a fourth-year studying elementary and early childhood education, said. “I’m sad, but also happy to be here excited to just have these memories.”

MORE THONTENT

title: Parents of THON dancers 'in awe' of the scope, philanthropy of THON
author: Kippi Hall | The Daily Collegian
description:
While Penn State students may be used to THON’s enormity, parents of THON dancers like Neil Crowley, father of Lily Crowley, are “amazed” at how big the event is.

Christine Zambrio, mother of Julia Zambrio, was impressed that the philanthropy is entirely student-run and how much “time and effort these kids put into it” year-round.

Even parents that are Penn State alumni, like Rob Corcoran, father of Luke Corcoran, marvel at the dedication of students to the cause: “I always thought to myself, 'My gosh, I don’t know if I can do it,’ and Luke said he was going to do it … I was in shock and awe of him.”

title: As THON continues to evolve, Penn State alums say ‘the spirit’ remains
author: Alex Fischer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Thousands of students have called the Bryce Jordan Center home over this THON Weekend, but many alumni have also traveled near and far to return to the place they once called home.

David Smith said he and his wife attended THON to support their niece, Shannon Pinizzotto, who's dancing this year as part of an Independent Dancer Couples (IDC).

Although school pride and fundraising efforts have continued throughout the years, Smith, class of 1982 Penn State graduate, said many things about THON are different from when he was a student.

“I think it was only in place about five years," Smith said. “We were in the White Building, and there were no lines. We could just walk in. There were no wristbands.”

Smith said THON has grown in size and complexity, as well as in the amount it raises, but remains the same at its core.

“If a dancer would collect $50, that was a lot,” Smith said. “ The spirit of THON is still the same.”

Penn State alums Jim and Cassie Vomero brought their daughter to THON this year to show her what it’s like.

“We wanted to show her what we grew up with, what we enjoyed the whole time we were at college,” Cassie Vomero, class of 2006 Penn State graduate, said.

Jim said THON has grown a lot from when he and Cassie were in school.

“When we got involved, it was way more scrappy,” Jim Vomero, class of 2009 Penn State graduate, said. “The university certainly supported it, but not like today, where they have all this space and all this opportunity.”

Jim encourages those at THON to enjoy every line dance, even when tired.

“I can tell you now, 20 years later, I’m still singing those line dances at random points in my life,” Jim said.

Cassie, a THON 2005 dancer, said many things are different from when she was a dancer, including the 10 cents she spent per text message and the lack of social media.

“What’s the same is the community, the love, the passion everyone has,” Cassie said.

“Embrace it all. Do everything you can for the dancers out there. Keep going. The end is near, the memories will last forever.”

Though THON remains an important part of many alums’ Penn State story, Holly Maitland-McKenna said she didn’t know THON existed at all during her time as a student.

Maitland-Mckenna, class of 1987 Penn State graduate, only learned about THON when she moved to Harrisburg in 2012. Now a swim instructor at Penn State Harrisburg, she said her students taught her about THON and inspired her to give back.

Maitland-McKenna began an annual “Swim for a Cure” swim-a-thon event to raise money for THON, and became an adviser for Penn State Harrisburg Benefitting THON.

Then, in 2017, one of her cousins was diagnosed with a tumor behind her eye and became a Four Diamonds child.

“That just made it even more important to raise money for the cause,” Maitland-McKenna said.

Maitland-McKenna’s cousin had to have two bone marrow transplants, each one costing around $35,000.

“When I hit $70,000, I was like, ‘Okay cool, that paid for those,’” Maitland-McKenna said. “I got really excited.”

Her swim-a-thon has been increasing in length over the years. In 2022, she swam for 46 hours. Three weeks later, she danced in THON for the Dancer Marathon Alumni Interest Group.

“Be meaningful about your involvement with THON and try to raise as much money as you can,” Maitland-McKenna said. “It’s the best weekend of the year.”

MORE THONTENT

title: Let OL Cooper Cousins’ bicep tattoo tell it: his decision to return to Penn State was easy
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
Upon players announcing their return to Penn State for the 2026 season, Cooper Cousins tweeted a puzzle piece emoji.

Cousins, an important piece for the future, was the first player in the puzzle to announce his return. It was an easy choice for him.

“My vision was clear and set in stone,” Cousins said. “I’m going to go to Penn State University. I am going to get a degree from Penn State University. I’m going to play football at Penn State University, and that was really it for me. So, there was no doubt in my mind.”

Following the Pinstripe Bowl win over Clemson, a game that had Cousins earn his first collegiate start at left guard, he sat down with his parents to discuss his future with the Nittany Lions. His father, Ed, who was a prison guard, and his mom, Holly, who is an elementary school reading specialist, believed Cousins should evaluate his options as the program transitioned from James Franklin to Terry Smith to Matt Campbell.

Cousins had other plans. Just as quickly as he committed to Penn State as a sophomore at in-state McDowell High School in the 2024 recruiting class, becoming the first player to do so in the class, he reaffirmed his commitment to the Nittany Lions for the 2026 campaign.

“I really don’t want to take my time with this,” Cousins recalled telling his parents. “I really want to stay here because I really like this place.”

Cousins isn’t lying when he talks about his affinity for Penn State. When you think of what it means to bleed the blue and white, his name usually comes up as a player who embodies that. In fact, the offensive lineman and Erie native showed a tattoo on his left bicep of the Penn State logo within an outline of Pennsylvania.

The reasons that Cousins cited to stay were similar to the reasons why some of his teammates returned to Happy Valley.

“I think the guys that did stay here are pretty much the same as me,” Cousins said. “With those points that I talked about, we bleed this place. We love this place. I got it tattooed on my bicep. This place is truly something special, and I was never thinking about leaving a place like Penn State.

Cousins projects as a starter next year, and said he’ll likely see most of his work during spring ball at right guard. He’s entering his junior season coming off of what he revealed to be lower body injuries.

But Cousins is notoriously a gym rat. During his freshman year, Franklin would watch from his office window in perplexity as Cousins would practice his pass protection with his headphones in under the stars while everyone else had left.

He’d also stay in the offensive line team meeting room till the late hours in the night, texting ex-starting left tackle Drew Shelton at 11 p.m. with a question and a picture on the whiteboard — Shelton told him to go home.

That dedication for himself, along with his new and returning teammates, hasn’t changed.

“We're working our tails off right now, and there's more work to be done and we know that,” Cousins said. “We're going to continue to grind each and every single day so we can fulfill that vision that we have for ourselves.”

Offensive coordinator Taylor Mouser referred to Cousins as someone who “eats nails for breakfast” and is a “massive human road grader.” Yet, Cousins is more than just a highly touted 6-foot-6, 316-pound offensive lineman.

He’s been someone who has been a huge advocate for THON. He’s been a friend to Gannon Kupko Jr., who was partnered with Penn State through Team Impact, a program that “matches children facing serious illness and disability with college sports teams, creating a long-term, life-changing experience for everyone involved.”

He was an Allstate Wuerffel Trophy Watch List nominee, a recognition given to players who embody community service and both academic and athletic achievement.

Cousins’ impact goes on a long way both on and off the field. As someone who has been referred to as a future captain by both teammates of the past and present, Penn State has a foundational piece in a new era of the program.

It might be a new regime, but one face that won’t be new is Cousins, who despite all the twists and turns over the last few months, had no intention of leaving a place that is close to his heart.

“No matter what happened, no matter who came here, no matter who’s the coach here or what was going on, I truly am in love with this place,” Cousins said. “I’m truly in love with the university and I love the game of football, and I want to do all that here. Like I said, there was no doubt in my mind I was staying here. I’m stuck.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse continues dominant season with win at Drexel
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State took the field early Sunday morning amidst the incoming snowstorm on the East Coast.

Despite it being their first road trip of the year, the Nittany Lions came out of Philadelphia with a 16-3 victory over Drexel.

The blue and white’s early scoring success was on display against the Dragons as senior Maddie Greco was the first to score just 14 seconds into the game.

While Drexel kept the Nittany Lions off the scoreboard for the next five minutes, it couldn’t generate any offense of its own. After a missed shot by sophomore Ellie Wisch and junior Delaney Radin, Wisch earned her third goal of the season to take the early 2-0 lead.

That lead continued through the first quarter as Radin scored her first of the day and freshman Rocquette Allen scored her 15th goal of the year off an assist from Radin.

Following a miss from Emma Kelly, the blue and white were back to its scoring roots with another set of goals from Anna Salerno, Payton Wainman, Lizzie Anné and another from Salerno to end the first quarter up 8-0.

After not having any offensive success in the entire first quarter, Drexel earned its first shot of the game to start the second period, but it was saved by Sydney Manning.

With junior goalie Kate Sharp in the net in relief for Lucy Dailey — who allowed eight goals without a save through 15 minutes — the Nittany Lions had their first shot of the quarter saved, but quickly found the back of the net with Anné’s second goal of the day.

The Dragons' offense continued to find momentum with six shots and four on goal through the second quarter, but failed to capitalize on any of the opportunities.

The blue and white, meanwhile, added another three to finish the quarter as Wainman earned the first hat trick of the day, taking a 12-0 lead into the half.

While Drexel earned the draw control to start the half — just its fourth of the day — a quick turnover and foul gave the Nittany Lions a free position opportunity, which Emma Kelly capitalized on for her seventh goal of the year.

After 36 minutes with nothing on the scoreboard, the Dragons earned their first goal from Anne Madden, taking a small sliver out of Penn State’s now-12-point lead. Backup goalie Arden Jansen went in at halftime and was quickly at a 0% save percentage.

As the third quarter wound down, the Nittany Lions only had two shots in the entire 15 minutes. However, Drexel didn’t have much success either and scored just twice to cut the deficit to 11 points.

The fourth quarter started with Dailey back in the net for the Dragons. Following her eight goals against in the first quarter, she let in another two from Wainman — who reached a career-high five goals — to start the fourth quarter.

Down by 13 points midway through the final quarter, Drexel nets its third goal of the game, but is soon after brought back to a 13-point difference with Radin’s second goal of the game, assisted by Wainman.

With the game winding down, the Dragons attempted another pair of shots, but didn’t find the back of the net. Penn State outshot Drexel 30 shots to 16, recording twice as many shots on goal as it moves to 5-0 on the year.

Up next

The Nittany Lions stay on the road as they take on Cornell at 3 p.m. on Saturday. The Big Red are 1-1 to start the year.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball earns shutout win over Charleston Southern, goes 5-0 on weekend
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State wrapped up its time at the SpaceU Classic in Orlando against Charleston Southern on Sunday morning. The blue and white rode an eight-game winning streak and looked to go undefeated at the tournament with a victory.

Coach Clarisa Crowell’s squad just keeps on rolling, earning a shutout 7-0 victory over Charleston Southern and taking a 13-2 record into Week 4.

The two squads already faced each other on Friday, with Penn State routing the Buccaneers, 10-2. Sophomore right-hander Kinsey Clopton shut down the Nittany Lions scorching offense in the first, but the bats quickly caught fire in the second.

First baseman Kirsten Finarelli drew a five-pitch lead-off walk and designated player Gabby Gradishar followed with another walk. Clopton couldn’t find her control and walked catcher Madison Siedel, which brought in sophomore right-hander Brenna Conley.

With bases loaded and no outs, right fielder Cara Bohner lifted a sacrifice fly to center and drove in the first run of the game, 1-0. Shortstop Kaylie Walters tapped an infield single in front of catcher Emma Smith and everyone was safe. However, Conley locked in and retired the next two batters.

While the blue and white’s offense only plated one run across in the first three innings, junior right-hander Bridget Nemeth dealt on the mound. The Glendora, California, native totaled three strikeouts and allowed just one hit over the first four frames.

The bottom of Penn State’s lineup set up its big bats for some damage in its half of the fourth. Bohner reached on a bobbled error by second baseman Maddie Lee. Walters advanced Bohner into scoring position with a bunt. Leadoff hitter Natalie Lieto blooped a single into no man’s land to right center, and second baseman Allison Oneacre came up with runners on the corners with just one out.

The freshman barreled a two RBI double that just missed clearing the wall in left center. Third baseman Brooke Klosowicz then followed with a single through shortstop Emma Villaescusa, but Oneacre was thrown out at home.

However, left fielder Michela Barbanente delivered with a two-out RBI single to left and Klosowicz scored. Following Finarelli’s walk, Gradishar blasted a double deep into left, plating Barbanente and extending Penn State’s lead, 5-0.

Nemeth provided another shutdown inning in the fifth and the Nittany Lions’ offense tacked onto their lead in the bottom half. Bohner and Walters started the scoring with back-to-back singles, but Lieto grounded out and pinch runner Kalli Karwowski was thrown out at home.

Lee committed her second error of the game at second base and allowed Walters to score off a stolen base by Lieto, who advanced to third off the miscue. Oneacre plated another run off a sacrifice fly to right and Penn State was suddenly up 7-0.

Freshman Brevyn Kellepouris came in for relief and pitched two excellent innings, allowing just one base runner off a hit by pitch, and securing the Nittany Lions' ninth straight victory.

Up Next

Penn State hits the road again for the Tennessee Invitational, beginning with the opening game against N.C. Central at 12:30 p.m. Friday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: 'THON is home' | German family reflects on over 20 years of THON support
author: Reese Igou | The Daily Collegian
description:
Every Four Diamonds family has a different story, and the German family has a unique one of their own.

In 2002, Kirsten was diagnosed with brain cancer at just six years old. Her doctors found an extremely rare, cancerous ependymoma tumor a quarter the size of her brain on the side of her head.

This unexpected diagnosis came as a shock to the family. After surgery and weeks of radiation the cancer was removed, but treatment left Kirsten with lasting side effects. Then, in 2024, she was diagnosed with thyroid cancer.

“I really don’t remember my life without having cancer,” Kirsten said.

Even though her life has been full of doctors appointments, treatments and surgeries, she still manages to maintain her positivity. A big part of this is the hope and home THON has provided her family, Kirsten said.

“THON is home,” Kirsten said. “THON is my place to be able to go and not have to think about the world. I don’t have to think about what my hair looks like … the medication I take every single day … THON’s that place of peace for me … a place like no other, full of colors and support.”

THON’s continuous partnership with the Four Diamonds organization, as well as its collaboration with more than 400 student organizations around the Penn State community, help make it the largest student‑run philanthropies ever.

“It’s the 15,000 students in this building that don’t know Kirsten that are raising money…the next penny might be the one that puts us over the top,” Kirsten's father Greg German said. “We’re thankful for the thousands of hours and thousands of kids.”

Kirsten said Four Diamonds has supported her family from the moment she received her diagnosis.

“We never saw a bill,” Kirsten’s mother Kim German said. “They truly eliminate all of that. It's incredible not to have that worry.”

Kirsten said the financial assistance and family support over the past 24 years has significantly lessened anxiety for the Germans.

“The focus became my family… instead of worrying about where our money is going to go,” Kirsten said.

Throughout their journey, the family has continued to grow closer and keeps an unwavering love for each other throughout the hardships, Kim said.

Though Kirsten said her body will always hold the lasting effects of cancer, she still finds ways to enjoy her favorite things, like amusement parks and traveling. She believes it’s essential for places, such as Hersheypark, to provide accessible accommodations for individuals.

“Cancer doesn't end when the diagnosis … you have to adapt,” Kirsten said. “Every day is a different day.”

Kirsten has not let cancer deter her from her passions. Currently, she is working as a fourth-grade cyber school teacher and continues to travel with her family. Their goal is to visit all 50 states.

“We try to live life to the fullest,” Kim said.
