The right-handed pitcher continued her dominance and mowed down the Bobcats' bats in the fourth. Britton totaled four strikeouts and allowed just one run off two hits and three walks over four innings.

Penn State’s offense stayed rolling in the fifth and added more insurance runs. Barbanente slapped a one-out single to right and swiftly recorded her second steal of the day. After Finarelli popped up to shortstop, catcher Madison Seidel knocked a two-out RBI single to center and widened the Nittany Lions’ lead, 5-1.

Freshman Gabby Gradisher broke the game open with her first collegiate home run that was belted deep into left field. Fresh off an outstanding performance in the win over UCF, coach Clarisa Crowell brought in freshman Brevyn Kellepouris in the bottom of the fifth with a comfortable 7-1 lead.

The Nittany Lions offense turned the game into a total slugfest and exploded in the sixth, plating nine runs in the inning off six hits and four walks. Barbanente, Finarelli, Gradishar, Hanik, Allison Oneacre and Jordyn Jochims all recorded an RBI in the frame.

The score was well out of reach, and Penn State wrapped up its 11th victory of the campaign in just six innings.

Up Next

Penn State looks to extend its seven-game winning streak against Notre Dame in thesecond game of Saturday’s doubleheader.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: No. 10 Penn State men’s lacrosse suffers second unranked upset, falls to Navy on the road
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 10 Penn State — after bouncing back from an overtime loss to Villanova with a convincing 13-7 victory over then-No. 3 Princeton on the road — traveled to Annapolis, Maryland, for a dance with Navy on Saturday at noon.

The Nittany Lions fell in a nail-biting contest for their second unranked loss of the season, losing to the Midshipmen, 12-11, to close out a two-game road trip. Penn State falls to 2-2 on the season while Navy improves to 3-1.

First half

Navy started off the game strong on both ends of the field. After a defensive stop, William Goers took the ball from behind goaltender Preston Hawkins’ crease with a step on Will Costin. Goers evaded an incoming slide before dunking the ball into the cage to open the scoring.

The Nittany Lions responded quickly, working the ball around the box to Kyle Lehman, who stepped into a top-corner shot to tie the game. Lehman took the lead less than two minutes later as goaltender Dan Daly wasn’t able to get back in the crease after a failed 10-man ride.

Mac Haley answered with a goal with 7:51 remaining in the first quarter, picking off Hawkins’ outlet pass before firing into the open net. Coach Jeff Tambroni elected to call his first timeout as the Nittany Lions weren’t able to cleanly clear the ball.

However, Navy’s 10-man ride proved ineffective for a second time. Penn State’s broken clear ended with a long-distance shot by Alex Ross. It was backed up by Jack Iannantuono, who fed the ball to long-stick midfielder Lucca DiBartolomeo for a second open-net goal.

Patrick Carragher added to the blue and white’s lead with 4:14 on the clock, taking an interior pass from Hunter Aquino before shooting past Daly’s stick.

But it didn’t take long for the Midshipmen to tie the game, 4-4. First, it was Alec Gregorek from the middle of the box off a centering pass by Jack Ponzio. Then, Goers scored a man-up goal in transition with 19 seconds remaining in the first quarter.

Ponzio began the second quarter on the right foot, scoring his first goal of the season to give Navy a 5-4 lead. Goers earned his hat trick with 11:09 remaining in the first half, bouncing a long-distance shot under Hawkins’s stick to take a 6-4 lead.

Liam Matthews brought the Nittany Lions within one, catching a pass on the doorstep and diving into a shot over Daly’s stick. With 3:49 left in the half, Chase Robertson tied the game, 6-6, on a mid-range shot that stung the cage short-side on Daly.

Jack Flaherty gave Navy a 7-6 lead with 43 seconds remaining on a skip pass from Goers.

Second half

Hawkins immediately saw action to start the third quarter, making a stick save on a close-range shot. On the subsequent transition, Lehman got the ball to Jon King, who rifled a shot into the back of the net to tie the game, 7-7.

Aquino scored his first goal of the game with 7:15 left in the third quarter on the man-up as AJ Marsh took a one-minute slashing penalty. After Carragher pitched a pass to the perimeter, Aquino shot past Daly. However, Flaherty tied the game, 8-8, as he found himself wide open streaking down the center.

Matthews notched his second of the day on a one-timer from Luke Walstrum’s cross-crease assist to take a 9-8 lead going into the fourth quarter.

Daly started the fourth quarter hot with a series of saves on Lehman to keep Navy within striking distance. On the other end of the field, Haley made a pass to Gregorek through the entire Penn State defense before the sophomore delivered a game-tying goal, 9-9.

Walstrum isolated short-stick midfielder Graham Parker in order to dodge and shoot past Daly to take a 10-9 lead before Sean Gallagher slid to defend. However, Haley got his hands free to tie the game, 10-10, with 5:21 remaining in the game.

With 3:01 left in the game, Haley threw an underhand shot on net with the shot clock winding down. Hawkins wasn’t able to make the save as the ball squeaked through his five-hole. Haley tallied another goal to take a 12-10 lead 36 seconds later.

Walstrum found Carragher to bring the Nittany Lions within one with 1:51 on the clock, but the damage had already been done. Navy coasted to the 12-11 lead.

Up Next

The Nittany Lions return to Happy Valley for their Military Appreciation game against Yale at Panzer Stadium at 1 p.m. on Feb. 28.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball makes costly errors, offense goes flat in loss to Richmond
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
After starting 2026 in one of the worst ways possible, with a 1-2 record where Penn State was outscored 48-11, the Nittany Lions left their disaster in the desert with a chance to rebound against Richmond.

The blue and white cruised to a shutout Game 1 win on Friday, but with the chance to go above .500 and keep the streak of results coming, coach Mike Gambino’s squad ultimately fell to the Spiders, 3-0.

After he earned the first win on the mound for Penn State this season, with a five-inning outing with only two hits allowed, right-handed arm Ben Hudson got his second start of his Nittany Lions’ career against the Spiders.

Both the sophomore and Richmond starter Joey Giordano cruised to three-up, three-down first frame, and the second kept the two level. Despite allowing singles, they both got out of the inning unharmed, with a punchout.

Penn State shortstop Preston Yaucher reached third via a single and a throwing error, and as designated hitter Michael Anderson drew a walk, it looked as if Penn State had the first chance to score with runners on the corners and two outs. Ultimately, Giordano axed the jam and relied on his defense to end the top half of the third inning.

In the home half, things got sloppy for the Nittany Lions. Hudson walked two, and a Bryce Molinaro error at the corner scored one, with an eventual second scored via a sacrifice fly. Molinaro got a hit to start the top of the frame, but the offense was no dice and went down scoreless; however, the Spiders’ bats didn’t.

A pair of singles and a hit-by-pitch got Hudson in a jam, and a sacrifice fly pushed the lead to 3-0, yet the Medford, New Jersey, native recorded a punch out on a dropped third strike, the same way he got out the inning prior.

Luckily for Penn State, just like it’d given Richmond a chance, Giordano returned the favor with a pair of walks, except the Nittany Lions’ offense couldn’t cash in.

Gambino opted for a change with righty for righty, as Ben DeMell replaced Hudson, the same change made in the Nittany Lions’ strong pitching win over Grand Canyon.

The redshirt senior turned things over quickly to the Spiders’ pitching again, as they made their own change with lefty Giordano replaced on the mound with right-handed arm Bijan Anvar, who silenced the blue and white bats in consecutive fashion with defensive outs.

DeMell took things over with a punch-out, and despite allowing a single, he navigated out of the inning. Left fielder Jesse Jaconski started off the seventh frame with a single, but when Avery Smith struck out, and the senior got caught on the steal, it killed all chance of scoring with one swoop.

Yaucher then struck out for the second time on the day, and the inning flipped over. DeMell kept in a groove with a one-two-three inning started by a whiffing strikeout. Richmond made a few adjustments in the infield and also replaced Anvar with 6-foot-7 righty Justin Gay.

The Nittany Lions reached base only via a hit-by-pitch, as the offense again went quiet. Even as the offense couldn’t buy him any slack, DeMell kept up the fire on the mound. The Concord, Ohio, native recorded his third strikeout of the game and got out of a light scare courtesy of a Richmond double.

With the blue and white bats' last chance to make a crack, up stepped right fielder Jack Porter, yet he promptly went right back to the pine after a four-pitch foul flyout. First baseman Spencer Barnett followed with an even briefer at-bat, flying out to center on two pitches.

Jaconski walked into the box with Penn State’s last chance, and while he extended the at-bat and saw five pitches, the last one he looked a little too long at, didn’t swing, taking a strike three.

Penn State finished with four hits, which only trailed Richmond by one. With a more efficient strike rate that resulted in one more strikeout than the Spiders, the Nittany Lions’ performance is nothing to scoff at — costly errors, small ball and strong Richmond defense defeated them in the margins.

No blue and white batter recorded a multi-hit day. Both Hudson and DeMell split eight innings of work, with only one earned run and three total walks.

Up Next

Penn State closes out its Richmond series following the cancellation of its Sunday clash, with Game 2 of a doubleheader later Saturday. First pitch is scheduled for 1:25 p.m.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball faces Ohio and Notre Dame
author: Brielle Gari | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State continues its trip in Orlando, Florida, for its third weekend of the season, after a 2-0 start to the day.

Following their 4-1 weekend at the Houston Invitational, the Nittany Lions continue their successful season in the Sunshine State.

It was a successful start for Penn State as it defeated Charleston Southern in a 10-3 final, dominating with a six-run third inning.

The Nittany Lions took down No. 25 UCF in a thrilling end to maintain an undefeated weekend and earn their first ranked victory of the season.

Ohio

Penn State is set to play Ohio for its third matchup of the SpaceU Classic at 11:30 a.m. Saturday morning.

This will be the ninth time in program history that both teams have met in the dirt, with the last time being in 2024.

The Nittany Lions crushed the Bobcats in five innings, 8-0, and again 4-1, in the Paradise Classic.

So far in the 2026 campaign, the Bobcats are 8-4 and hold a 1-2 record in the SpaceU Classic. They dropped a pair of games to UCF, a team the Nittany Lions defeated, but came out victorious over Notre Dame in eight innings, 5-4.

Outfielder senior Belle Hummel leads the Ohio lineup with a .537 batting average. During her junior season, she started in all 56 games, representing the Bobcats.

Hummel also is at the top of the list in slugging percentage of .780, 22 hits, and 12 RBI.

Ohio’s roster relies heavily on its veterans; another notable player for the Bobcats squad is Ohio native, senior Colleen Bare. A .477 batting average, she is second overall in hits and slugging.

Notre Dame

Penn State’s second game of Saturday, and fourth in the classic, will be against Notre Dame, with the first pitch tentatively scheduled for 2 p.m. The Nittany Lions have had multiple games delayed due to other games occurring during the multi-team weekends.

Saturday's matchup will be the seventh overall meeting between programs. Notre Dame leads the blue and white 4-2.

During the right-handed pitcher’s sophomore season, Kami Kamzik threw a complete game against the Nittany Lions back in February of 2025. Securing six strikeouts in her time in the circle.

Kamzik has been Notre Dame’s main pitcher so far this season, having a 1.99 ERA. Entering the circle for their second game of SpaceU Classic, the Junior fought hard with her good start attempts, but the Ohio offense had very consistent at-bats.

Holding a 7-6 overall record on the season, Notre Dame is 0-2 in the SpaceU Classic. The Fighting Irish finished 11th in the ACC in the 2025 season, only earning seven wins within the conference. Notre Dame had just a 23-31-1 overall record for its 2025 campaign, a number it looks to improve on in 2026.

Having a pretty new squad, the Fighting Irish welcome six freshmen to the team.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State track and field closes regular season at Penn State Tune-Up, Philadelphia Metro
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a record-breaking two months of action, Penn State’s final regular season meets are here.

The Nittany Lions earned their final marks before the postseason on Friday, competing in the Penn State Tune-Up and Philadelphia Metro.

Penn State Tune-Up

Penn State started the day hot, earning a top-three sweep in the women's weight throw.

Senior Alex Pancoast led the way with a first-place throw of 20.05 meters, followed by Gabby Cope’s 18.88 meter throw and Gabi Deglau’s personal best of 18.12 meters.

The men's weight throw saw similar success with three of the top floor places, led by graduate student Collin Burkhart.

Burkhart threw 21.12 meters for first, with Sam Engebretson’s 19.19-meter throw for third and sophomore Matt Sarnowski’s fourth-place throw of 17.74 meters.

Sophomore Brady Mider finished second in the men's shot put, throwing a personal best 16.69 meters.

The Nittany Lions earned another sweep in the men's pole vault, as Tristan McGarrah and Mason Bucks finished first and second with jumps of 5.15 meters and 4.90 meters, respectively.

Penn State's winning continued in the women's pole vault, as sophomore Amelia McBain took the win, jumping 3.75 meters.

Sophomore Addyson White earned big finishes in both the women's 60-meter hurdles and 60m, finishing first and second with times of 8.79 and 7.50 — the latter a personal best.

In the men’s 60m, junior Ethan Black and freshman Will Landwer qualified for the finals with personal bests of 6.81 seconds and 6.88 seconds. Black improved his best again with a second-place 6.79 seconds, while Landwer had a false start.

The Nittany Lions went back to the win column in the women’s mile, as junior Claire Zubey ran a 4:49.15 for first place. Sophomore Eva Kynaston finished shortly behind in 4:51.57 for fourth place.

Sophomore Josh Stolarski ran his first 400-meter of the season on Friday, finishing second in 48.37 seconds. The women’s 400m saw junior Kylie Jacoutot run 57.58 seconds for second and Abasiono Akpan run 57.73 seconds for a personal-best third place.

Joe White finished just shy of first in the men’s long jump, recording a jump of 6.92 meters for second.

Penn State continued to sweep events in the men’s high jump, as freshman Trip Campbell jumped 2.11 meters for first and junior Robert Allen set a season-best 2.08 meters for second.

While the women’s team fell short of first in the high jump, they took second through fourth place with Hannah Riolo leading the way. Riolo jumped 1.70 meters alongside Gwen Cudmore, who performed slightly better than Katie Becker’s 1.65 meters for fourth place.

Senior Sarah Pickering ran her first-ever indoor 800-meter on Friday, winning the race with a time of 2:08.84.

Freshman Tyler Rader wrapped up the action on the track in the men’s 3,000 meters, where he ran a personal-best 8:18.11 to win the race and close the Penn State Tune-Up.

Philadelphia Metro

The Nittany Lions started the day with the men’s mike, where a trio of Nittany Lions finished second, fourth and sixth.

Jacob Smith led the way with a personal-best 4:06.76, followed by Justin Healey’s 4:07.89 and Jake Borgesi’s 4:12.70.

The second-biggest moment to cap off the regular season was Carter Fitzgerald’s return to track after not racing since May 2025. He competed in the men’s 500-meter and ran the second-fastest time in school history in 1:01.97.

Penn State wrapped up the regular season in record-breaking fashion as Allon Clay, Niko Schultz, Handal Roban and Nick Sloff ran the fastest distance medley relay in school history in 9:18.58 — the second-fastest currently in the NCAA.

Up next

The Nittany Lions head to Indianapolis for the Big Ten championship, kicking off on Feb. 26.

MORE TRACK AND FIELD COVERAGE

title: 'She just lights up the room' | Vyvyanna Garcia DaSilva trades in her committee T-shirt for a dancer number
author: Kahlie Wray | Managing Editor
description:
After two years of shuffling through the Bryce Jordan Center as a THON committee member, Vyvyanna Garcia DaSilva is now standing on the floor among over 700 dancers for 46 hours. A couple hours in, she said all she can think about is how high up she used to be.

“It’s a totally different experience,” Garcia DaSilva, a third-year studying accounting, said. “As a dancer there’s certain limitations, not being able to go on the mezzanine level and just enjoy the nice quiet atmosphere is different. Even seeing the upper bowl from the floor is jaw dropping, like wow I was that high up.”

During her first two years, she served as a Rules and Regulations Event Safety committee member before joining the Donor and Alumni Relations Committee her junior year. However, after one of the Accounting Society and Beta Alpha Psi’s THON chairs offered her a dancer position, she decided to trade in her committee member gear for a comfy hoodie, shorts and dancing shoes.

“She rang me the day I had a CM (committee member) interview position,” Garcia DaSilva said. “And she was like, ‘Vyv, I would like to formally offer you a THON 2026 Accounting Society Beta Psi Alpha dancing position.’ I was in the Kern Building and I was like, ‘Oh my God yes, I would love to.’”

The Bucks County, Pennsylvania, native is the Accounting Society’s current president and is also a member of DASH, a THON special interest organization. She said she’s dancing for a lot of reasons, but at the top of her list is the Declan Homan family.

The Homan’s have been DASH’s THON family for over two years. Garcia DaSilva said DASH is grateful to be paired with them.

“Declan, our THON kid, plays all the fun sports and he’s full of energy,” Garcia DaSilva said. “I dance for him. I dance so no kid stands alone.”

Around her neck, she’s wearing a silver Four Diamonds necklace gifted to her by the Homan’s just minutes before the dancers stood.

“They gave it to me and I almost cried,” Garcia DaSilva said. “ I love it and I’m never taking it off.”

The Accounting Society and Beta Alpha Psi don’t have a formal presence in the stands, but Garcia DaSilva is not without supporters. Her boyfriend, Nathan McMahon, is in the stands rooting for her.

McMahon, a recent Penn State graduate, has a unique understanding of the feat she’s undertaken having danced for DASH in 2024. He said it isn’t always easy.

“I’m here just trying to keep the mood up, making sure she’s doing all the stretches, eating and drinking constantly,” McMahon said. “Eating is a big thing. There was this one hour where I was crazy tired and then I had a snack and I was like, ‘Oh I’m fine now.’”

McMahon is accompanied by her family in the stands and will be supporting them throughout the weekend as well.

“It’s definitely a big undertaking,” McMahon said. “So I’d say I’m proud of her and all the dancers, because I know what it takes to do it.”

Eszter Badescu, a Donor and Alumni Relations Committee member, said she was immediately excited when Garcia DaSilva told her she was going to dance. Badescu is tasked with giving “high-stakes” donors tours and ensuring they’re comfortable, but whenever she has a break she checks in with Garcia DaSilva.

“I’m texting and calling her, making sure that she’s doing well,” Badescu, a second-year studying psychology and statistics, said.

Badescu, along with other committee members, is planning to visit Garcia DaSilva with a basket of goodies during the weekend. She said they’re all eager to support her.

“She just lights up the room,” Badescu said. “Her being down there is really great, just being able to see her is keeping me going. She’s the glue.”

MORE THON CONTENT

title: THON’s Entertainment Committee works to maintain fun, keep energy high
author: Jacob Godshall | The Daily Collegian
description:
During THON Weekend, many acts take the stage, keeping it occupied the majority of the 46-hour event. These performances are decided on and coordinated by THON’s Entertainment Committee.

The Entertainment Committee is also in charge of managing the performances throughout THON Weekend, as they work to keep the energy of the event up.

According to DJ Captain Claire Nguyen, the role of the Entertainment Committee is, predictably, to entertain.

“I always joke that we're the loud committee,” Nguyen, a fourth-year studying nutritional sciences, said. “Being around all those people that are just always so excited to do what they do, we have a very fun job … our whole goal is to make people happy.”

As one of six DJ Captains, Nguyen works with two DJ coordinators to ensure everything runs smoothly on stage. DJ captains also help coordinate displayed content.

“(We assist with) audio and visuals, and kind of feeding in some videos, what's going on screens, PowerPoints and running actual deliverables about what's happening on stage behind the scenes,” Nguyen said. “So nothing that you see on stage, but everything that happens around it almost.”

Nguyen said the majority of music is selected based on “overall vibes,” but music is chosen to specifically fit during theme hours.

“For example, we have a western theme hour,” Nguyen said. “So, we have more western and country themed music. (There’s) also a Hollywood (theme), and we have a lot of musicals coming up for that.”

The committee chooses performers via auditions, according to Stage Performance Coordinator Gunan Thangavel. Throughout the weekend, the committee works to organize performers and coordinate their acts throughout the weekend.

“(We coordinate) events like 100 days and the 5k, all the way up to THON Weekend where we're introducing every act that comes onto the stage,” Thangavel, a third-year studying aerospace engineering, said. “We basically, throughout the year, audition these acts and help support them through the audition process.”

Thangavel and his co-captain are part of the audition process, working through the logistics.

“Performances that we pick and line up, we want to make sure that it can help support the dancers the best they can,” Thangavel said. “Stuff like high-energy performances, and whatever they need.”

When deciding on performers, Thangavel chose people who could keep the crowd captivated.

“Definitely high energy, things that people want to watch,” Thangavel said. “A cool dance team could be really cool to watch on stage, and it just does it to get the crowd's attention.”

Similarly, Senior Production Liaison and Livestream Logistics Captain Emily Galiano said keeping energy high is a priority.

“It's awesome because we're able to get these organizations on the screen, and obviously once they see themselves on the screen, they get all excited and hyped up,” Galiano, a fourth-year studying advertising and public relations, said. “It's really great just being able to draw attention to all the hard work they're also putting in in the stands.”

Galiano, being in charge of THON livestream, said the committee put a larger emphasis on the livestream this year — during the National Act, around 2,000 people tuned in.

“(We’re) just kind of putting more time into the curation of the timeline for it,” Galiano said. “Making it more interesting to people at home, including packages, some interviews of THON volunteers, dancers, families.”

The Entertainment Committee wants to ensure performers, activities and on-stage content is in line with THON’s mission, according to Galiano.

“Anything that we know will appeal to not only the families and children, but also to the dancers, is great,” Galiano said.

MORE THON COVERAGE

title: Penn State mic-man Brady Berger dances in THON for second year in a row ‘so kids can be kids’
author: Mia Debelevich | News Editor
description:
For the second year in a row, Brady Berger decided to undertake the 46-hour feat of dancing in THON — a decision he described as a no-brainer.

Berger, a fourth-year studying supply chain and information systems, is dancing on behalf of Penn State Cheerleading. He also serves as Penn State’s mic-man for athletic events. He described his organization as supportive in his journey to align with THON’s mission: finding a cure.

“I dance so kids can be kids, so they can wake up happy and healthy,” Berger said. “There’s not enough good things to say about THON.”

Berger said he also dances for his mom, who was diagnosed with breast cancer.

“THON is very special to me,” Berger said. “The cause, the tireless hours, how we can be a small part in something so big.”

Sebastian Goldstein, one of Berger’s teammates, described him as a smart leader who maintains high morale on the team.

“I’m very proud of him,” Goldstein, a second-year studying agricultural engineering, said. “He’s a person that puts his heart out there, especially for the kids.”

Goldstein described Berger as a very passionate person, both within cheerleading and in his commitment to THON. He said Berger always pushes the people around him to live up to their full potential.

A big part of Berger’s goal at Penn State is to be as much a part of THON as he can, Goldstein added.

“Everything he does is FTK,” Goldstein said. “He’s buzzed his head, he’s dancing for the second time. He’s really shown his dedication and love for the organization.”

Penn State Cheerleading is representing four families at THON, including the Juniper Beckinger family, who is in attendance this year. Berger said families like Juniper’s bring light to THON and the cheerleading team’s collective goal.

Another teammate Allie McNesby said Berger’s role in THON and as mic-man shows how much effort he puts into his responsibilities at Penn State.

“He’s a role model for the school, but also for the friendships he has here,” McNesby, a first-year student in the division of undergraduate studies, said. “That has meant so much to us this year.”

McNesby said everyone on the cheerleading team finds Berger’s work this year amazing. She said the team being in the stands is one way to support both Berger as a dancer and THON as a whole.

“We’re just so proud of him,” McNesby said. “We just can’t wait for him to do this for 46 hours, for THON.”

Berger said he thanks Four Diamonds families for letting him and all dancers into their lives and journeys.

“There’s nothing like this at any other university,” Berger said. “We will always dance until there’s a cure.”

MORE THON COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's hockey's Gavin McKenna feeds off crowd, hits McGregor celebration in historic night
author: Chase Fisher | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State superstar forward Gavin McKenna said to his parents over a phone call on Thursday night, “If I score, I’m doing it.”

What McKenna was referring to was the UFC fighter Conor McGregor-inspired celebration he performed after his goal in the first period on Friday against Ohio State. McKenna had that strut in his pocket for last weekend’s road series against No. 2 Michigan, but was finally able to use it after he scored against the Buckeyes.

“Yeah, (I was) just emotional,” McKenna said. “I was just having fun out there. A lot of stuff going on in my life right now, so being out in the rink, being with the guys, that's my happiness. So, I just thought I’d show some personality.”

McKenna made his first trip back to Pegula Ice Arena a memorable one on Friday night, appearing in front of his home crowd for the first time since he was charged with two counts of assault after an alleged altercation on Jan. 31 following the Beaver Stadium outdoor game.

The freshman posted eight points, seven assists and one goal, destroying the single-game program records for assists — a five-way tie with four — and points — a three-way tie with five. His seven assists were also a Big Ten record.

“It feels good,” McKenna said. “Wherever you go, you want to leave your mark. And I think tonight was a pretty good game. I thought just coming here, this program's got a lot of history. Obviously, it's pretty new, but the stuff they've done to get to this point and bring in these high-end players, it's pretty special, and I'm glad to be a part of it.”

Of his seven apples, five were primary assists and two were secondary. And some of them stemmed from aggressiveness, as his seven shots were tied for the second most he’s totaled in a game all season, with a few of them being redirected for goals.

“Yeah. I mean, it's unbelievable,” Fink said. “Obviously, putting up eight points is ridiculous in a game, especially college hockey. So I'm proud of him. He's playing with heart. He's playing his game. He's shooting the puck. And, yeah, it's awesome playing alongside him.”

McKenna’s celebration wasn’t the only antics of the night. Penn State’s student section, known as The Roar Zone, took a chant used by fans of McGregor and altered it to fit McKenna.

The chant went: “There’s only one Gavin McKenna. There’s only one Gavin McKenna. He’s coming for you, either a five or a two, walking in McKenna Wonderland.”

That wasn’t the only one, with students singing “We want Dustin,” a reference to McGregor’s arch rival in the UFC, Dustin Poirier.

McKenna, with a smirk, said he “didn’t really hear” the chants throughout the night, as he’s “just dialed in” during the game.

“It’s always a fun crowd to play in front of,” McKenna said.

McKenna mentioned postgame that the team has been a good support group for him. He said that being on the ice with his teammates has been a nice “getaway” from dealing with all the non-hockey matters.

“Yeah, I think it's a great group to be a part of, very supportive, always there if I need it — we've got great leadership,” McKenna said. “I got guys like Dane (Dowiak), Fink. Following all those guys to lean on through this whole process. I've got the whole staff. All of Penn State, honestly, has been amazing to me. So, I'm so glad I came here, and a lot of thanks to these guys.”

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: ‘She's so full of life’ | Thiara Sandoval Yonfa fulfills freshman year dream to dance
author: Emma Naysmith | The Daily Collegian
description:
THON 2026 dancer, Thiara Sandoval Yonfa first learned about THON’s mission when she arrived at Penn State from her home country, Ecuador. She said the dance organization Volé Dance Company introduced her to the weekend’s efforts.

“When I learned about THON at Penn State, it really gave me an opportunity to allow kids to be kids, and to see that no kid has to suffer this way,” Sandoval Yonfa, a third-year studying architecture said.

She had the opportunity to dance with Volé during THON Weekend 2025, but this year she’s standing for longer and with a different group — she’s one of two dancers representing American Institute of Architecture Students (AIAS).

Sandoval Yonfa said she THONs for her mom’s friends battling cancer.

“As I grew older, my mom's friends started getting diagnosed with different sorts of cancer,” Sandoval Yonfa said. “Seeing how it affected her, her friends and their families really put things into perspective.

Knowing she wanted to dance since freshman year, Sandoval Yonfa said receiving the dancer confirmation email felt “great.”

Weekend highlights for Sandoval Yonfa so far have included the silent disco where she had the opportunity to dance her heart out, a Roots meal that revived her and seeing friends around the Bryce Jordan Center.

Dana Sanchez met Sandoval Yonfa in class their first year and said they “instantly clicked” over both being from Latin America and speaking Spanish.

Now her roommate, Sanchez, a third-year, studying biotechnology, said she’s very proud of Sandoval Yonfa’s heart, strength and spirit. Sanchez said visiting Sandoval Yonfa on the THON floor was a bonding moment she’ll never forget.

“She teaches me something every single day,” Sanchez said. “What she has taught me the most, is to chase your dreams and do what you do with purpose.”

When energy gets low throughout the weekend, Sandoval Yonfa said everything from a shared look, hug or even just a wave from friends inspires her to continue standing.

Dancer Relations Committee member and friend of Sandoval Yonfa and Sanchez, Ariana Aray, said she’s bonded with the pair through also being Latina.

“I always grew up between two cultures … with speaking Spanish with my family at home and then having my American friends,” Aray, a second-year studying health policy and administration, said. “It's been really great to have friends who speak Spanish and understand that part of me.”

Aray said she knows Sandoval Yonfa can “put her mind to anything she wants to do.”

Nicole Langan, one of Sandoval Yonfa’s DRCMs, said she was immediately impressed by her passionate approach to the weekend.

“She's so full of life … talks to everybody and is so social,” Langan, a third-year studying accounting and finance, said. “That just influences everybody, especially in this setting where you're meant to come together.”

In this experience and her time at Penn State, Sandoval Yonfa said while physically distanced from family, she still feels their presence.

“I know they're always in my heart, they're always in my mind and I feel their energy around me … and I have a new family here at Penn State,” Sandoval Yonfa said. “It's like leaving home to go to another home, changing a language to speak another language.”

Sandoval Yonfa said she wants to remind others to “Keep fighting for what you want, represent your community, your culture and your country, love always leads forward.”

MORE THONTENT

title: ‘It’s gone really quick’ | Levi Haines reflects on illustrious Penn State career on Senior Night
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
As Levi Haines’ name was announced during Senior Night festivities, the packed Rec Hall crowd rose to their feet for the first time of the night.

Haines, joined by a multitude of family members, received his flowers before the dual as the lone starter recognized on Senior Night.

“Obviously, not a guy you’re excited to graduate, just from a leadership standpoint, you’re excited for him in every stage of life,” Cael Sanderson said on Wednesday.

Haines’ Penn State career began earlier than expected, forgoing the final part of his senior season at Biglerville High School to compete in collegiate opens as a gray shirt.

That prepared him for a freshman season in 2022-23 most wrestlers would dream of. Starting at 157 pounds, Haines rolled to a 25-2 record, earning a Big Ten title and coming in second to North Carolina’s Austin O’Connell in the National Championship match.

He continued his momentum into his sophomore season, maintaining an undefeated record throughout to win both a Big Ten title and a national title. He made the jump to 174 pounds and just like his freshman season, dropped two matches while winning a Big Ten title, this time coming in third place at nationals.

He made the same jump competition wise from his freshman to sophomore season, as he did in his junior to senior season, where he currently sits as the No. 1 wrestler at 174 pounds and an undefeated record.

Just by a snap of the finger, Haines competed and won his last dual match of his Penn State career on Friday against Princeton, one that’ll always be remembered.

“It’s gone really quick,” Haines said. “When you’re always trying to pursue mastering yourself or mastering your craft, time kind of goes quick because you’re just focused on the task at hand, and time just seems to slip by really quick when you’re doing that.”

When you have an illustrious career like Haines, who holds an 81-4 record across four seasons, it’s hard to pick out a favorite moment, but one moment stood out from the rest.

“My memory is last year, when we had a bunch of guys wrestle back to get third place,” Haines said of the NCAA Tournament. “It was pretty special. It’s not the easiest thing to do, and I’m really proud of our guys and how they showed up and showed up for the team. Just weren’t thinking for themselves… they went out the next best thing and scored a lot of team points on the backside.”

Haines’ answer is a reason he was selected a captain of what could be another historic team. It’s also the reason why Sanderson emphasized not “letting a guy like that leave.”

The Arendtsville, Pennsylvania, native seemingly adapted to Penn State pretty quickly, allowing himself to grow from his high school days. It’s prepared Haines for the biggest stages, ones that he’s already competed on, and ones that he’ll compete on in the future.

“I’ve grown the most in being myself in the bigger moments,” Haines said. “I feel like when I was younger, sometimes I let the outside environment get to me a little bit, and being here, surrounded by the right people has kind of helped me open my eyes and be comfortable being myself in those big moments.”

Haines’ received another standing ovation, and the loudest cheer of the night as he was introduced prior to his match against Princeton’s Cody Tavoso. His work on the mat and off the mat has earned the respect of the Penn State faithful, and he finished off his Penn State dual career in the way he wanted most — a pinfall.

“I always want to pin everybody,” Haines said.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Cael Sanderson confirms Penn State’s Aaron Nagao will medically retire from wrestling
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
Ahead of Penn State capping off its final dual of the regular season against Princeton with a 50-0 win, there was a surprising addition to the Senior Night festivities.

Aaron Nagao, who was the starter at 141 pounds prior to suffering a season-ending shoulder injury, joined Levi Haines and Brian Borden to partake in the ceremony.

Shortly after Nagao and his teammates walked to the mat alongside their families to take a photo with Cael Sanderson, Penn State Sports Networker Jeff Byers, the radio voice of Nittany Lions’ wrestling, revealed Nagao would be medically retiring. Sanderson confirmed the news at his press conference after the dual.

“He's been very special to our program. Just a wonderful kid,” Sanderson said. “Just classy, happy, grateful — obviously, a tremendous competitor. Unfortunately, got the injury bug the last couple years.”

Nagao returned to the mat this year after nearly two years removed from competing due to a previous long-term injury.

Before the season started, he expressed excitement about making his return to wearing a blue and white singlet.

“I am excited. I haven’t wrestled — it’s been almost two years since I’ve competed," Nagao said in November. “Just hopefully shake the dust off, if there is any, glorify God and let the hard work show.”

He got off to a hot start, but then suffered an injury at the Black Knight Invitational in November. Nagao returned to the mat in December for the Southern Scuffle, where he won his first match.

But then he didn’t return to action, having to medically forfeit his next matchup. In the middle of January, Sanderson revealed Nagao’s fate for the 2025-26 campaign, saying “he’s definitely done for the season.”

“The reality of what Aaron was coming back from, just an uphill battle,” Sanderson said in January. “Shoulders are really challenging in our sport. It’s complicated, obviously, joint positions you get put in wrestling, but if anyone could do it, it was Aaron. So, we want to give him the best support and opportunity we could give him.”

The La Habra, California, native finished his final season of wrestling with a 5-2 record, and a 51-18 career showing with five seasons between Penn State and Minnesota.

Nagao finished his collegiate wrestling career with another year of eligibility remaining, and will likely remain with the team heading into the postseason.

While his days of competing on the mat are done, Sanderson expressed appreciation for who he is as a person and a wrestler, indicating he has a bright future ahead in whatever he chooses to pursue.

“Just a high-character kid. Obviously, really good at playing a lot of positions and helped lift everyone on the team,” Sanderson said. “He’s a kid that’s gonna be really successful in his life. We’ll certainly miss him. Super grateful he came to Penn State.”

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball defeats No. 25 UCF in dramatic fashion, earns first ranked victory of 2026
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State had its first big test of the season against No. 25 UCF in the second game of Friday’s doubleheader at the Space U Classic in Orlando. The Nittany Lions looked to stay hot and extend their five-game winning streak while the Knights searched for their third straight victory.

The Nittany Lions held on late despite a last-minute comeback and picked up their sixth straight win behind their hot offense, 6-5.

It wouldn’t be easy for the blue and white to carry its offensive momentum into this contest with UCF’s ace Isabella Vega on the mound. Coach Clarisa Crowell brought a different approach, starting freshman Brevyn Kellepouris.

Despite a one-out walk and a hit-by-pitch, Penn State didn’t plate any runs in the first, and then were quickly retired in order the next inning. However, UCF’s offense also started slow as Kellepouris allowed no base runners through the first two innings.

The Nittany Lions broke the scoreless tie in the third with a little help from the Knights’ defense. Shortstop Kaylie Walters drew a one-out walk, and center fielder Natalie Lieto advanced her to second with a groundout.

With two outs, the blue and white’s hottest hitter had a chance to drive in Walters. Third baseman Allison Oneacre lifted a short routine fly ball to left, but Carley Jo Penner bobbled the out and Walters scored.

Kellepouris held the 1-0 lead later in the inning with clutch pitching. Kendall Yarnell hammered a one-out double to center, but the freshman retired the next two batters with a strikeout as the third out.

While the Knights’ offense started slow, Penn State added to its lead in the fourth. Designated player Brooke Klosowicz smothered a leadoff home run over the left field wall, making it 2-0. The blue and white left a few more runners stranded, but Kellepouris delivered another shutdown inning.

The Nittany Lions went down quietly in their half of the fifth, but the Knights put on pressure. Zoe Calvez singled into left center for just the second UCF hit of the night. With a little momentum, Kendall Yarnell followed with a single of her own to left.

With just one out, Kellepouris now faced Reagan Vokoun in a big position late in the game. The left-handed pitcher kept her composure and Vokoun grounded into an inning-ending double play.

The blue and white struck again in the sixth off second baseman Michela Barbanente’s first home run of the campaign. The junior’s solo dinger to center gave Penn State a much-needed insurance run late with its lead at 3-0.

Crowell turned to her bullpen after Kellepouris recorded her third strikeout for the first out in the bottom half of the sixth, but the freshman delivered her best outing of the season. The Olathe, Kansas, native allowed just three hits and one walk over 5.1 innings against a tough UCF offense.

Penn State played small ball in the seventh and plated another insurance run. Left fielder Cara Bohner led off with a double that split the gap in right center. Walters advanced pinch runner Kalli Karwowski with a bunt, and Lieto knocked her in with a groundout.

However, the Knights couldn’t stop the bleeding with two outs. Oneacre walked and first baseman Kirsten Finarelli knocked a single to center, and right fielder Calvez muffed the ball, which allowed another run to score.

With Finarelli on third off the error, UCF turned to its bullpen and brought in Hildie Dempsey. However, Klosowicz powered a RBI double all the way to the wall, extending Penn State’s lead to 5-0.

The Knights avoided the shutout in their last breath and mounted a strong comeback. Sophomore Abigail Britton attempted to close the game out, but surrendered three runs in the seventh and Crowell brought in junior Bridget Nemeth with two outs.

However, the Glendora, California, native walked the first batter she faced and suddenly the Knights had the tying run at the plate. Nemeth couldn’t find her control, and Crowell brought Kellepouris back into the game to face Beth Damon with the bases loaded.

Damon delivered with a clutch single to center field, scoring two runs and suddenly the Knights were within one at 6-5. Despite all the drama, Kellepouris came through and closed the game with her fourth strikeout against Ashleigh Griffin.

Up Next

The Nittany Lions look to stay hot at the Space U Classic against Ohio at 11:30 a.m. Saturday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Gavin McKenna tallies eight points as No. 6 Penn State defeats Ohio State
author: Greg Finberg | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 6 Penn State entered its recent stretch eager to show it could hang with the best.

The Nittany Lions left that gauntlet, which featured consecutive series against No. 1 Michigan State and No. 2 Michigan, with zero wins. But they took the Spartans to overtime and the Wolverines to a shootout — performances that proved their worth in a stacked Big Ten conference.

That confidence was showcased in the Nittany Lions’ return to Pegula Ice Arena on Friday. Gavin McKenna tallied a career-best eight points as his squad used an offensive barrage to defeat Ohio State, 11-4.

McKenna’s seven assists and eight points set Penn State single-game records, far outpacing the previous record. Penn State’s 11 goals tied a program record, which was set in 2018 against Robert Morris.

“(McKenna) loves hockey. He just loves being on the ice. He just has a blast,” Guy Gadowsky said. “How he thinks the game, sees the game is just different. But if you watch what he does with the backhand side of the stick, I've never seen anything like that. It's like he has two forehands. He can really fire that puck, too.”

Ohio State controlled early possession, but it was Penn State that earned the first real chance of the contest. After Carter Schade drew an interference penalty, Jackson Smith connected with McKenna, who patiently skated near the blue line before finding an awaiting Aiden Fink at the faceoff circle.

The junior forward ripped a one-timed slap shot short side and past Kristoffer Eberly for an early 1-0 lead. And the Nittany Lions’ power play wasn’t done there.

BANG, BANG!#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/ibp24sEv4X— Penn State Men’s Hockey (@PennStateMHKY) February 21, 2026

After another Buckeyes penalty, it was McKenna’s turn to light the lamp. The freshman forward skated atop the slot before his wrist shot trickled through Eberly’s five-hole to double Penn State’s advantage.

His celebration: Conor McGregor’s iconic Billionaire Strut.

“Just emotional. I was just having fun out there. There’s a lot of stuff going on in my life right now. So being out in the rink, being with the guys, that's my happiness. So I chose to show some personality.”

Decent Celly 🤷‍♂️#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/Ki9vhpSagA— Penn State Men’s Hockey (@PennStateMHKY) February 21, 2026

Josh Fleming joined the party minutes later with what could be the save of the season. Davis Burnside thought he had a surefire goal as he stared down an empty net, but Fleming dove across and used his outstretched glove to deny Ohio State’s captain.

DA NA NA, DA NA NA!!#SCTop10 pic.twitter.com/i7CFxzxx5a— Penn State Men’s Hockey (@PennStateMHKY) February 21, 2026

Fleming finished with 38 saves on 42 shots — a performance that drew Fink’s praise.

“My mind was blown,” Fink said of Fleming’s save. “The way he dove back and made that save sparked an energy on the bench. He's an amazing goalie. He always keeps us in games, and he played fantastic tonight … We're glad he plays for Penn State.”

Felix Caron finally broke through with a power-play tally to pull one back for the Buckeyes before the first intermission. The first period was full of scoring chances, but it was no match for the second period, which featured a combined eight goals.

McKenna’s big night continued when Penn State returned to the man advantage in the second period. The freshman’s slap shot was redirected by Matt DiMarsico, whose 16th goal of the season gave the Nittany Lions a two-goal cushion.

Off the shoulder and in!!#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/pDCqltkxtW— Penn State Men’s Hockey (@PennStateMHKY) February 21, 2026

Penn State’s offensive barrage continued one minute later, this time with DiMarsico on the assisting end. The junior forward skated behind the net before firing a cross-crease feed to JJ Wiebusch for a tap-in goal.

JJ ALL DAY!!#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/2czNT27X6d— Penn State Men’s Hockey (@PennStateMHKY) February 21, 2026

Wiebusch’s tally marked his 14th of the campaign. But it also opened the scoring floodgates, with four goals following in the next four minutes of game time.

First, it was Caron who buried his second goal of the contest to pull one back for the Buckeyes before Shea Van Olm wasted no time pulling that momentum back with an immediate answer.

McKenna padded that cushion with his fifth point and fourth assist of the contest, a crossing feed to a wide-open Luke Misa, who scored his 6th goal of the season.

“Misa is a really good player, a young player,” Fink said. “He's really fast. He can get those boots moving every period. He brings intensity, quickness and also smarts. I think there were a couple of plays today that he made that were very smart. He's going to be a driver for our line.”

Burnside capped the scoring burst with a breakaway goal — Ohio State’s third tally past Fleming’s outstretched glove.

A seven-minute goal drought allowed the Pegula faithful to re-energize for another outburst, which none other than McKenna provided with his sixth point across the first two periods. The potential No. 1 pick in the 2026 NHL draft showcased his elite vision with a saucer pass to a streaking Van Olm, who buried his second goal of the period.

ICE TUDDYYYYYYYYYYY!!#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/519n9zRVff— Penn State Men’s Hockey (@PennStateMHKY) February 21, 2026

Guy Gadowsky lauded Reese Laubach’s drive following a four-point series in Ann Arbor last weekend. The San Jose Sharks prospect showcased that hustle with a gritty backcheck, which created a two-on-one break that led to DiMarsico’s second goal of the game to close a crazy second period.

Dirty Dangles!!#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/diNtoObcbS— Penn State Men’s Hockey (@PennStateMHKY) February 21, 2026

Penn State had scored so many goals that when Misa buried his second tally of the contest, neither he nor his linemates bothered skating to the bench to fist bump their teammates — a diversion from the tradition that follows nearly all of the team’s goals.

Smith and Laubach added late goals as the Nittany Lions ended their winless streak with a dominant win to improve to 19-9-1.

Injury Report

Nick Fascia limped off the ice in the third period and didn’t return. Gadowsky said Fascia got a “bad cut” on his leg.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: THON 2026 Line Dance (With Lyrics)
author: Zeyda Frye | Multimedia Editor
description:
With the start of THON Weekend 2026 also comes the release of the highly anticipated Line Dance.

Dancer Relations captains took to the Bryce Jordan Center stage shortly after the start of THON to perform the weekend’s first Line Dance.

The Line Dance is an annual recap created by THON and will be performed during each of the 46 hours to keep dancers motivated and energy high throughout the BJC.

MORE COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling shuts out Princeton on Senior Night to wrap up regular season
author: AJ Piazza | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State returned to Rec Hall for its last match of the regular season against Princeton. The Nittany Lions took care of business with a 50-0 victory over the Tigers.

Penn State had three pins in this match and were in control throughout.

125 Pounds: No. 1 Luke Lilledahl (PSU) vs No. 12 Marc-Anthony McGowan (PU)

Luke Lilledahl started the match trying to get to Marc-Anthony McGowan. McGowan was taped up and used a similar technique as Nebraska’s 184-pounder Angelo Ferrari, continuously dropping to one knee. McGowan was issued with stalling. To end the first period, McGowan gave Lilledahl a point with another stalling call.

McGowan escaped immediately and continued to stall, giving Lilledahl another point. McGowan got another stalling call, giving Lilledahl a 4-1 lead. Another stall point was awarded for Lilledahl, giving the sophomore a 5-1 lead.

Another minute, another stalling as Lilledahl led 7-1. McGowan stalled out, giving Penn State a 6-0 lead.

133 Pounds: No. 2 Marcus Blaze (PSU) vs No. 32 Ethan Rivera (PU)

Marcus Blaze secured the first takedown of the night, as he grabbed Ethan Rivera’s leg and got him down on the mat. Rivera was more aggressive than McGowan, but was unable to get any offense going.

Blaze got an escape in the second period, and a takedown after to take a 7-2 lead. Blaze got another takedown after dragging Rivera by the leg into the center of the mat. The freshman got the major-decision victory, 11-3.

Penn State led 10-0.

141 Pounds: No. 12 Braeden Davis (PSU) vs Matthew Martino (PU)

Braeden Davis was aggressive like he has been in his last few matches. The junior got two takedowns in the first period and was all over the mat.

Matthew Martino was the only wrestler to record a takedown in the middle frame. Martino was the more aggressive wrestler in the second period, but Davis led 8-4 because of an escape point.

Martino threw Davis off the match a couple times, but Davis didn’t let it affect him and got his third takedown of the match. Davis got away with an 11-8 decision victory.

Penn State led 13-0.

149 Pounds: No. 1 Shayne Van Ness (PSU) vs No. 14 Eligh Rivera (PU)

Shayne Van Ness made Eligh Rivera fly. Van Ness got a takedown, and was in control until Rivera got a takedown of his own and took a 4-3 lead. Van Ness answered back with a toe-tapper takedown, keeping the toe in while Rivera was off the mat. Van Ness led 7-4.

The junior got his third takedown of the match, but was looking for the bow-and-arrow and couldn’t get it. Van Ness continued to be on top of Rivera and Rivera started stalling.

Van Ness got the major-decision victory, 15-6, with the riding time point

Penn State led 17-0.

157 Pounds: No. 4 PJ Duke (PSU) vs Gavin Hawk

In the first 30 seconds, PJ Duke got his first takedown and then added another 30 seconds later after hooking Gavin Hawk’s leg and dropping him to the mat. Duke got his third takedown and held a 9-2 lead with over a minute left in the opening period.

The freshman continued to hook his leg and take Hawk down. Duke led 12-4 going into the middle frame, getting his fifth takedown of the match.

It looked like Hawk caught Duke, but he didn’t, as Duke got his sixth takedown of the match and it became a cycle. Duke got another takedown, would let Hawk up, and would take him down again.

Duke with eight takedowns and gave Penn State a 22-0 lead.

165 Pounds: No. 1 Mitchell Mesenbrink (PSU) vs No. 19 Ty Whalen (PU)

After back-and-forth hand fighting, Mitchell Mesenbrink got the first takedown of the night.

Mesenbrink shortly after got another takedown, then pinning Whalen.

Penn State led 28-0.

174 Pounds: No. 1 Levi Haines (PSU) vs Cody Tavoso (PU)

Levi Haines went right to work with two early takedowns in the first period.

The senior led 6-1 after the first frame and added to his lead with a reversal and a takedown and got the pin.

Penn State led 34-0.

184 Pounds: No. 1 Rocco Welsh (PSU) vs Xavier Giles (PU)

Rocco Welsh copied the Duke cycle. He took Giles down, let him back up, and took him down again. Welsh had four takedowns and had a 12-3 lead after the first period.

In the second period, the Ohio State transfer added his fifth and sixth takedown of the match. Welsh barely let Giles get any space when he was on his feet and got the tech-fall victory after his seventh takedown.

Penn State led 39-0.

197 Pounds: No. 1 Josh Barr (PSU) vs Conor McCloskey (PU)

The match began — 10 seconds later, Josh Barr got a takedown.

Barr got Connor McCloskey down on the mat and got the pin, making it three pins in four matches for Penn State.

The Nittany Lions led 45-0.

285 Pounds: No. 6 Cole Mirasola (PSU) vs Sebastian Garibaldi (PU)

Cole Mirasola from downtown got the takedown against Sebastian Garibaldi. Mirasola continued to pump the gas, netting two more takedowns to take a 9-3 lead. The redshirt freshman continued to be on the attack with five takedowns in the first period, recording a 15-4 lead.

Mirasola continued to get Garibaldi on his back, as he got two more takedowns and a tech-fall victory, 21-6.

Penn State wins 50-0.

title: Penn State men’s volleyball opens home series against Harvard with sweep
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
After defeating conference foe and typical Ivy League squad, Princeton, over a two-match series, the Nittany Lions returned to Happy Valley from New Jersey for a clash in the Rec Hall, South Gym. Coincidentally, the wrestling team faced off against the Tigers in the main venue at the same time.

The similarities continued, with another typical Ivy League squad, Harvard, stepping in against the Nittany Lions on the court, and the fortunate stroke of serendipity held true throughout the game, as the blue and white cruised to a three-set sweep.

Despite not having the luxuries of playing in Rec Hall’s main stage, Penn State sent out its star-studded first unit of Tristan Hassell, Ryan Merk, Jaidyn Bethel, Gage Gabriel, Michael Schwob, Sean Harvey and Owen Rose.

The Crimson, who entered 2-7 and winless in the EIVA after their first two outings, started relatively well and matched the Penn State group’s pace. The first 14 points of the first set were split between the two squads. A service error and attack error by the Crimson, along with a Schwob block, however, allowed the blue and white to find momentum from there.

Harvey committed an attack error, but Rose immediately got the point back with a kill, and four straight Penn State blocks followed to inflate its lead to 15-8 – despite a Harvard timeout. The energy provided by the blue and white defense kick-started something special.

Schwob logged an ace and assisted Harvey on a kill, forcing the Crimson to call another timeout. After the break, the Crimson attempted to display that they had figured things out, with back-to-back kills. But instead, it awakened the blue and white, who went on a seven-point stretch to force set point.

The points came primarily via Harvey, who notched three aces until Harvard scored another point. The first set soon went Penn State’s way, as Harvey anchored the offense with four kills and those triad of aces, along with Rose on the defensive side, who posted six total blocks.

The second set resembled the first, with the first 20 points of the set split evenly between the two units until a fifth Harvey ace of the night put the blue and white ahead. Although this time it wasn’t enough to snag sole momentum for Penn State, as the two teams traded the next few points until Hassell joined in on the scoring fun with an ace of his own.

With the slight upper hand, 15-13, it looked as if Penn State’s offense would shut down a second set, until the Crimson’s attackers stole that spotlight. Harvard outside hitter, Sawyer Nichols, smashed a pair of aces amid a four-point scoring run that gave it the lead.

With a three-point lead, it seemed as if Harvard would control the set late, yet Penn State got things level with a Hassell kill and a couple of Gabriel aces to tie things up at 19, at which point the Crimson called another timeout.

Things continued to go back and forth, and even with a Schwob ace, it went into bonus points. Eventually, with the score tied up at 25 a piece, Harvard made a pair of blunders on an attack and serve to give the Nittany Lions the set.

The third set started with a slight blue and white edge, but quickly logjammed. However, the defense locked back in and allowed Penn State to cruise to an 11-6 lead courtesy of a triad of blocks. Gabriel, shortly after, logged another ace to force a Harvard timeout.

The break didn’t suit the Crimson much better, as points continued to fall in Penn State’s favor courtesy of strong frontline play. Up big, 22-13, the Nittany Lions just had to keep it clean to secure the sweep. Schwob shortly got a kill to force match point, and then helped assist Rose on the kill to secure the win.

With the result, the blue and white improved to 9-4 and 3-0in conference play.

Up Next

Penn State continues its series with Harvard at 3 p.m. on Saturday, and this time the Nittany Lions will be inside the main Rec Hall court.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball takes down Charleston Southern in first game of SpaceU Classic
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a trip to the Lone Star state, Penn State returned to Florida for the Space U Classic in Orlando. The Nittany Lions opened play on Friday afternoon against Charleston Southern.

In the first of two meetings between the teams this weekend, the blue and white defeated the Buccaneers 10-3.

Penn State stepped up to the plate first, facing right-hander Gianna DeLuco. After centerfielder Natalie Lieto led off with a groundout, second baseman and reigning National Freshman of the Week, Allison Oneacre, knocked a double to the right side.

First baseman Kirsten Finarelli mirrored with a double of her own, bringing in her classmate to give the Nittany Lions an early 1-0 lead.

Right-hander McKenna Young got the nod for Penn State in the bottom of the frame. Center fielder Blakely Kingsmore led off with a single and quickly stole second base.

With two runners on and one out, catcher Emma Smith singled up the middle, driving in Kingsmore. Charleston Southern took the lead a batter later after shortstop Kaylie Walters fumbled a ground ball.

The Buccaneers added on another run with a single to left field from third baseman Charlee Yourman.

Following Young’s second walk of the inning, Charleston Southern loaded up the bases with two outs. The Nittany Lions got out of the jam, but the Buccaneers ended the first inning with a 3-1 lead.

A one-out single from right fielder Breanna Hanik and a walk to Walters turned things back over to the top of the order for Penn State.

Lieto flew out to center, but Hanik managed to advance to third, and was brought home by Oneacre, getting the Nittany Lions a run back heading into the bottom of the second inning.

Coach Clarisa Crowell made a call to the bullpen early, bringing in right-hander Mackenzie Duncan to replace Young. The freshman allowed Kingsmore to reach on a bunt, but no further damage was done by Charleston Southern.

To start the third inning, third baseman Brooke Klosowicz singled to the left side. Following Klosowicz, left fielder Michela Barbenente reached on an error, and designated player Macy Chamberlin got on via a walk.

Catcher Madson Seidel took advantage of the bases-loaded situation and doubled down the right field line, bringing in two runs.

The Buccaneers turned to the bullpen, bringing in right-hander Brenna Conley. Hanik jumped on the new arm quickly, singling up the middle to bring in two more runs.

An infield single from Walters and a hit by pitch from Lieto loaded up the bases once again, still with no outs. Oneacre stepped up to the plate and delivered two more runs, extending the Penn State lead to 8-3.

The Nittany Lions couldn’t tack on any more runs, but a six-run inning gave the blue and white a cushion heading into the fourth frame.

Designated player Emily Chandler led off the inning with a walk, but Charleston Southern didn’t put any more on as Duncan recorded a two-strikeout frame.

The Buccaneers recorded two quick outs, but Conley allowed free bases to Walters and Lieto, bringing up Oneacre with two runners on. Charleston Southern avoided any damage as Oneacre flew out to center field.

With one out in the fourth, Kingsmore reached on a throwing error from Duncan, but was caught stealing by Seidel a pitch later.

The Buccaneers got two runners on, but they couldn’t find their way home as Penn State held a comfortable five-run lead heading into the fifth.

Barbenente stepped to the plate with one out and a runner on first. The junior delivered a double off the left field fence, putting runners in scoring position for Chamberlin.

Chamberlain grounded out to Conley but recorded an RBI. The Nittany Lions added on another run with a double from Seidel.

Seidel couldn't be brought around, but Penn State held a 10-3 lead heading into the bottom of the frame.

Duncan and the Penn State defense left the fifth inning unfazed, allowing just one runner via a hit by pitch.

Charleston Southern recorded two quick outs, but a double from Oneacre, a free base to Finarelli and a single from Klosowicz loaded up the bases for Barbenente.

Yourman made a tough play at the hot corner, preventing the threatening Nittany Lions from bringing in any runs.

Penn State had another strong defensive outing as the Buccaneers went three-up, three-down. The blue and white held a commanding seven-run lead heading into the final frame.

The Nittany Lions went down in order in their final at-bat, but Charleston Southern had a lot of work to do in the bottom of the seventh.

Duncan put the cherry on top for Penn State, striking out the side to end the game and finished with eight strikeouts on the day.

Up Next

Penn State returns to action at 7 p.m. Friday to face host team UCF.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: ‘We’re not going away’ | Coalition of Graduate Employees demonstrates for BoT to drop PLRB appeal
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Coalition of Graduate Employees (CGE) held a demonstration on Friday to protest an appeal filed by Penn State to the Pennsylvania Labor Relations Board (PLRB).

The CGE voted to formally unionize on Nov. 13, 2025. Penn State’s appeal was filed a month later, challenging the inclusion of research assistants (RAs) into the union. The CGE currently has an online petition calling for Penn State to drop the appeal and The Board of Trustees (BoT) received at least 25 public comments calling for it to be formally recognized.

Sangjun Ko, an organizer for CGE and a member of its bargaining committee, said Penn State’s appeal is arguing that RAs are not official workers — and thus unable to unionize — despite a 2018 decision from the PLRB stating otherwise.

“They're trying to carve out the union,” Ko, a graduate student studying mathematics, said. “This rally is intended to demand out of to the Board of Trustees that they respect the vote, they respect our democratic voice and they respect this decision handed down by the labor relations board.”

The demonstrators began to move from a staging area by the Moore Building toward the Nittany Lion Inn at 1 p.m. Protestors held signs that read “Drop the appeal,” “Respect the vote” and “Research is labor.”

Upon reaching their destination, demonstrators were led through chants such as “Stop the delay, give us our pay,” “Respect our vote, drop the appeal” and “What's disgusting? Union busting.”

Kiana Mitchell said it’s important to inform people about the union process, as the appeal by Penn State delays the CGE’s ability to formally unionize.

“We already voted,” Mitchell, a graduate student studying genetics and reproduction in the department of animal sciences, said. “We could already have the union as it is, as it stands, but Penn State is trying to delay our process with bargaining.”

Mitchell said she hopes Penn State will realize the CGE is not trying to have an adversarial relationship with them. Their combined efforts would allow for improvements to be made for graduate students at the university, she added.

The organizers of the demonstration proceeded toward the Kern Building, once the larger group dispersed, to encourage other graduate students to sign their petition to Penn State.

Eloisa Sanchez said he believed Penn State’s refusal to recognize RAs as workers invalidates their contributions to the university and hopes that the current demonstration will cause Penn State to begin to see the CGE “as a union”.

“We put in a lot of time and effort and a lot of dedication to doing our work and, by not respecting research assistance as workers, Penn State is also not respecting our energy, our time, our dedication to our work,” Sanchez, a graduate student studying curriculum and instruction, said.

Sanchez said he can see future demonstrations taking place if the BoT does not meet the demands of its graduate students in having a formal and recognized union.

“We’re not going away,” Sanchez said. “We're just going to get stronger, get more people to keep joining our efforts and we're not gonna give up. If anything, I think we’ll continue to be fueled to keep working.”

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: Penn State baseball pitching shines in shutout victory over Richmond
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State continued its first road trip of the season after dropping two out of the three games in its opening weekend at the MLB Desert Invitational.

The Nittany Lions continued their travel away from home as they made their way to Virginia for a four-game series against Richmond.

The blue and white kept the Spiders' offense quiet throughout all nine innings as it totaled 12 strikeouts, leading to a 5-0 win.

Glenn Smith started on the mound for the Spiders. The sophomore last pitched in Richmond’s home-opening series, where he let up five runs off four hits.

Smith started the first inning with some signs of struggle after walking centerfielder Justin Turcovski. However, following Turcovski being thrown out at second base after a steal attempt, the top of the first came to a close after second baseman Jayden Davis grounded into a double-play.

Colin Fitzgerald recorded a quick first out for Penn State in the bottom of the first after second baseman Michael Elko grounded out to shortstop. The Spiders followed a quick first out with a pair of singles.

Fitzgerald managed to get a quick strikeout for the second out, leading to a challenge from the Spiders after they attempted a double steal to get the first run of the game. Ultimately, catcher Dylan Winebrenner was called out after taking off from third to home.

Right fielder Jack Porter got on base with a bunt single for the Nittany Lions. The Spiders recorded their second out after right fielder Trevor Dosenbach made a diving catch in foul territory. Smith picked off Porter at second base to close out the top half of the second.

Penn State managed to keep the game scoreless after Richmond threatened with two runners on and two outs. A groundout to Davis closed out the second inning for the Nittany Lions.

The blue and white went down quietly in the top of the third after two strikeouts and a groundout kept it off the scoreboard.

The Spiders began to create another scare after Winebrenner hit a triple with one out. However, Fitzgerald managed to escape unscathed after striking out the next two batters to close out the third.

A pitchers' duel continued through the fourth inning with neither offense finding any momentum. Fitzgerald racked up a pair of strikeouts, reaching seven on the day.

Penn State found itself on the scoreboard first after Spencer Barnett sent a solo home run over the right field wall. This was the Pittsburgh native's first homer as a Nittany Lion, sending the ball 393 feet over the wall.

Smith’s day ended after the solo shot from Barnett. Penn State’s offense was able to record two more runs off of reliever Angel Santiago-Cruz. Shortstop Bryce Molinaro singled in Michael Anderson, followed by a sacrifice fly from Porter.

Fitzgerald remained in the game for the bottom half of the sixth. This inning marked his last of the game. The senior pitched six full innings without giving up a run and recorded eight strikeouts.

Barnett wasn’t done clearing the fence as he launched his second solo shot that went 374 feet over the center field wall, extending the lead to 4-0.

Dimond Loosli continued to keep the Spiders scoreless after taking the mound for Penn State in the bottom of the seventh.

Christian Dray came in to relieve Sanitago-Cruz, which soon led to a Davis RBI double into left field, increasing Penn State’s lead to five. Dray’s outing was cut short as Keane Hyer closed out the eighth.

Penn State turned to Anderson to close out its first game of the series against Richmond. The Nittany Lions ended the game with a double play.

Up Next

Penn State will return to Pitt Field for a doubleheader against the Spiders starting at 10 a.m. on Saturday

title: Letterman support remains priority in Matt Campbell's Penn State overhaul
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
When James Franklin was head coach at Penn State, he made a clear effort to get to know the lettermen. He hired a handful to the staff in various roles, had them visit during games and practice and interacted with them in other events related to the program.

Now with Matt Campbell at the helm, he’s making it a priority to know all the lettermen like Franklin once did. One of the first things Campbell did when he arrived at the Lasch Building for the first time was have Vice President for Intercollegiate Athletics Pat Kraft show him the list of former Nittany Lions hanging on plaques in the facility.

“Our greatest gift is those that came before us,” Campbell said at National Signing Day media. “It's still one of the absolute joys that you have walking in that facility every day and you see the names that have played and coached and the tradition that has come before us. I'll be honest with you, I've really tried to work really hard, and will continue to work really hard at unifying and aligning our history, our former players back involved in our football program, because I think it's so critical.”

During his introductory press conference, Campbell name-dropped coaches and players from the past like Joe Paterno, Todd Blackledge, Kyle Brady, Kerry Collins, Jack Ham, Courtney Brown and Saquon Barkley.

The list notably features names from several different teams and generations — Campbell has hit the ground running to know each and every player who previously wore the blue and white.

“He's met with almost all of the lettermen,” cornerbacks coach Terry Smith said. “He started with the ‘50s, ‘60s. We've done the ‘70s, ‘80s, ‘90s, 2000s and tonight we have 2010s to current. We're doing Zooms. He's opened himself up. He's opened up the building, Lasch, to all lettermen and their families. He's consciously trying to pull them back in, and he's doing a fantastic job. The feedback has been tremendous. It's not just talk. It's really good.”

Smith, who played wide receiver at Penn State, is one of a good chunk of lettermen on Campbell’s initial staff. In particular, Campbell went out of his way to highlight assistant linebackers coach Dan Connor, assistant quarterbacks coach Trace McSorley and Director of Talent Acquisition Alan Zemaitis during his presser.

There’s also assistant cornerbacks coach Jordan Lucas and Director of Player Development Jordan Hill who stuck around. Campbell also brought in a pair of lettermen himself in defensive coordinator D’Anton Lynn and assistant defensive line coach DeOn’tae Pannell.

Lynn, who was previously USC’s defensive coordinator and is an ex-Penn State cornerback, talked about how what Campbell is building reminded him of the program he used to play for.

“I feel like everything's a family decision at this point, and me and my wife felt like we were more prepared this time around,” Lynn said. “The more I talked to coach Campbell on the phone, he really kind of reminded me of why I went to Penn State, from what he was going to do with the culture here. I was excited to be on board.”

While there are staffers with previous ties to Penn State, the majority of coaches Campbell brought with him aren’t necessarily that in tune with the program’s history. That hasn’t stopped them from learning it.

“Don’t come to school here if you don’t care about the people that played before you,” offensive coordinator Taylor Mouser said. “You can’t come be their coach at Penn State if you don’t care about the history of decades and decades and decades of sacrifice.”

Campbell is making a clear effort to know those lettermen and he couldn’t be more determined to build the future of the program with the support of those from the past.

“It’s what has made the greatest time and the greatest eras and the greatest moments in this history of Penn State football,” Campbell said. “It makes it an honor to represent every day and I know that. And to continue to build that into our football team, we’re going to need everybody, and certainly our past coaches, our past players that want to be a part of this. We’re excited about that.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Column | Examining Penn State women’s lacrosse’s goalie situation
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
For the past two years, there’s been no question at Penn State who the starting goalkeeper was.

After starting in the first game of her collegiate career, Sydney Manning has been, without a doubt, the Nittany Lions’ starting goalie from here on out. That is, until she was benched in the second half on Wednesday for sophomore Arden Jansen.

Manning was off to a solid start against Pitt, recording one save and one goal at the end of the first quarter.

However, as the game progressed, she let in another five goals without a save, letting the Nittany Lions’ lead dwindle away. With 9:13 left in the third quarter, she was sidelined for Jansen, who recorded a much better save percentage and won the game for the blue and white.

This raises the question: should Manning remain at goalkeeper for Penn State, or should coach Kayla Treanor move on and find someone else in net?

There weren’t many questions about Manning’s performance after her first season in Happy Valley. After starting in her collegiate opener against Bucknell, she graced the starting lineup for the majority of her freshman season, coming off the bench in just three games.
