But when I suffered this loss during my senior year of high school, I felt comfort in the fact that I was soon to be going to a school which had significant efforts to help find a cure.

My passion for cancer research was sparked long before I walked on Penn State’s campus, but it has only increased since participating in THON. Each year, since I was a freshman, I have pushed myself to attend as many fundraising events and to raise as much money as possible. Dancing in THON has been a goal of mine since I was a freshman. While it is painful, I think about the insufferable pain felt physically by cancer patients each day, and the mental toll it takes on the families supporting them.

I was blessed with a happy and healthy childhood. My MomMom did not have to rush me

around to appointments everyday, rather we played in the pool outside. She did not have to give me medicine at certain times a day, rather we enjoyed our “eeyore blue star wars toast” and “orange creamsicle smoothies.” The children fighting cancer do not have that luxury.

I am dancing in THON so every child can play in their yard, so families can eat dinner at the table together, rather than running around from appointment to appointment. I am dancing in THON so medical bills are not a stresser and to see the smiles of cancer survivors in years to come.

title: Students show solidarity, shave heads at ‘No Hair, Don’t Care’
author: Grace Levy | The Daily Collegian
description:
THON held its annual “No Hair, Don’t Care” fundraiser today in the HUB-Robeson Center (HUB) from 11 a.m.-4 p.m. At the event, students shave their heads just days before the 46-hour dance marathon as a show of support for children who may be dealing with hair loss.

To participate, students donated $10, with $5 being credited to an organization, committee or independent dancer couple of their choosing.

The yearly event tends to draw in a lot of students as excitement builds for the weekend ahead. This year, 150 students were expected to participate, according to Hair Donation Committee Captains Payton Buffone and Sidney Cohen.

“It's so much fun,” Cohen, a graduate student studying special education, said. “Everyone is just happy to be here, getting excited for THON. We got a DJ going, we have a fun banner we painted, we have head bands. Many people just want to see their friends shave their heads, so lots of energy just getting excited for THON as we take a stand in just a few days.”

While students could sign up for the event as it occurred, for many, the anticipation started much earlier in the year. Luke Bennethum, a donor and alumni relations captain, said he and his committee members decided to participate together in the fall.

“I was (feeling) a lot of anticipation leading up to it, but a lot of my co-captains were around, so I got to go with them around,” Bennethum, a third-year studying political science, said. “It's always a good thing when you're doing this for children with cancer.”

For Bennethum, the motivation to participate in THON is personal, as is the “No Hair, Don’t Care” message of support.

“My older brother is a childhood cancer survivor, so it's kind of a way of giving back to families who helped us,” Bennethum said. “Even though he wasn't a Four Diamonds kid, a lot of the community came around and lifted our family up, so I just want to be a part of that for other people.”

That mission is the focus of the event, which aims to show solidarity for those with childhood cancer, while also raising money in the final days before THON weekend commences.

“This event is more something to get us excited for THON weekend — it's always the weekend before THON,” Buffone, a third-year studying accounting, said. “THON’s mission always is to provide emotional and financial support, so this is really getting us to focus on the emotional side during the time we’re really trying to push our donor drives and obviously fundraise as much as we can.”

The Hair Donation Committee hosts two other donation events during the year, one at 100 days until THON and the other during THON weekend. During these events, donors who have at least 12 inches of untreated hair can donate their hair to Wigs for Kids, a THON partner organization that creates wigs for children suffering from hair loss free of charge.

Some participants, such as Solomon Miller, have donated their hair for wigs in the past and hope to do so again in the future. At the event, Miller said he shaved off nearly 12 inches of hair after growing it out for years.

“When I was in middle school, my mom gave me the idea of donating it just because it's something I have that other people don’t, so why not share that?" Miller, a second-year studying biobehavioral health, said. "I can grow it back — I’m going to grow it back and donate it.”

Many students have participated in the event in the past, coming back to support their friends and peers, but also the larger mission of supporting children and families impacted by childhood cancer.

“THON has been a huge part of my time at Penn State,” Jeffrey Walsh, a fourth-year studying supply chain and information systems. “I feel really lucky to be able to volunteer for this organization and I wanted to stand with the other people in the organization that are participating in this, so I thought this would be a good way to show that.”

MORE THON COVERAGE

title: 'We just put ourselves in a tough position’ |  Turnovers, mental errors a key factor in Penn State basketball’s loss to Rutgers
author: Chase Mitchell | The Daily Collegian
description:
For the first time this season, Penn State entered a Big Ten game as the favorite.

The Nittany Lions went into Wednesday’s matchup against Rutgers as 4.5-point favorites, since the Scarlet Knights entered with a 10-15 record and sat right around the bottom of the conference alongside the blue and white.

However, the tale of the night was the number of turnovers and points scored because of them. It wasn’t just the turnovers that caused Penn State’s 85-72 loss, though, but also the fouls and other mental mistakes.

“They’re a good defensive team,” Penn State guard Kayden Mingo said. “But, you know, taking care of the ball is one of the things we do best. No matter who we play, I feel like we should be able to take care of the ball – especially me.”

Mingo scored 16 points on the night but committed a couple of fouls and gave away possession a few times.

All season, Penn State has been one of the better ball-handling squads, consistently staying around the top of the NCAA in fewest turnovers per game.

Averaging 9.8 turnovers per game, the Nittany Lions are tied for 29th in the country in giving up the fewest turnovers.

However, the 15 turnovers allowed against Rutgers are going to put a dent in that ranking.

More alarming than that, though, is the 28 points given up off the turnovers that allowed the Scarlet Knights to score often, and in bunches.

“Ten turnovers in the first half that led to 19 points really killed us,” Mike Rhoades said. “Not enough defense today. We gave up 28 points off the turnovers, then their bench scored 28 points. We had trouble guarding the ball, and some really bad fouls… Really hurt us.”

Turnovers have not been the most alarming problem in many of Penn State’s losses, including last weekend’s defeat to Oregon, when Penn State only lost the ball nine times.

Rhoades commented on the frustration of the sudden turnover problem and the difficulty to identify the overarching issues.

“If I knew it, we would keep fixing and addressing it,” Rhoades said. “Some of it’s matchups, some of it’s turnovers. I think in some of these games, we’ve had turnovers that we can’t defend. I really think the games where we got off to a good start and we’re in a position to play better than today, was because we took care of the ball… You can’t set your defense and give yourself a chance on defense when you turn it over and they leak out and score.”

Rutgers, a solid defensive team, was allowing opponents to score 75.6 points per game on average. However, forcing just 11 turnovers per game entering its matchup with the blue and white placed it in the lower echelon of the NCAA.

“We got off to a good start, you know, it’s our defense,” Rutgers coach Steve Pikiell said. “We didn’t let them score for the first I don’t know how many minutes, but our defense got us off to a good start.”

Penn State has also been one of the more disciplined teams in terms of fouling all season. The Nittany Lions entered the night committing 15.6 fouls per game on average, and their 19 committed against the Scarlet Knights won’t help the cause.

Seven-footer Ivan Jurić even fouled out against Rutgers after his fifth personal foul late in the second half. He scored eight points on the night and gave up three turnovers as well.

Guard Freddie Dilione V also entered foul trouble territory after he sent a Scarlet Knight to the charity stripe for two shots after his fourth personal foul with five and a half minutes left in the second frame.

As a team, Rutgers knocked down 20 of its 23 attempts from the free throw line for an 87% mark.

Forward Josh Reed has been an underlooked piece of Penn State’s offense this season, but has also been a key defensive player for the blue and white.

Reed recorded a career-high 22 points against Rutgers, but had a pair of fouls and turnovers to go along with it.

With the season well in the rear-view mirror, and a likely tough seeding for the Big Ten Tournament on the horizon, it’s no longer about correcting the overarching problems that Rhoades mentioned, but the smaller, mental mistakes that are within the blue and white’s control.

“Yeah, it’s hard, for sure. We didn’t come out with any energy tonight and that showed early on,” Reed said. “Being down 20 at half doesn’t help. We just put ourselves in a tough position.”

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Notebook | Penn State men's lacrosse coach Jeff Tambroni talks game with Navy, player personnel
author: AJ Piazza | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State men’s lacrosse got a large, much-needed victory against then No. 3 Princeton 13-7. This came after an upset loss to Villanova in overtime, 14-13. The Nittany Lions will continue their road trip as they will visit a Navy team full of returning players. The squad will look for its first back-to-back win of the season and try to build on that momentum against the Midshipmen.

Coach Jeff Tambroni talked to the media, and here’s what he had to say.

Previewing Navy

“It’s one of the nicest lacrosse playing facilities in the country, and I think a part of that is it’s in the heart of the hotbed of lacrosse and the history surrounding the stadium in the way that they honor both service and conflicts that we’ve had here in the US,” Tambroni said. “Super blessed to be not just a lacrosse player but an American. I just think it is one of the neatest lacrosse facilities that college has to offer.”

This will be Navy’s toughest task of the season, and the Midshipmen are coming off a victory against VMI. Last season, the Nittany Lions got the best of Navy with a 13-7 win, but most of that Navy team is returning, while most of Penn State’s is not. One player who stood out was AJ Marsh.

“He impacts every part of the field. He’s very, very talented and typically covers the opponent's best in their base, their bottom third of the defensive end. He’s really good in transition,” Tambroni said. “He can distribute the ball, he’ll dodge if you’re putting him under duress. He affects the entire field, and I think that’s just rare in any day and age.”

Marsh will be circled as the guy to watch for Navy. Penn State will need to make sure he’s marked up at all levels of the game.

New Look Offense

Penn State has lost a lot of big names on offense over the last few years. The Nittany Lions lost TJ Malone and Matt Traynor in the last two seasons. The blue and white have a young offense, and it seems to be working.

Penn State is averaging 14.7 goals, with Hunter Aquino leading the way in goals with 10 on the team, but the most impactful player is freshman Jack Iannantuono. Iannantunono has nine points in three games and leads the team in assists with five.

“Jack Iannatuono really does make a big difference. To have someone back that distributes the ball the way he does, he definitely makes people better around him,” Tambroni said. “You know a little bit of a missing piece, I would say, from last year’s offense into this year’s offense.”

It’s a short sample size, but the freshman has already made an impact and has been able to spread the ball. Iannantuono is tied for 6th in the Big Ten in assists. With Liam Matthews back in the fold, it will be interesting to see how Tambroni will spread the attack around, but Iannantuono will continue to find his place on the field if he keeps dishing the ball the way he has to start the season.

Alex Ross and Preston Hawkins

Alex Ross was a preseason All-American, and it seems that he has taken his game to a new level. Against the Tigers, Ross was massive. The senior had three caused turnovers and three ground balls.

“I think the biggest thing for Alex is just simplifying his game and shifting over to a more primary defenseman,” Tambroni said. “I think his first couple years, he played around some really talented veteran players like Jack Posey and Kevin Parnham and he was asked to be a little bit more of a matchup defenseman. I think with the void of Jack and Kevin and others who are no longer in the defensive end, we are still asking him to cover the opponent's best attack, but he’s also managing a little bit more of our tactics.”

Ross won Big Ten Defensive Player of the Week and continues to be one of the best defenders in the country.

Preston Hawkins has skyrocketed since his first game. Hawkins had a rough first outing and got pulled early. The freshman played better against Villanova, but really showed out against Princeton.

“Significant growth since he walked on campus as a freshman, and then I’d say consistent growth since the season started. It’s a lot going in there as a freshman in any position, multiply that by 50 to play that particular position and then pile on top of that,” Tambroni said.

So far this season, Hawkins has a 10.812 goals against average and a 0.444 save percentage. He has been trending in the right direction as the season has gone on.

“He just continues to stay the course and remains confident in his approach. He was under a lot of duress against Princeton and managed it really well mentally,” Tambroni said. “So I’m just really impressed so far with his growth and his ability to keep coming back from potential disappointments. He has used it as growth, part of journey, and he continues to keep getting better.”

Hawkins won Big Ten Freshman of the Week and will need to continue to play as he did against Princeton for the Nittany Lions to be successful this season.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: ‘We just put ourselves in a tough position’ | Penn State attributes loss against Rutgers to slow start
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Three hours before Penn State faced Rutgers on Wednesday night, Mike Rhoades said his players had a “really good” shootaround while showing “great energy,” but it was a different story when the two teams tipped off.

The Scarlet Knights got off to a 16-2 lead with 12:22 left in the first half as the Nittany Lions looked lethargic in the beginning of an 85-72 loss inside the Bryce Jordan Center. Penn State had a more productive second half, but its start was attributed as a main reason why the team was defeated.

“Guys look excited to play from three o'clock to six o'clock,” Rhoades said postgame. “I don't know if it was a pregame meal, no excuses. The start of the game just really crushed us … Just a tale of two halves. This is a disappointing start to the game that killed us.”

It took Penn State until the 13:28 mark of the first half to get on the scoreboard, which was courtesy of a mid-range jumper by guard Kayden Mingo. The Nittany Lions had missed their first six shots from the field.

Additionally, Penn State wasn’t taking care of the ball as it had four turnovers prior to its first basket. Rutgers managed to score seven points off of those mistakes, guiding it to an 11-0 lead with 13:48 left in the first half.

“If you go back and look, we had a lot of turnovers early in the game,” Rhoades said. “The other thing is letting a good player get going early, and you see that ball go in early, that rim gets bigger and bigger. We didn't guard (Tariq) Francis early very well, and he got himself going, and got some other guys going.”

Francis scored a team-high 22 points for the Scarlet Knights, including 15 points in the first half. The guard had seven of Rutgers’ first 16 points as well as two assists and a steal during his squad’s run to begin the game.

The Scarlet Knights continued their strong start by earning a 39-20 halftime lead, which prompted forward Josh Reed to say it’s “hard for sure” to come back from that.

“We're down (19 points) at halftime,” Reed said postgame. “We made a little run in the second half, but like I said, being down (19 points) at halftime doesn't help. We just put ourselves in a tough position.”

When the Nittany Lions get themselves in a situation like that, Rhoades said the team leans on Reed and Mingo, who scored 22 and 16 points, respectively. When Penn State cut the lead down to eight points late in the second half, it was on a 3-pointer by Reed with an assist from Mingo.

Overall, Penn State’s offense was more efficient in the second half, shooting 68% from the field and 15-of-19 at the free throw line. Still, the Nittany Lions only outscored Rutgers 52-46 in the final 20 minutes, so their lackluster start came back to haunt them with the 13-point loss.

Wednesday night wasn’t the first time this season Penn State got in a position like that, as the team held halftime deficits of 50-32 against Ohio State on Jan. 26 and 56-26 versus Maryland on Jan. 18. In both games, the Nittany Lions made runs in the second half, but their sluggish start prevented them from winning.

Rhoades said he doesn’t know a common theme across these poor starts to games this season. He said some of it is turnovers or certain matchups, but he mentioned the times it hasn’t been an issue are when his players are taking care of the ball.

During this latest example of a slow start, Reed said the players “didn't come out with any energy,” which “showed early on.” He said it starts in practice on days leading up to the game to have enough energy for the contest.

“We gotta be better about that,” Reed said. “Just gotta come into practice, ready to work, ready to get better every day. Learn from this game and then move on to the next game.”

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Preview | Penn State Baseball set to take on Richmond for four games
author: Ashley Felsberg I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State Baseball is fresh off a blowout against Kansas State, losing 24-5 to the Wildcats at its third game of the MLB Desert Invitational. Prior to this match, the Nittany Lions suffered a 15-3 loss against the Air Force Academy and pulled through with a 9-3 win against GCU.

This match against Richmond will be a non-conference meetup, for a four-game series, starting on Feb. 20, going through Feb. 22. There will be one game played each day, with the exception of Feb. 21, in which there will be the first match at 10:30 am and the second one at 2:00 pm.

While the blue and white are coming off an early-season record of 1-2, they have a good chance at excelling in this series. The Nittany Lions last saw Richmond just last season, where they outscored the Spiders 17-6.

In the 2025 matchup, the blue and white outperformed the Spiders in at bats, runs, hits, and RBI’s. Penn State did lose five of their starters from last season to the MLB, however, in an effort to fill this void, head Coach Gambino added 11 newcomers, three of which top 25 prospects from the northeast.

Likewise, Richmond has an entirely new structure, as over half of its roster is new additions. These new additions include 15 freshmen and eight transfer players. Because these transfer players are seniors, they bring a strong baseball background and familiarity with them.

Richmond began its season this year with a loss to Monmouth, but quickly recovered, beating the Hawks in the remaining two games of the three-game series. Still, the Spiders are 3-2 after a loss to UNC and a win against James Madison University.

While the matchups against Monmouth were competitive, each with the final scores benign within two runs of each other, the Hawks suffered a devastating shutout against the Tar Heels, 10-0.

Each of Richmond’s prior games this season, like Penn State, has been nonconference. This makes it difficult to judge how they will do in their prospective conference, Richmond’s being the Atlantic 10.

Richmond’s first conference matchup will take place on Mar. 13 against Saint Joseph's. Penn State will also have its first conference meeting that same day, against Iowa State.

The matchup between the Nittany Lions and Hawks will commence at 1:00 pm on Friday at Pitt Field in Richmond, VA.

MORE BASEBALL CONTENT

title: Penn State men's basketball fails to comeback from large first-half deficit against Rutgers
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State hosted Rutgers in its Academic Achievement Night & Faculty and Staff Appreciation Night, and as a part of the Venmo Big Ten Rivalry Series – quite the festivities for two of the Big Ten’s bottom three teams via KenPom.

Both squads arrived at the Bryce Jordan Center with 2-8 records over their last 10, and in this clash, both teams were presented with golden opportunities to gain some traction amid difficult seasons.

Ultimately, in the matchup of the lower-class conference competitors, the Scarlet Knights snagged the opportunity with an 85-72 victory.

To start the game, Mike Rhoades sent out Penn State’s usual first unit of backcourt Kayden Mingo and Freddie Dilione V, front court Dominick Stewart and Josh Reed, with 7-footer Ivan Juric down low.

Rutgers sent out a similar lineup, with the same exact makeup as the Nittany Lions’ starters. Both units started two freshmen, a senior, a sophomore and a junior. For Rutgers, its star guard Tariq Francis anchored that bunch.

The Scarlet Knights set their tone with the first bucket of the game, winning the tip and promptly throwing an alley-oop to senior big man Emmanuel Ogbole. That pace remained for Rutgers, as they jumped out to a nine-point lead after just under five minutes of play, with Rhoades making frequent changes on the floor in an attempt to close the early gap.

Penn State’s offense continued to be an early problem, as it didn’t get its first score until nearly six and a half minutes in, courtesy of a Mingo midrange, and it cut an early Rutgers lead to 11-2.

The Nittany Lions' possessions continued to remain empty, as Penn State fell even further behind, via a sloppy five turnovers and a shot that only fell once on eight attempts over the first eight minutes of play. Just under the midpoint of the half, the blue and white got its first back-to-back scoring possessions of the game via a crafty Reed midrange bucket and fastbreak Dilione layup to cut the lead to ten.

Both offenses stayed streaky over the next five minutes, but with the slight pace, confidence and momentum advantage, and eventually the Scarlet Knights directed that into more consistent offensive play. With the buckets flowing through Francis, who had a game-high 15 points at the half, the Scarlet Knights led 39-20.

The blue and white offensive performance at the half had no player score more than six points, shot 41% as a team, didn’t make a three-pointer over eight attempts and committed ten turnovers.

After the break, the Nittany Lions got a few looks inside the arc. The scoring wasn’t pretty, but they made it a 15-point game after four minutes of play, following perfect shooting on Penn State’s first four attempts.

Reed was assessed after a review for a hook-and-hold flagrant one foul, which handicapped momentum and allowed the Scarlet Knights to push the lead to 19. Rutgers continued to space out the floor and hit open cuts to the basket, with the Nittany Lions dumbfounded inside the paint.

The one bright side for the blue and white was Melih Tunca, who, in his first three minutes on the floor in the second half, contributed three makes on three shots. Reed joined him in efficiency, starting 3-of-4 on field goal attempts in the second half.

It wasn’t enough, as Rutgers continued to get to the foul line in droves, practically gifting the Scarlet Knights extra points on each possession. At the midpoint of the half, the Scarlet Knights had earned nine charity stripe points compared to Penn State’s three.

Over the next few minutes, the Nittany Lions doubled their points from the line, but Rutgers had found other ways to score and had inflated the lead to 67-53.

The charity stripe continued to treat the blue and white well, as it made it an 11-point game at the line courtesy of a pair of Stewart makes. Rhoades continued to tinker with lineups, even utilizing a small-ball group with Reed as the tallest player on the court.

Reed came alive down the wire with a three-pointer to make it an eight-point game and set a new single-game career scoring high. It wasn’t enough, as despite moments of pushing back, Penn State couldn’t find a way to best a Rutgers team that had its number from the tip.

For the Nittany Lions, Reed finished with a new personal collegiate best 22 points on 70% shooting, followed by Mingo and Dilione with double-digit scoring. Francis led the Scarlet Knights with his team-high 22 points, joined by forward Dylan Grant with 15 points.

With the result, the Nittany Lions fall to 11-16 on the season and 2-14 in conference play.

Availability Report

The lone player on the sideline with redshirt Reggie Grodin was international forward Tibor Mirtić, who was listed as out pregame.

Up Next

The Nittany Lions head to Lincoln, Nebraska, for a 2 p.m. clash against the Cornhuskers on Saturday. It’ll be a standalone road trip game before the Nittany Lions head back to Happy Valley, with the game streamed on the Big Ten Network.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Breaking down the THON 2026 line dance lyrics
author: Kahlie Wray | Managing Editor
description:
THON 2026 is officially underway as Dancer Relations Captains took to the stage on Friday night, delivering the first Line Dance of the weekend. Here’s a breakdown of this year’s lyrics.

Intro

BJC, let’s hear you roar // hand-in-hand, take the floor

Dance along, all invited //Nittany lions – united

26 our moments here // spread our mission far & near

Love- it leads us through // feel the power PSU

Penn State verse

Hockey Valley, turf meets ice

Penn State held its first-ever Winter Sports Weekend, which featured the first-ever men’s and women’s hockey game in Beaver Stadium.

Benny’s pizza, one big slice

The Virginia-based Benny Leone’s franchise recently opened in Happy Valley, offering large slices of pizza.

Madam Major, royalty

Ellie Sheehan was named Penn State Blue Band’s first-ever female drum major in the organization’s 125-year-plus history for the 2025-26 season.

Sing our love and loyalty // A new chapter, glory found

The first half is from the Penn State Alma Mater.

Pollock Reno // breaking ground

The Pollock Residence halls are undergoing renovations.

B-dubs- wings to-go

In March 2025, Buffalo Wild Wings officially opened at 134 S. Garner St.

There’s a car in Trader Joe’s

In October 2025, a driver crashed into the Trader Joe’s in Patton Township.

EDU, time to roam

The Penn State Wireless project introduced “eduroam” as the primary Wi-Fi network on campus in April. The “psu” network officially went out of service in November.

No more swipe, tap your phone

Penn State announced its mobile-first credential initiative. Faculty, students and staff transitioned to the mobile-id card “in an effort to streamline university operations and enhance campus life.”

Campbell — joins the crew

Following James Franklin’s firing in October, Penn State football named Matt Campbell the 17th head football coach.

Terry Smith, we thank you

Terry Smith was named Penn State interim head coach following Franklin’s firing. The team won four straight games as well as the Bad Boy Mowers Pinstripe Bowl during his tenure. Smith is now the associate head coach under Campbell.

On the mat, 4 straight wins

Penn State wrestling won its fourth straight national championship in a row in 2-25.

Swoosh is out, stripes are in

Penn State Athletics will switch to adidas apparel starting in the 2026-27 academic year. The agreement will span a 10-year period beginning on July 1.

Susan Welch, what was that?

The Susan Welch Liberal Arts Building was evacuated due to a structural issue in November 2025. It will remain closed throughout the spring 2026 semester.

When’s the power coming back?!

A powerful windstorm called a derecho swept through Centre County in May. At the height of the storm, about 35,000 Centre County residents lost power.

Chorus

Let’s go State, feel the beat//Every lion bring the heat!

Are you ready? Raise your voice//Happy valley make some noise!

46-stand to fight//Dancing forward in their light

For the cause — hearts transform//This is where our dreams are born

World events

Jewels go missing from the Louvre// Thieves in shadows make their move

The Louvre Museum in Paris was shut down briefly after two thieves disguised as construction workers stole eight pieces worth 88 million euros ($102 million) during a heist in October.

Nasa Boots – touch back down// Solar Secrets have been found

United States astronauts Suni Williams and Butch Wilmore returned to Earth after an eight-day mission to the International Space Station turned into a nine-month stay in space.

Furthermore, scientists discovered that solar flares have the potential to reach 60 million degrees, which is over six times higher than originally believed.

Windy City enters Rome // Vatican – Leo’s home

American Cardinal Robert Prevost was elected the 267th pontiff, following the death of Pope Francis. Prevost is a Chicago native and chose the name Leo XIV.

Green turtles safe at sea

The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) officially ruled that the green sea turtle is no longer on the endangered species list.

Goodall and her chimpanzees

Celebrated conservationist Jane Goodall, best known for her work with chimpanzees died at age 91 in October.

U.S. teams go for gold

The 2026 Milano Cortina Olympic Games commenced on Feb. 6, with 232 athletes representing the U.S.

As of Friday night, the U.S. has earned 29 medals. Figure skater Alysa Liu and the women’s ice hockey team have contributed to the country’s 10 gold medals.

NHL – lifts the hold

For the first-time since 2014, NHL athletes are allowed to compete in the Winter Olympics.

Lando Norris winning race

British Formula One driver, Lando Norris was the 2025 FIA Formula 1 Driver’s World Champion.

Sweden reaches zero waste

Sweden is closer to zero waste than any country has ever been before. In 2022, the Swedish government launched a national plan to reduce the consumption of single-use plastics. By 2026, they expect to see a 50% reduction in consumption.

Socials, not down under

Australia implemented a social media ban for under-16s. Facebook, Instagram, Snapchat, Threads, TikTok, X, YouTube, Reddit and streaming platforms Kick and Twitch are some of the included platforms under the ban.

UK voters now they’re younger

The United Kingdom lowered the voting age to 16.

New Years ball drops 2 times

In honor of the upcoming 250th anniversary of when the Declaration of Independence was signed, the Times Square Alliance — the group responsible for organizing the ball drop — decided to drop two balls. The second featured a red, white and blue design representing the flag.

No more pennies, still got dimes

The U.S. Treasury Mint retired the penny 232 years after its creation.

Pop Culture verse

Reaching flow state scrolling reels

Instagram reels have gained popularity and with users being able to curate their feed, they reach “a flow state” while scrolling.

LeBron Edits a big deal

The NBA's all-time leading scorer, LeBron James became the focus of many Tik Tok edits. LeBron is a 23-year veteran of the league and has had his fair share of viral moments.

Irwin dancers now there's 2 // Mirrorball's to the zoo

Robert Irwin won season 34 of "Dancing with the Stars," 10 years after his sister, Bindi, won season 21. The siblings are best known for being wildlife conservationists at the Australia Zoo.

Fireworks in zero g

American singer Katy Perry went to space aboard Jeff Bezos's Blue Origin Rocket in April 2025 with an all-female crew. "Firework" is the name of Perry's hit 2012 single which peaked at number one on the Billboard Hot 100.

Final bell, "can't see me"

Newly-retired professional wrestler John Cena did his iconic "You Can't See Me" move at the end of his final WWE match in December 2025.

KPOP hunters top the charts

Netflix animated musical, "KPOP Demon Hunters" soundtrack his No. 1 on the Billboard 200 albums chart. "Golden," a popular song on the album won the best song written for visual media at the 2026 Grammys.

Happys back to steal our hearts

Adam Sandler returned as Happy Gilmore in the star-studded “Happy Gilmore 2.” Celebrities such as Bad Bunny, Kid Cudi, Travis Kelce and professional golfers all made cameos.

Crazy Train, always revvin'

British heavy metal singer John Michael "Ozzy" Osbourne died at the age of 76 on July 22. He’s best known for his work in the band Black Sabbath and his robust solo career.

What's not clocking

Grammy-award winning singer Justin Bieber, said “It’s not clocking to you that I’m standing on business, is it” during a run-in with paparazzi last June.

The heated exchange was widely circulated and became a viral internet meme.

6 or 7

6 or 7 is in reference to a viral internet meme that took over the minds of Generation Alpha and Gen Z. The phrase is followed by an up-and-down hand motion and has no apparent meaning.

Blast old Taylor, let’s all sing

American singer and songwriter Taylor Swift announced she regained the rights of the master recordings for her first six albums from Shamrock Capital, a private equity firm that purchased them from her old manager Scooter Braun.

AI songs, that's a thing?

Due to an increase in artificial intelligence, some artists have incorporated AI into their songwriting, production and in some cases, vocals.

Conrad Endgame, save the date

The third and final season of hit TV-series "The Summer I Turned Pretty" premiered in the summer of 2025. Belly Conklin, the series' main character ending the love triangle at the show's center, choosing Conrad Fisher over his brother, Jeremiah.

Creator of the show, Jenny Han, announced there would be a movie following the couple released in the future.

Upside down, close the gate // lets all sing

Popular Netflix series, "Stranger Things" returned for its fifth and final season in November 2025.

In the show, an inter-dimensional gate cracked open the wall of the tank room, which allowed a monster, the demogorgon, to travel out of the Upside Down.

Pixel world Steves all blocky // Hold-on, watch-out Chicken Jockey!

A Minecraft Movie starring Jack Black and Jason Momoa was released in 2025. Many theatre audiences threw popcorn and yelled “Chicken Jockey” every time a baby zombie riding a chicken appeared on screen.

THON verse

Lets go State, feel the beat // Every lion bring the heat

Are you ready? Raise your voice // Happy Valley, make some noise

46- stand to fight // Dancing forward in their light

For the cause-hearts transform // This is where our dreams are born

For the Kids, on the green

THON’s best DJ steals the scene

Anthony Coscuna won THON’s best DJ Competition. He will perform live during the weekend.

NFL wears cleats with pride

10 NFL players, eight of which are former Nittany Lions, spotlighted THON during the 10th annual “ My Clause, My Cleats” campaign. The campaign is a player-led initiative where players highlight a non-profit or charity organization of their choice through cleat customization.

Volunteers lead in stride// Dream forward, records set

THON raised a record $2 million during its 2025 Dream Forward campaign.

Line dance turns the big 5-0

The THON Line Dance which is performed periodically throughout the weekend turned 50-years-old.

50 themes to help us grow // Grab my hand - let’s take flight

With our diamonds shining bright // Courage makes our actions bold

Showing strength-paths unfold //Gaining wisdom from our knight // and our honest guiding light

Lead with love, ignite the spark // All as one we leave our mark

WATCH THE FIRST LINE DANCE

title: Penn State notebook | Cael Sanderson talks Levi Haines, Senior Night ahead of regular-season finale
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
After beating Ohio State by a score of 36-5, Penn State faces Princeton at 7 p.m. on Friday at Rec Hall to round out the regular season.

Ahead of the bout against the Tigers, Cael Sanderson met with the media on Wednesday afternoon in the Lorenzo Wrestling Complex.

Here’s what he had to say.

Levi Haines

Of Penn State’s starters, 174-pounder Levi Haines will be the lone one to partake in the Senior Night festivities on Friday.

The Arendtsville, Pennsylvania, native has been a four-year starter for the Nittany Lions, which featured him winning a national title at 157 pounds as a sophomore.

“He wanted to come here, and we did our best to take good care of him,” Sanderson said. “He keeps getting better. Great leader. Obviously, not a guy you’re excited to have graduate. You’re excited for him. Every stage in life is designed to be more exciting. He’s given a great effort every day here.”

As far as Haines’ future of whether he’ll remain in State College and train as part of the Nittany Lion Wrestling Club, Sanderson didn’t reveal an answer.

“We haven’t had that conversation yet,” Sanderson said. “Well, we kind of have, actually. You don’t let a guy like that leave. We’ll do everything we can to keep him here and keep him a part of the program and training here.”

Braeden Davis

It was tough sledding for Braeden Davis against Ohio State, losing the 141-pound matchup against Jesse Mendez, who, along with Penn State’s 165-pounder Mitchell Mesenbrink, is a frontrunner to win the Hodge Trophy, which is given to the best wrestler in the country.

Davis fell to Mendez, 18-2, losing via tech fall after Mendez recorded three takedowns by the time the bout concluded.

“Just making sure we have a better game plan, but the big thing is don't get discouraged,” Sanderson said. “Don't die of shame. It's one match, and it's history. We're on to the next.”

Davis filled in for Aaron Nagao midway through the season after Nagao suffered a season-ending injury. Davis has also battled with a “skin issue” and a hand injury, but Sanderson isn’t worried about where he stands following the blowout loss.

“He was a little behind, starting late, as we all know, and the training’s been a little different for him, but he'll be at his best in March and that's something we're excited to see,” Sanderson said.

Senior Night

While Haines will be the only starter honored on Senior Night, there are a few other wrestlers who likely will be joining him before the dual.

Sam Beckett, who is a 165-pounder and graduate student, and senior heavyweight Lucas Cochran, are the only other Nittany Lions on the roster who boast senior eligibility.

With that being said, it’ll be one of the smaller celebrations that Sanderson has had for his wrestlers during his time in State College.

“We don't have a lot of guys walking, which is good. It's good for the program. It’s not as good if you're trying to bring new guys in when you have a roster cap, but obviously you're planning, and you see what's going to happen in the future,” Sanderson said. “We have a special group of guys that will be walking, and they've obviously meant a lot to the program, and the last four or five years they've been here have been remarkable.”

Passions off the mat

Mesenbrink recently went viral on social media for a video of him singing “Jealous” by Nick Jonas on TikTok live.

Sanderson was asked about encouraging his wrestlers to have passions off the mat. He revealed that when he wrestled at Iowa State, he spent hours drawing and painting. Separately, during the media availability, he talked about Haines and his passion for hunting, in which Sanderson said he would prefer him to do that then go downtown to the bars.

Sanderson said it’s important to have something where one can “find relief mentally and enjoy it.”

“If somebody's passionate about something, you don't need to wait until you're done wrestling before you get out there,” Sanderson said. “It's discipline with time, and part of a high-level training plan is doing what you enjoy doing.”

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State wrestler Aaron Brooks receives 2-year ban
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Ex-Penn State wrestler Aaron Brooks has officially received his ruling.

Brooks was initially suspended for three years because of anti-doping rules violations, but his ban from wrestling was dropped to two years on Wednesday. The initial ban came in June from a purchase he made in April.

According to the arbitration ruling, Brooks was using Life Extension DHEA after being released from the hospital following a treatment for double pneumonia. The supplement was expected to help with potential sleep apnea, which his doctors informed him of.

The four-time national champion failed a drug test and was charged with anti-doping violations, with his ban still being up in the air at the time.

The USADA initially wanted to give Brooks a four-year ban, but dropped it to two years after ruling that he didn't break the anti-doping rules intentionally.

Brooks will miss the next two world team trials, but will be back in time to compete for a chance to make the 2028 U.S. Olympic team, which will take place in Los Angeles.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: No. 23 Penn State women’s lacrosse beats No. 25 Pitt in overtime thriller
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Amidst a 3-0 start to the Kayla Treanor era, Penn State was set to face its toughest opponent of the year.

Recently ranked No. 25, Pitt entered Holuba Hall for the Nittany Lions’ first-ranked matchup of the year. The blue and white defeated the Panthers in overtime, 12-11.

After Penn State scored quickly in the first three games of the year with multiple goals in the first few minutes, things were a little slower in Holuba Hall on Wednesday. Nearly three minutes into the game, the Nittany Lions got on the board first as graduate transfer Anna Salerno earned her fifth goal of the year off an assist from Delaney Radin.

Just one minute later, Penn State was back on the scoreboard as Payton Wainman scored her eighth goal of the year, giving the blue and white a two-score lead.

For the third time in five minutes, Kelly MacKinney scored off another assist from Delaney Radin, before Pitt finally got on the scoreboard with its first goal of the day.

Both sides exchanged some defensive success with turnovers caused by Radin and Maggie Driver as neither team scored for over three minutes. However, it was the Nittany Lions who found their way back to the scoreboard, as Radin scored her first of the game after two assists earlier in the quarter.

Penn State wound down the first 15 minutes with Wainan’s second goal of the game, assisted by MacKinney, and held the Panthers off the scoreboard with a free-position shot save by Sydney Manning.

After a dominant first period from the blue and white, Pitt was first on the scoreboard to start the second quarter as Gigi Leonzi scored her first of the day.

Freshman Rocquette Allen came into the game leading the Nittany Lions with 12 goals this season, but was held silent in the first quarter. At the 12:21 mark, she earned her first goal of the game, but it was quickly negated as Avery Moon brought it back to a three-goal game for the Panthers.

Senior Emma Kelly quickly earned a hat trick with three goals in the second quarter, further extending Penn State’s lead with a Pitt goal sandwiched between.

The third quarter opened just as the second quarter did, as Pitt cut the deficit to four goals with Paige Telatovich earning her first of the day. Exactly one minute later, Kaitlyn Giandonato scored her first of the day, hot on a run.

After Treanor subbed Arden Jansen in net after Manning recorded just one save against six goals, the Nittany Lions found momentum again with MacKinney’s second goal of the game.

Penn State spent the next five minutes mostly on offense, but couldn’t score. When the Panthers had a chance to score on inexperienced Jansen, she recorded her first save of the game off a free position shot.

Following the save, the Nittany Lions eventually made their way down the field after some early struggles, but ultimately found their 11th goal of the game as Radin scored again.

Entering the fourth quarter, Penn State was up by five goals. However, Pitt cut that deficit early in the period as Leonzi earned her second goal of the game, and soon after decreased it again with a 2-0 run to start the quarter.

The Panthers’ run continued with their third straight goal as they made it a two-goal game with less than 10 minutes remaining. Pitt’s offense remained strong with the following draw, staying on the attack and eventually scoring again to make it a one-goal game.

Both teams had opportunities with the ball, each taking a timeout before attempting to score. However, both teams came up unsuccessful after Maddie Greco fell, losing the ball and giving the Panthers possession with 47 seconds left.

Pitt took the ball down the field, and after some offensive deliberation around the net, made the attack and scored with Jansen out of the net to tie the game.

The Nittany Lions earned early control of the ball to start overtime, but a bad pass by Salerno changed possession after less than a minute. Fortunately for Penn State, Pitt only had control for 30 seconds before turning the ball over as well.

After Penn State missed a pair of shots, Allen made just her second goal of the game to win 12-11 in overtime.

Injury report

Tewaaraton Award watchlister Erika Ho was not in the starting lineup against Pitt and saw no playing time.

Up next

The Nittany Lions will hit the road for the first time this season, traveling to Philadelphia for a matchup against Drexel at noon on Sunday.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's hockey's Aiden Fink named alternate captain for rest of season
author: Evan Smith | The Daily Collegian
description:
A new captain has been announced.

Aiden Fink has been named an alternate captain for the remainder of the 2025-26 hockey season, announced by Penn State on Wednesday.

Junior forward Aiden Fink added to Penn State's leadership group as alternate captain for the remainder of the 2025-26 season!!Read ➡️ https://t.co/4O1PmGRg8z#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/KbsSQov5lr— Penn State Men’s Hockey (@PennStateMHKY) February 18, 2026

The Predators prospect has scored 22 points in 21 games on the blue and white’s first line this season and is the fastest Nittany Lion to reach 100 career points. He's also seventh in team history with 109 points.

Last season, Fink was the first Penn Stater to be named a Hobey Baker top-10 finalist after breaking Penn State’s single-season scoring record with 53 points. He joins Ben Schoen, Reese Laubach and Jarod Crespo in the leadership group.

title: Penn State track and field's Handal Roban named Big Ten Track Athlete of the Week
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Handal Roban's hard work is paying off.

Roban was named the Big Ten Track Athlete of the Week after his performance at the ASICS Sound Invite last week, where he set the school record in the 800-meters.

OUR Big Ten Conference ATHLETE OF THE WEEK ‼️👀🔗 https://t.co/2ytKQd6i2a#WeAre pic.twitter.com/4xGoJ01fBP— Penn State Track & Field/Cross Country (@PennStateTFXC) February 18, 2026

The St. Vincent and the Grenadines native broke his old school record, running a time of 1:44.03.

Roban now has school records in both the 600-meters and indoor 800m, along with the No. 2 time in the outdoor 800m and No. 3 time in the 1,000-meter.

title: Former Penn State women's soccer duo sign professional contracts in Europe
author: Annie Alberts | The Daily Collegian
description:
Two of Penn State women's soccer alumni have gone international.

Kaitlyn MacBean has signed a contract with IFK Norrköping. This is the first professional contract MacBean has signed in her career. Meanwhile, in the Czech Republic, Julia Raich signed a contract with 1.FC Sʟᴏᴠᴀ́ᴄᴋᴏ ᴢ̌ᴇɴʏ, a women's football club based in Uherské Hradiště.

MacBean har landat i Peking.Den amerikanska anfallaren har skrivit kontrakt med IFK över 2026.Läs mer på https://t.co/F2iAbxNcHj pic.twitter.com/lJVbvvp5gJ— IFK Norrköping (@ifknorrkoping) February 18, 2026

MacBean's last season at Penn State was in 2025. In four seasons, she scored 39 goals, started in 66 games and ended her collegiate career with 88 points.

In addition, she was a part of the All-Big Ten Third Team, named an Academic All-Big Ten selection and received three weekly accolades from TopDrawerSoccer National Team of the Week in 2023. In 2022, she was selected for the Big Ten All-Freshman Team.

🚨 🇺🇸 V zimní přestávce ženský tým Slovácka posílila americká záložnice Julia Raich, která naposledy působila v univerzitním týmu Penn State Nittany Lions 🦁#spolu #zaslovacko pic.twitter.com/xJgpmkykrL— 1.FC Sʟᴏᴠᴀ́ᴄᴋᴏ ᴢ̌ᴇɴʏ (@1FCSzeny) February 18, 2026

Raich's last season at Penn State was also in 2025. She played five seasons, scored 11 goals, 14 assists and played 72 games. She ended her collegiate career with 36 points.
